questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 207-01/207-08, controlled substances over 2 ounces found outside a building are reported as a "narcotics offense" to the Investigative Support Section, Real Time Crime Center. No Pct 61 number is assigned; the ISS serial number is entered on the Complaint Worksheet instead.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 207-01/207-08, controlled substances over 2 ounces found outside a building are reported as a "narcotics offense" to the Investigative Support Section, Real Time Crime Center. No Pct 61 number is assigned; the ISS serial number is entered on the Complaint Worksheet instead.

questions.text: When an offense at a religious institution is investigated as a possible bias incident but the CO/Duty Captain determines it is NOT bias motivated, the precinct detective should forward copies of the 61 and OCR to:
options: A) C.O., Hate Crimes Unit only
B) C.O., Hate Crimes Unit AND Chief of Detectives, through channels
C) Chief of Detectives, Hate Crimes Unit, direct
D) Operations Unit for a Non-Bias Log number
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 207-10, for a NON-BIAS determination, the detective forwards copies to C.O., Hate Crime Unit AND Chief of Detectives, through channels. For a POSSIBLE BIAS (confirmed), the detective forwards only to C.O., Hate Crime Unit (direct). The exam often mixes these addressees between bias and non-bias procedures.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 207-10, for a NON-BIAS determination, the detective forwards copies to C.O., Hate Crime Unit AND Chief of Detectives, through channels. For a POSSIBLE BIAS (confirmed), the detective forwards only to C.O., Hate Crime Unit (direct). The exam often mixes these addressees between bias and non-bias procedures.

questions.text: A Corrections Officer is alleged to have committed domestic violence against his wife. What is the proper documentation?
options: A) Prepare a Report on Typed Letterhead (49) to the Chief of IAB and notify IAB Command Center
B) Record on a Complaint Report (61) with additional copies for IAB; no 49 or IAB notification needed
C) Notify IAB Command Center and prepare both a 49 and a Complaint Report
D) Refer the allegation to the Corrections Department's internal affairs unit
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 207-22, minor misconduct (defined as DWI and Domestic Violence) against a non-NYPD government agent is documented on a Complaint Report (61) with additional copies to IAB. No 49 or separate notification to IAB is required for minor misconduct.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 207-22, minor misconduct (defined as DWI and Domestic Violence) against a non-NYPD government agent is documented on a Complaint Report (61) with additional copies to IAB. No 49 or separate notification to IAB is required for minor misconduct.

questions.text: Regarding the CCRB investigating supervisor hierarchy for a patrol command, which of the following is NOT in the hierarchy?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 207-28, the investigating supervisor hierarchy for patrol commands is: (1) ICO, (2) SOL, (3) Platoon Commander, and if all three lieutenants are unavailable, the CO or Duty Captain. The Operations Coordinator is notably absent from this list.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 207-28, the investigating supervisor hierarchy for patrol commands is: (1) ICO, (2) SOL, (3) Platoon Commander, and if all three lieutenants are unavailable, the CO or Duty Captain. The Operations Coordinator is notably absent from this list.

questions.text: A child 9 years old or younger is reported missing. Who performs the Patrol Supervisor's duties at the scene?
options: A) The Patrol Supervisor of the command of occurrence
B) The detective assigned to the case
C) The CO/XO/Duty Captain of Pct/PSA/TD of occurrence
D) The Missing Persons Squad supervisor
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 207-23, when a missing person is a child 9 years old or younger, the CO/XO/Duty Captain of the Pct/PSA/TD of occurrence performs the Patrol Supervisor's duties. The Captain effectively takes over the Patrol Supervisor's role for these cases.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 207-23, when a missing person is a child 9 years old or younger, the CO/XO/Duty Captain of the Pct/PSA/TD of occurrence performs the Patrol Supervisor's duties. The Captain effectively takes over the Patrol Supervisor's role for these cases.

questions.text: The Desk Officer of the missing person's RESIDENCE command must finalize the Complaint Report within what time frame?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 207-23, the Desk Officer of the Residence Command must immediately review the 61 and finalize it within 2 hours of receipt. This is distinct from the D.O. of the command of occurrence, who reviews and signs the reports but does not have the same 2-hour finalization requirement.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 207-23, the Desk Officer of the Residence Command must immediately review the 61 and finalize it within 2 hours of receipt. This is distinct from the D.O. of the command of occurrence, who reviews and signs the reports but does not have the same 2-hour finalization requirement.

sergeantFocus.text: Ensure members understand which incidents require a formal Complaint Report and which are properly documented through alternative means. Misdocumentation can affect crime statistics and case tracking.
Supervisors must ensure that complaints taken for another command are promptly and accurately forwarded. Delays can compromise investigations.

Patrol supervisors should ensure that officers conducting follow-up canvasses or receiving new information properly route it to the assigned investigative unit rather than creating new complaint reports.
Supervisors are responsible for ensuring the complaint file is maintained in an orderly and up-to-date fashion, facilitating case review and audits.
The Intelligence Division consultation for public officials is a new emphasis in the 2025 revision. This is a "shall" requirement, not discretionary.

The patrol supervisor has dual responsibilities: (1) promptly report to SVU, and (2) safeguard the crime scene pending detective arrival. The supervisor "shall" ensure evidence preservation -- this is mandatory.
These notifications frequently involve elderly or incapacitated persons. Ensure responding members are sensitive to the circumstances and thorough in documentation.

chapters.title: Arrests
chapters.readme:
# Section 208 — Arrests

## Source
- **Patrol Guide Section:** 208
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide1.pdf
- **Priority:** High
- **Status:** Completed

## Learning Objectives
- Understand the legal standards and departmental procedures for effecting arrests
- Know the processing requirements for different categories of arrests (felony, misdemeanor, violation)
- Identify the Patrol Supervisor's responsibilities during arrest processing
- Recognize specialized arrest procedures including warrant arrests and off-duty arrests
- Master the three levels of search and authorization requirements
- Know DAT eligibility criteria and the three categories of disqualification
- Understand family offense/DV definitions, concurrent jurisdiction, and mandatory arrest provisions
- Know Miranda warning requirements and juvenile interview restrictions
- Understand lineup/showup procedures and identification requirements
- Know consular notification obligations for foreign nationals

## Procedures

| Procedure | Title | Status |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| P.G. 208-01 | Law of Arrest | completed |
| P.G. 208-02 | Arrests — Removal to Facility | completed |
| P.G. 208-03 | Arrests — General Processing | completed |
| P.G. 208-04 | Arrest by Civilian | completed |
| P.G. 208-05 | General Search Guidelines | completed |
| P.G. 208-06 | Arrests — Photographs | completed |
| P.G. 208-07 | Arrests — Fingerprinting | completed |
| P.G. 208-08 | Arrests — DNA Collection | completed |

| P.G. 208-12 | Arrest Processing — Misdemeanor Complaints | completed |
| P.G. 208-13 | Arrest Processing — Violation Complaints | completed |

| P.G. 208-23 | Arrest Processing — Online Booking System (OLBS) | completed |
| P.G. 208-24 | Lineups and Showups | completed |
| P.G. 208-25 | Arrest Processing — Multiple Defendants | completed |
| P.G. 208-26 | Arrest Processing — Juvenile Offenders | completed |
| P.G. 208-27 | DAT — General Procedure | completed |
| P.G. 208-34 | Arrests — Organized Crime Offenses | completed |

| P.G. 208-84 | Early Diversion Initiative (Project Reset) | completed |

## Sergeant Focus Areas

### Key Supervisor Procedures
- **P.G. 208-01 — Law of Arrest:** Warrant scope (statewide vs county), warrantless arrest standards for petty offenses vs crimes, felony vs misdemeanor pursuit into private residences
- **P.G. 208-02 — Removal to Facility:** Supervisor verifies arrest at scene BEFORE removal; designates MOS to document all personnel present and BWC activation
- **P.G. 208-03 — General Processing:** High risk prisoner = TWO MOS escort at ALL times; Resisting Recidivist = 2+ qualifying arrests in 5 years; desk officer observes ALL prisoners; juvenile parent/guardian must be present for Miranda
- **P.G. 208-05 — Search Guidelines:** Three search levels; strip search requires reasonable suspicion + immediate supervisor approval; CO/Duty Captain resolves disagreements; desk officer has FINAL say on religious head covering
- **P.G. 208-09 — Rights in Custody:** Six-part Miranda; cease interrogation on request for attorney or silence; attorney must know prisoner's identity; muster room interview under continuous observation
- **P.G. 208-24 — Lineups/Showups:** Minimum 6 persons; suspect selects position; detective supervisor supervises entire procedure; do NOT advise suspect of right to attorney
- **P.G. 208-27 — DAT General:** Class E felonies, misdemeanors, violations eligible; Department REQUIRED to accept listed ID forms; desk officer makes FINAL determination on identity; never hold DAT-eligible prisoner solely for debriefing
- **P.G. 208-36 — Family Offenses/DV:** FCA vs NYPD expanded definitions; concurrent jurisdiction (18+, FCA relationship, family offense); interview separately; child abuse failure to report = Class A misdemeanor; crime against child = arrest, no DAT
- **P.G. 208-56 — Foreign Nationals:** Mandatory vs non-mandatory notification countries; Intelligence Division Operations Unit handles mandatory notifications
- **P.G. 208-69 — Notifications:** Operations Unit direct for government employees, MOS, food stamps >$500; Intelligence Division for UN employees, foreign armed forces, youth gangs
- **P.G. 208-81 — DAT Disqualifiers:** Three categories: Condition of Defendant (ID, warrant, FTA 2 years), Specific Charges (family offense, sex crime, hate crime), Order of Protection Needed (27 offenses)
- **P.G. 208-82 — DA Conferral:** Same day or next tour; if >4 days, reassign; 1st platoon extends into 2nd platoon
chapters.keyTerms:
# Section 208 — Key Terms, Forms, and Abbreviations

---

## Core Arrest Definitions

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Arrest** | The taking of a person into custody in order that they may be held to answer for a criminal offense or violation | P.G. 208-01 |
| **Pick-Up Arrest** | An arrest of a person wanted by another command or agency | P.G. 208-17 |
| **Preferred Name** | The name an arrestee prefers to be called, based on their gender identity | P.G. 208-02 |
| **Probable Cause** | Facts and circumstances that would lead a reasonable person to believe a crime has been, is being, or is about to be committed | P.G. 208-01 |
| **Reasonable Suspicion (for strip search)** | Articulable facts supporting a belief that weapons, contraband, or evidence are concealed on the person; required standard for authorizing a strip search | P.G. 208-05 |

## Search Levels

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Frisk / Field Search** | External body examination conducted immediately after apprehension; special attention to waistband, armpit, collar, groin | P.G. 208-05 |
| **Search at Police Facility** | Thorough search by same-gender member with removal of outer garments; NOT the same as a strip search | P.G. 208-05 |
| **Strip Search** | Search requiring removal of undergarments; may NOT be conducted routinely; requires reasonable suspicion and approval of arresting officer's immediate supervisor or borough court section supervisor; same gender, secure area, utmost privacy | P.G. 208-05 |

## Prisoner Classification

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **High Risk Prisoner** | A prisoner determined by the desk officer to be an escape risk; requires **TWO MOS** assigned to escort at **ALL times** | P.G. 208-03 |
| **Resisting Recidivist** | A person arrested for Resisting Arrest, Assault 2nd (sub 3), Assault on PO, or Aggravated Assault on PO, **2 or more times** within the **past 5 years**; processing enhanced with detective squad | P.G. 208-03 |

## DAT-Related Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **DAT (Desk Appearance Ticket)** | An appearance ticket issued in lieu of continued detention for Class E felonies, misdemeanors, and violations; directs defendant to appear in court at a later date | P.G. 208-27 |
| **Satisfactory Form of Identification** | NYS license, passport, US military ID, citizenship papers, out-of-state license, resident alien card, Municipal ID (including Canadian), Public Benefit Card; Department REQUIRED to accept these forms | P.G. 208-27 |

## Miranda Warnings — Six Parts

| # | Warning Text | Source |
|---|-------------|--------|
| 1 | You have the **right to remain silent** | P.G. 208-09 |
| 2 | **Anything you say** may be used against you | P.G. 208-09 |
| 3 | You have the right to an **attorney before and during** questioning | P.G. 208-09 |
| 4 | An attorney will be **provided if you cannot afford** one | P.G. 208-09 |
| 5 | You have the right to **remain silent until you consult** with an attorney | P.G. 208-09 |
| 6 | Are you **willing to answer** questions? | P.G. 208-09 |

## Domestic Violence / Family Offense Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Family/Household (FCA Definition)** | Legally married, formerly married, related by marriage, related by blood, child in common, intimate relationship | P.G. 208-36 |
| **Family/Household (NYPD Expanded)** | FCA definition PLUS persons currently or formerly living together in a family-type relationship | P.G. 208-36 |
| **Family Offense** | Specific offenses committed between family/household members, including harassment, assault, disorderly conduct, stalking, menacing, sexual misconduct, criminal mischief, strangulation, identity theft, grand larceny, coercion, unlawful dissemination of intimate image | P.G. 208-36 |
| **Concurrent Jurisdiction** | When both Criminal Court and Family Court have jurisdiction; requires: (1) family offense, (2) FCA relationship, (3) offender 18+ (or 16-17 charged with felony-level family offense) | P.G. 208-36 |
| **Order of Protection** | A court order directing a person to refrain from certain conduct toward a protected party; violation may be a mandatory arrest | P.G. 208-37 |

## Identification Procedures

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Lineup** | An identification procedure where a suspect is placed among at least **5 other persons** (minimum **6 total**); same sex, race, approximate age/height/build; suspect selects position; detective supervisor supervises | P.G. 208-24 |
| **Showup** | A **prompt, on-the-scene, singly, one-to-one** display of a suspect to a witness; used close in time and proximity to the crime | P.G. 208-24 |

## Foreign National Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Foreign National** | Any person who is **NOT a United States citizen**, regardless of immigration status | P.G. 208-56 |
| **Mandatory Notification Countries** | Countries requiring automatic consular notification upon arrest of their citizen, handled through the Intelligence Division Operations Unit (24 hrs) | P.G. 208-56 |

## Bribery Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Bribery Arrest (PG 208-34)** | The arrest process for the CHARGE of Bribery; covers notification chain, reporting, and court appearances | P.G. 208-34 |
| **Attempted Bribery (PG 208-35)** | The process of getting wired up when a UMOS is offered a bribe; Attempted Bribery is NOT a criminal charge — the perp is charged with Bribery | P.G. 208-35 |
| **4 Ds (Bribery Court Appearances)** | Date, Docket#, DA's name, Disposition — the four items an arresting officer must record for all court appearances in a bribery case | P.G. 208-34 |

## DV Investigation Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **DRAMAS** | Mnemonic for personal family offenses: Dis Con / Reckless Endangerment / Aggravated Harassment 2nd, Harassment 1st or 2nd / Menacing 2nd or 3rd / Assault 2nd or 3rd (or Attempts) / Strangulation 1st or 2nd, Criminal Obstruction of Breathing, Stalking all degrees | P.G. 208-36 |
| **C GIF CUSS** | Mnemonic for property/sexual family offenses: Criminal Mischief (all degrees) / Grand Larceny (3rd and 4th) / Identity Theft (1st, 2nd, 3rd) / Forcible Touching / Coercion 2nd (sub 1, 2, 3) / Unlawful Dissemination of Intimate Image / Sexual Misconduct / Sexual Abuse 3rd & Sexual Abuse 2nd sub 1 | P.G. 208-36 |
| **Primary Physical Aggressor** | The person identified as the primary aggressor in a simultaneous misdemeanor vs. misdemeanor family offense; determined by comparative extent of injuries, defensive acts, current/future threats, and prior DV history | P.G. 208-36 |
| **ICCU** | Inter City Correspondence Unit — receives forwarded paperwork for DV incidents occurring outside NYC but within NYS | P.G. 208-36 |
| **Hope Card** | Wallet-size card or digital version for Final OOPs; UMOS scan QR code with Dept phone to verify (Ops Order 11 s.2025) | P.G. 208-36 |
| **DVPO** | Domestic Violence Prevention Officer — opens/manages cases in DVIMS, assists in apprehension, conducts home visits, follows up with victims on photos | P.G. 208-37 |
| **DVIMS** | Domestic Violence Incident Management System — used by DVPO for case management (NOT the same as ECMS) | P.G. 208-37 |
| **ECMS** | Enterprise Case Management System — used by assigned detective to document DVPO arrest efforts | P.G. 208-37 |
| **I-Card** | Investigation Card activated for named DV perpetrator; must be activated no later than end of assigned tour; should include NYSID # | P.G. 208-36 |
| **Rosario Material** | DIRs are potential Rosario material; if an arrest is made, A/O must give copy of DIR to ADA | P.G. 208-36 |

## Firearm Seizure in DV

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Mandatory Seizure** | WILL seize firearms and license/permit if: OOP exists against holder, physical force or threat of involved in incident, or license holder is arrested (regardless of charge) | P.G. 208-36 |
| **Discretionary Seizure** | MAY seize if UMOS reasonably believes presence of firearms creates imminent risk of physical injury or higher | P.G. 208-36 |

---

## Forms

| Form Name | Form Number | Purpose | Reference |
|-----------|-------------|---------|-----------|
| OLBS Worksheet | PD244-159 | Online Booking System worksheet for arrest processing | P.G. 208-03 |
| Arrest Supplement | PD244-157 | Supplemental arrest information and documentation | P.G. 208-03 |
| Desk Appearance Ticket | PD260-121 | Appearance ticket issued in lieu of detention | P.G. 208-27 |
| Investigation Card | PD373-163 | Record of field investigation; does NOT preclude DAT issuance | P.G. 208-27 |

---

## Units and Abbreviations

| Abbreviation | Full Name | Context |
|-------------|-----------|---------|
| **BWC** | Body-Worn Camera | Must be activated during medical/psychological inquiry and at arrest scene |
| **OLBS** | Online Booking System | Primary arrest processing system |
| **NYSID** | New York State Identification Number | Fingerprint-based identification; check every 30 min max during DAT processing |
| **DA** | District Attorney | Conferral required same day or next tour for DAT cases |
| **FCA** | Family Court Act | Defines family/household relationships for concurrent jurisdiction |
| **SVU** | Special Victims Unit | Notified for sex offenses and child abuse |
| **DOCCS** | Department of Corrections and Community Supervision | Notified for parole violator arrests |
| **TBTA** | Triborough Bridge and Tunnel Authority | Notified for offenses at TBTA facilities |
| **CUNY** | City University of New York | Notified for offenses at CUNY facilities |
| **DOE** | Department of Education | School Safety Division notified for DOE-related offenses |
| **VTL** | Vehicle and Traffic Law | VTL 1192 (DWI) is a DAT disqualifier |
| **ICCU** | Inter City Correspondence Unit | Receives forwarded DV paperwork for incidents outside NYC but within NYS |
| **DVPO** | Domestic Violence Prevention Officer | Opens/manages cases in DVIMS; assists in apprehension within pct/PSA confines |
| **DVIMS** | Domestic Violence Incident Management System | DVPO case management system (NOT ECMS) |
| **ECMS** | Enterprise Case Management System | Assigned detective documents DVPO arrest efforts here |
| **IAB** | Internal Affairs Bureau | Notified for bribery arrests and MOS DV offenses; provides log number |
| **BCS** | Borough Court Section | Where perps are transported; also where ICO obtains post-arraignment info |
| **LLS** | Language Line Service | Certified interpreter service used to verify DV info from non-English speaking victims |
| **FORMS** | Finest Online Records Management System | System for inputting DIRs, photos, and other records for NYC incidents |

---

## Critical Numbers

| Number | What It Represents | Source |
|--------|-------------------|--------|
| **6** | Minimum total persons in a lineup (suspect + 5 others) | P.G. 208-24 |
| **2 years** | Failed to appear in court — DAT disqualifier | P.G. 208-81 |
| **5 years** | Lookback period for resisting recidivist classification (2+ arrests) | P.G. 208-03 |
| **30 minutes** | Maximum interval for NYSID recheck during DAT processing | P.G. 208-27 |
| **4 days** | If next tour > 4 days away, reassign DA conferral to another officer | P.G. 208-82 |
| **$500** | Federal food stamp threshold for Operations Unit notification | P.G. 208-69 |
| **27** | Number of specific offenses on the Order of Protection needed list (DAT disqualifier Category 3) | P.G. 208-81 |
| **7** | Copies of 49 (Report on Typed Letterhead) to Chief of Department in a bribery arrest | P.G. 208-34 |
| **3** | Steps in the Attempted Bribery (getting wired up) procedure (PG 208-35) | P.G. 208-35 |
| **3** | Places the IAB Log # must appear when MOS commits DV offense (DIR Results box, 61 Details, FORMS Database) | P.G. 208-37 |
| **5** | Copies of 49 (+1 if guns removed) for MOS DV offense report | P.G. 208-37 |
| **10 years** | Lookback period for Menacing 1st (do Menacing 2nd with conviction in last 10 years) — DAT Category 3 disqualifier | P.G. 208-81 |
| **5 years** | Lookback period for Aggravated Family Offense (family offense with prior conviction within 5 years) — DAT Category 3 disqualifier | P.G. 208-81 |

questions.text: A sergeant receives a warrant issued by a city court in Westchester County. The suspect is located in a neighboring county. Can the warrant be executed there?
options: A) No, city court warrants can only be executed within the city limits
B) Yes, city/town/village court warrants may be executed in the county of issuance or adjoining county
C) Yes, all warrants have statewide reach
D) No, the warrant must be re-issued by the adjoining county's court
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-01, warrants issued by a city, town, or village court may be executed within the county of issuance OR an adjoining county. Only warrants from NYC Criminal Court, district court, or superior court have statewide authority.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-01, warrants issued by a city, town, or village court may be executed within the county of issuance OR an adjoining county. Only warrants from NYC Criminal Court, district court, or superior court have statewide authority.

questions.text: An officer observes a traffic infraction in an adjoining county while off-duty. The officer is employed in Kings County. Can the officer make a warrantless arrest?
options: A) Yes, any offense in the officer's presence allows arrest
B) Yes, but only if the infraction is also a misdemeanor
C) No, petty offenses require the arrest to be within the geographical area of employment AND the county of occurrence or adjoining county
D) No, off-duty officers cannot make warrantless arrests
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-01, warrantless arrest for a petty offense (violation or traffic infraction) requires the offense be in the officer's presence, within the geographical area of employment, AND within the county of occurrence or adjoining county. An officer in an adjoining county outside their area of employment cannot make this arrest.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-01, warrantless arrest for a petty offense (violation or traffic infraction) requires the offense be in the officer's presence, within the geographical area of employment, AND within the county of occurrence or adjoining county. An officer in an adjoining county outside their area of employment cannot make this arrest.

questions.text: A misdemeanor suspect flees into a private residence. Under what circumstance may an officer enter without a warrant or consent?
options: A) Whenever the officer has probable cause for the misdemeanor
B) Only to prevent imminent injury, destruction of evidence, escape, or under exigent circumstances
C) Never — a warrant is always required for a misdemeanor suspect
D) Only if the suspect is also wanted on a felony warrant
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-01, for a misdemeanor suspect fleeing into a private residence, officers may enter ONLY to prevent imminent injury, prevent destruction of evidence, prevent escape, or under exigent circumstances. For a felony suspect, officers may pursue directly into the residence without these additional requirements.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-01, for a misdemeanor suspect fleeing into a private residence, officers may enter ONLY to prevent imminent injury, prevent destruction of evidence, prevent escape, or under exigent circumstances. For a felony suspect, officers may pursue directly into the residence without these additional requirements.

questions.text: During a search at a police facility, the arrestee's gender is not apparent. How should the searching officer's gender be determined?
options: A) The desk officer assigns the searching officer based on the arrestee's biological sex
B) A supervisor makes the determination based on visual assessment
C) The search is assigned to the gender requested by the arrestee, consistent with officer safety and resource availability
D) Two officers of different genders conduct the search together
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-05, if the arrestee's gender is not apparent or the arrestee objects to the assigned gender, the search is conducted by a member of the gender requested by the arrestee, consistent with officer safety and resource availability. Officers must NEVER search a person to determine their gender.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-05, if the arrestee's gender is not apparent or the arrestee objects to the assigned gender, the search is conducted by a member of the gender requested by the arrestee, consistent with officer safety and resource availability. Officers must NEVER search a person to determine their gender.

questions.text: Who must authorize a strip search?
options: A) The desk officer on duty
B) The arresting officer's immediate supervisor or borough court section supervisor
C) The commanding officer or duty captain
D) Any supervisor of higher rank than the arresting officer
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-05, a strip search requires the knowledge and approval of the arresting officer's IMMEDIATE SUPERVISOR or the borough court section supervisor. The CO/Duty Captain becomes involved only when there is a disagreement between the desk officer and an outside supervisor.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-05, a strip search requires the knowledge and approval of the arresting officer's IMMEDIATE SUPERVISOR or the borough court section supervisor. The CO/Duty Captain becomes involved only when there is a disagreement between the desk officer and an outside supervisor.

questions.text: The desk officer and an outside supervisor disagree about whether a strip search should be conducted. Who makes the final decision?
options: A) The desk officer, as the officer in charge of the command
B) The outside supervisor, as the arresting officer's supervisor
C) The CO or Duty Captain, after being notified by the desk officer
D) The Legal Bureau, after consultation
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-05, when there is a disagreement between the desk officer and an outside supervisor regarding a strip search, the desk officer notifies the CO/Duty Captain, who makes the FINAL decision.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-05, when there is a disagreement between the desk officer and an outside supervisor regarding a strip search, the desk officer notifies the CO/Duty Captain, who makes the FINAL decision.

questions.text: How many parts comprise the Miranda warnings as outlined in the Patrol Guide?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-09, Miranda warnings consist of SIX parts: (1) right to remain silent, (2) anything said may be used against you, (3) right to attorney before and during questioning, (4) attorney provided if cannot afford one, (5) right to remain silent until consulting attorney, (6) willing to answer questions.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-09, Miranda warnings consist of SIX parts: (1) right to remain silent, (2) anything said may be used against you, (3) right to attorney before and during questioning, (4) attorney provided if cannot afford one, (5) right to remain silent until consulting attorney, (6) willing to answer questions.

questions.text: A juvenile is arrested and the parent is present. The juvenile agrees to answer questions, but the parent objects. What should the officer do?
options: A) Proceed with questioning since the juvenile consented
B) Cease questioning — the parent's objection overrides the juvenile's willingness
C) Continue but limit questions to basic identifying information
D) Request a Legal Bureau consultation before proceeding
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-03, if a parent or guardian objects to questioning, NO questioning is permitted even if the juvenile is willing to answer. The parent's objection is controlling.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-03, if a parent or guardian objects to questioning, NO questioning is permitted even if the juvenile is willing to answer. The parent's objection is controlling.

questions.text: During custodial interrogation, the suspect states "I think I should talk to a lawyer." What is the proper response?
options: A) Continue questioning, as the statement was equivocal
B) Ask the suspect to clarify whether they want an attorney
D) Advise the suspect of Miranda rights again and continue
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-09, interrogation must cease immediately when a subject indicates they want an attorney or wish to remain silent. Officers should err on the side of cessation when any request for counsel is made.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-09, interrogation must cease immediately when a subject indicates they want an attorney or wish to remain silent. Officers should err on the side of cessation when any request for counsel is made.

questions.text: A person is arrested for a Class E felony and presents a valid out-of-state driver's license. Is the person eligible for a DAT?
options: A) No, DATs are only for misdemeanors and violations
B) No, out-of-state identification is not accepted for DAT processing
C) Yes, Class E felonies are DAT-eligible, and out-of-state licenses are a satisfactory form of ID
D) Yes, but only if the person also has a local address
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-27, DATs may be issued for Class E felonies, misdemeanors, and violations. Out-of-state driver's licenses are on the list of satisfactory identification that the Department is REQUIRED to accept.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-27, DATs may be issued for Class E felonies, misdemeanors, and violations. Out-of-state driver's licenses are on the list of satisfactory identification that the Department is REQUIRED to accept.

questions.text: A defendant arrested for a misdemeanor has an investigation card on file. Does this preclude issuance of a DAT?
options: A) Yes, any prior police contact disqualifies DAT issuance
B) Yes, an investigation card indicates the person is known to police
C) No, an investigation card does NOT preclude DAT issuance
D) No, but the desk officer must obtain supervisor approval
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-27, an investigation card does NOT preclude DAT issuance. Similarly, DNA owed is NOT a disqualifying factor.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-27, an investigation card does NOT preclude DAT issuance. Similarly, DNA owed is NOT a disqualifying factor.

questions.text: Which of the following is a DAT disqualifying factor under the "Condition of Defendant" category?
options: B) The defendant has an investigation card on file
C) The defendant failed to appear in court within the last 2 years
D) The defendant has a prior misdemeanor conviction
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-81, failure to appear in court within the last 2 YEARS is a disqualifying factor under Category 1 (Condition of Defendant). DNA owed and investigation cards are specifically NOT disqualifying. A prior conviction alone is not a disqualifier.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-81, failure to appear in court within the last 2 YEARS is a disqualifying factor under Category 1 (Condition of Defendant). DNA owed and investigation cards are specifically NOT disqualifying. A prior conviction alone is not a disqualifier.

questions.text: The arresting officer's next regularly scheduled tour is 5 days away. What must happen regarding DA conferral for a DAT case?
options: A) The officer confers with the DA upon return in 5 days
B) The conferral is assigned to another officer
C) The desk officer confers with the DA on the officer's behalf
D) The case is automatically upgraded from DAT to continued custody
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-82, if the arresting officer's next regularly scheduled tour is more than 4 DAYS away, the DA conferral must be assigned to another officer.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-82, if the arresting officer's next regularly scheduled tour is more than 4 DAYS away, the DA conferral must be assigned to another officer.

questions.text: Under the Family Court Act, which of the following relationships qualifies as "family/household"?
options: A) Current roommates who share an apartment but are not in an intimate relationship
B) Former coworkers who dated briefly three years ago
C) Persons who have a child in common but were never married
D) Business partners who have known each other for ten years
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-36, the FCA definition of family/household includes persons who have a child in common. Casual acquaintances, business relationships, and mere roommates (without a family-type relationship) do not qualify. Note: the NYPD expanded definition would cover persons living together in a family-type relationship, but a simple roommate arrangement is not a family-type relationship.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-36, the FCA definition of family/household includes persons who have a child in common. Casual acquaintances, business relationships, and mere roommates (without a family-type relationship) do not qualify. Note: the NYPD expanded definition would cover persons living together in a family-type relationship, but a simple roommate arrangement is not a family-type relationship.

questions.text: What additional category does the NYPD expanded definition of "family/household" include beyond the Family Court Act definition?
options: A) Persons who attend the same school or workplace
B) Persons currently or formerly living together in a family-type relationship
C) Persons who share a common friend group
D) Persons who live in the same apartment building
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-36, the NYPD expands the FCA definition to include persons currently or formerly living together in a family-type relationship. The FCA itself covers: legally married, formerly married, related by marriage, related by blood, child in common, and intimate relationship.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-36, the NYPD expands the FCA definition to include persons currently or formerly living together in a family-type relationship. The FCA itself covers: legally married, formerly married, related by marriage, related by blood, child in common, and intimate relationship.

questions.text: A 17-year-old is arrested for misdemeanor harassment against their former spouse. Does concurrent jurisdiction exist between Criminal Court and Family Court?
options: A) Yes, all three conditions are met
B) No, because the offender is under 18 and the charge is not a felony
C) No, because harassment is not a family offense
D) Yes, because the parties were married
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-36, concurrent jurisdiction requires: (1) family offense, (2) FCA relationship, and (3) offender 18+. A 16-17 year old qualifies ONLY if charged with a felony-level family offense. Misdemeanor harassment by a 17-year-old goes to Criminal Court only.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-36, concurrent jurisdiction requires: (1) family offense, (2) FCA relationship, and (3) offender 18+. A 16-17 year old qualifies ONLY if charged with a felony-level family offense. Misdemeanor harassment by a 17-year-old goes to Criminal Court only.

questions.text: At a domestic violence scene, no certified interpreter is available and the victim speaks limited English. A family member offers to interpret. What should the officer do?
options: A) Accept the family member as interpreter for the entire investigation
B) Decline — only certified interpreters may be used at any DV scene
C) Use the family member temporarily to stabilize the scene, then verify information with a certified member or Language Line
D) Postpone the investigation until a certified interpreter arrives
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-36, officers should avoid using family members or bystanders as interpreters. However, they may be used temporarily to stabilize the scene. Information must then be verified through a certified member or Language Line.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-36, officers should avoid using family members or bystanders as interpreters. However, they may be used temporarily to stabilize the scene. Information must then be verified through a certified member or Language Line.

questions.text: An officer suspects child abuse but decides not to report it because the evidence is inconclusive. What is the legal consequence of willful failure to report suspected child abuse?
options: A) No consequence if the evidence is inconclusive
C) Class A misdemeanor plus civil liability
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-36, willful failure to report suspected child abuse constitutes a CLASS A MISDEMEANOR and creates civil liability. The standard is suspected abuse — conclusive evidence is not required before reporting.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-36, willful failure to report suspected child abuse constitutes a CLASS A MISDEMEANOR and creates civil liability. The standard is suspected abuse — conclusive evidence is not required before reporting.

questions.text: In a lineup, how many total persons must be present?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-24, a lineup must include the suspect plus at least 5 OTHER persons, for a minimum of 6 total participants.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-24, a lineup must include the suspect plus at least 5 OTHER persons, for a minimum of 6 total participants.

questions.text: During lineup preparation, where should the suspect be placed in the lineup?
options: B) In a position randomly assigned by the detective
C) In the position selected by the suspect
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-24, the suspect must be permitted to SELECT their own position in the lineup.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-24, the suspect must be permitted to SELECT their own position in the lineup.

questions.text: The desk officer determines a prisoner is "high risk." What is the required escort protocol?
options: A) One MOS assigned during transport only
B) Two MOS assigned during transport to court
C) Two MOS assigned to escort the prisoner at ALL times
D) One MOS with supervisor check-ins every 30 minutes
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-03, a "high risk" prisoner requires TWO MOS assigned to escort the prisoner at ALL times — not just during transport, but continuously throughout processing and detention.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-03, a "high risk" prisoner requires TWO MOS assigned to escort the prisoner at ALL times — not just during transport, but continuously throughout processing and detention.

questions.text: A person has been arrested three times for Resisting Arrest in the past 4 years. What classification applies?
options: C) Resisting recidivist — enhance processing with detective squad
D) Violent felon — transfer to detective squad for processing
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-03, a "Resisting Recidivist" is a person arrested 2 or more times within the past 5 YEARS for Resisting Arrest, Assault 2nd (sub 3), Assault on PO, or Aggravated Assault on PO. Three arrests for Resisting Arrest in 4 years qualifies. Processing is enhanced with the detective squad.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-03, a "Resisting Recidivist" is a person arrested 2 or more times within the past 5 YEARS for Resisting Arrest, Assault 2nd (sub 3), Assault on PO, or Aggravated Assault on PO. Three arrests for Resisting Arrest in 4 years qualifies. Processing is enhanced with the detective squad.

questions.text: A foreign national from a mandatory notification country is arrested. The prisoner states they do NOT want their consulate notified. What should the officer do?
options: A) Honor the prisoner's wishes and do not notify
B) Document the refusal and proceed without notification
C) Contact the Intelligence Division Operations Unit — mandatory notification is required regardless of the prisoner's wishes
D) Notify the prisoner's attorney instead
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-56, for mandatory notification countries, consular notification is REQUIRED regardless of the prisoner's wishes. The Intelligence Division Operations Unit (available 24 hours) handles mandatory consular notifications.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-56, for mandatory notification countries, consular notification is REQUIRED regardless of the prisoner's wishes. The Intelligence Division Operations Unit (available 24 hours) handles mandatory consular notifications.

questions.text: For non-mandatory notification countries, what is the officer's obligation regarding consular notification?
options: B) Inform the prisoner of their right to have their embassy/consulate notified
C) No obligation — consular notification is optional for non-mandatory countries
D) Contact the Intelligence Division for guidance
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-56, for non-mandatory notification countries, the officer must INFORM the prisoner of their right to have their embassy or consulate notified. The prisoner may then choose whether to exercise that right.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-56, for non-mandatory notification countries, the officer must INFORM the prisoner of their right to have their embassy or consulate notified. The prisoner may then choose whether to exercise that right.

questions.text: During DAT processing, the OMNIFORM system shows no NYSID has been returned. How frequently should the officer check for results?
options: D) Only once, then proceed without NYSID
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-27, if no NYSID is returned during DAT processing, the officer should check back every 30 MINUTES maximum. The prisoner should not be held indefinitely waiting for NYSID results.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-27, if no NYSID is returned during DAT processing, the officer should check back every 30 MINUTES maximum. The prisoner should not be held indefinitely waiting for NYSID results.

questions.text: An officer is conducting a frisk of a prisoner in a wheelchair. When frisking the prisoner's lower body (legs and under buttocks), which direction should the officer's palms face?
options: A) Palms facing down, sliding along each leg
B) Palms facing up, sliding hands up each leg and under buttocks
C) Palms facing inward toward the body
D) It does not matter as long as the search is thorough
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-05, when frisking a mobility-disabled prisoner in a wheelchair, the officer frisks the lower body by sliding hands up each leg and under buttocks with palms UP. When searching the device (wheelchair) itself, palms face DOWN. The exam frequently reverses the UP/DOWN direction.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-05, when frisking a mobility-disabled prisoner in a wheelchair, the officer frisks the lower body by sliding hands up each leg and under buttocks with palms UP. When searching the device (wheelchair) itself, palms face DOWN. The exam frequently reverses the UP/DOWN direction.

questions.text: A UMOS is offered a bribe by a suspect during a traffic stop. What charge should the suspect face?
options: B) Bribery — the mere offer is sufficient; no actual exchange is required
C) Corruption of a Public Official
D) No charge unless the officer accepts the bribe
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-34 and 208-35, "Attempted Bribery" is NOT a criminal charge. If a UMOS is offered a bribe, the perp is charged with Bribery. An actual exchange does not have to take place; the mere offer is sufficient. A past exam offered "Charge with Attempted Bribery" as an answer choice and it was wrong.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-34 and 208-35, "Attempted Bribery" is NOT a criminal charge. If a UMOS is offered a bribe, the perp is charged with Bribery. An actual exchange does not have to take place; the mere offer is sufficient. A past exam offered "Charge with Attempted Bribery" as an answer choice and it was wrong.

questions.text: In a bribery arrest, the ADA refuses to draw up the bribery charge. Who makes the notifications to the C.O./Duty Captain and IAB?
options: A) The Desk Officer, as in a regular bribery arrest
B) The Arresting Officer — this is an exception to the normal notification chain
D) The patrol supervisor on duty
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-34, when the ADA refuses to draw up the bribery charge, the Arresting Officer (not the Desk Officer) notifies the C.O./Duty Captain and IAB Command Center. This differs from a regular bribery arrest where the Desk Officer handles notifications. This breaks the chain of command.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-34, when the ADA refuses to draw up the bribery charge, the Arresting Officer (not the Desk Officer) notifies the C.O./Duty Captain and IAB Command Center. This differs from a regular bribery arrest where the Desk Officer handles notifications. This breaks the chain of command.

questions.text: Which mnemonic lists the personal crimes that qualify as family offenses?
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-36, DRAMAS covers the personal family offenses: Dis Con, Reckless Endangerment, Aggravated Harassment 2nd/Harassment 1st or 2nd, Menacing 2nd or 3rd, Assault 2nd or 3rd (or Attempts), Strangulation 1st or 2nd/Criminal Obstruction of Breathing/Stalking all degrees. C GIF CUSS covers the property/sexual crimes.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-36, DRAMAS covers the personal family offenses: Dis Con, Reckless Endangerment, Aggravated Harassment 2nd/Harassment 1st or 2nd, Menacing 2nd or 3rd, Assault 2nd or 3rd (or Attempts), Strangulation 1st or 2nd/Criminal Obstruction of Breathing/Stalking all degrees. C GIF CUSS covers the property/sexual crimes.

questions.text: A domestic violence incident occurs outside NYC but within New York State. Which of the following is correct regarding documentation?
options: A) Prepare DIR and enter it into FORMS; prepare Complaint Report and enter into OMNIFORM
B) Prepare DIR and Complaint Report Worksheets; do NOT input to FORMS or OMNIFORM; forward to ICCU
C) No documentation required since the incident is outside NYPD jurisdiction
D) Prepare DIR in FORMS but use the Complaint Report Worksheet instead of OMNIFORM
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-36 and 208-70, for DV incidents occurring outside NYC but within NYS, prepare a Complaint Report Worksheet (do NOT input to OMNIFORM), prepare DIR (do NOT input to FORMS), and take photos (do NOT upload to FORMS). Forward all paperwork to ICCU (Inter City Correspondence Unit) by scanning/emailing and sending hard copies via Dept mail.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-36 and 208-70, for DV incidents occurring outside NYC but within NYS, prepare a Complaint Report Worksheet (do NOT input to OMNIFORM), prepare DIR (do NOT input to FORMS), and take photos (do NOT upload to FORMS). Forward all paperwork to ICCU (Inter City Correspondence Unit) by scanning/emailing and sending hard copies via Dept mail.

questions.text: When an MOS commits a domestic violence offense, in how many places must the IAB Log number be documented?
options: B) Two — the DIR and the Complaint Report
C) Three — the DIR "Results" box, "Details" of the 61, and FORMS Database
D) Four — the DIR, Complaint Report, Digital Activity Log, and FORMS
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 208-37, when an MOS commits a DV offense, the IAB Log number must appear in THREE places: (1) DIR in the box "Results of Investigation and Basis of Action Taken," (2) Complaint Report "Details" section, and (3) FORMS Database.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-37, when an MOS commits a DV offense, the IAB Log number must appear in THREE places: (1) DIR in the box "Results of Investigation and Basis of Action Taken," (2) Complaint Report "Details" section, and (3) FORMS Database.

questions.text: A Criminal Court Order of Protection is violated. The violation occurred in NYC. What charge is appropriate and where is the arrest processed?
options: A) Family Offense — processed in Family Court with concurrent jurisdiction
B) Criminal Contempt or Aggravated Criminal Contempt — processed in NYC Criminal Court; NOT a Family Offense and NO concurrent jurisdiction
C) Violation of Court Order — processed in the court that issued the OOP
D) Criminal Contempt — processed in Family Court since the OOP was issued there
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 208-36, Criminal Contempt for violating a Supreme Court, Family Court, or Criminal Court OOP is charged as Criminal Contempt or Aggravated Criminal Contempt. This is NOT a Family Offense, there is NO concurrent jurisdiction, and if the violation occurred in NYC, the arrest goes to Criminal Court.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 208-36, Criminal Contempt for violating a Supreme Court, Family Court, or Criminal Court OOP is charged as Criminal Contempt or Aggravated Criminal Contempt. This is NOT a Family Offense, there is NO concurrent jurisdiction, and if the violation occurred in NYC, the arrest goes to Criminal Court.

sergeantFocus.text: A DAT-eligible prisoner should NEVER be held solely for debriefing purposes. The only exception is when there is reasonable suspicion that the prisoner is involved in another crime. Holding a DAT-eligible prisoner without legal basis exposes the Department to liability.

The three categories are frequently tested. Remember: Category 1 = who the defendant IS, Category 2 = what the defendant DID, Category 3 = what the defendant MIGHT DO (future danger requiring court protection).
Supervisors must track DAT conferrals to ensure they happen within the required timeframe. A missed conferral can result in case dismissal.
Strangulation injuries are often internal and not immediately visible. Supervisors must ensure victims receive medical evaluation even when external injuries are absent.

The Training Sergeant (not the DV Sergeant) is responsible for ensuring all UMOS know how to take and upload DV photos. The DVPO reviews daily to ensure photos were taken. Injuries that were not visible initially may become visible later — follow-up photography is critical.

Petty offenses have the most restrictive requirements — the offense must be in the officer's presence, within the geographical area of employment, AND within the county of occurrence or adjoining county. Crimes (felonies and misdemeanors) require only reasonable cause and have no county limitation within the geographical area.
The supervisor's verification at the scene BEFORE removal is a critical checkpoint. The supervisor must also ensure a member is designated to document all MOS present and BWC activation status.
The desk officer must personally observe the physical and mental condition of ALL prisoners entering the command. High-risk prisoners require TWO MOS escort at ALL times — not just during transport.
Ensure arresting officers follow identification procedures thoroughly. Misidentification can lead to wrongful release or processing errors.
Supervisors must monitor felony processing timelines closely to ensure arraignment deadlines are met.
Supervisors must verify the validity of the warrant or wanted status before authorizing transfer of custody.

Supervisors must ensure all required notifications are made promptly. Failure to notify specialized units can compromise investigations and inter-agency relationships. The Intelligence Division notification list is particularly important — UN employees, foreign armed forces, and criminal anarchy all require immediate Intelligence Division contact.

The desk officer's authority over religious head covering decisions is absolute at the command level. This mirrors the desk officer's broad authority over prisoner processing decisions within the command.

Supervisors must ensure that strip searches are NEVER conducted routinely. Every strip search requires (1) reasonable suspicion, (2) immediate supervisor or borough court section supervisor approval, and (3) same-gender officer in a private, secure location. Document the basis for reasonable suspicion. NEVER allow a body cavity search under any circumstances.

The supervisor must verify that the civilian's arrest meets the legal standard — the offense must have been committed in the civilian's presence. If the legal basis is questionable, consult with Legal Bureau.

Supervisors must determine the citizenship status of all prisoners and ensure proper consular notification. Failure to provide mandatory notification can have diplomatic consequences and may compromise prosecution.
This mirrors the search guidelines in P.G. 208-05. The desk officer has ultimate authority within the command on religious head covering decisions during arrest processing.
Supervisors should ensure that eligible defendants are identified for Project Reset during processing. The program criteria are narrow — age 18+, first-time offender, non-violent misdemeanor only.

Supervisors play a critical role in MOS DV incidents. The C.O./Duty Captain makes the duty status decision (not IAB), but must have IAB conduct a background check first. The confidential file requirement is ongoing — the C.O. maintains it for ALL MOS who have been involved in DV incidents.

chapters.title: Summonses
chapters.readme:
# Section 209 — Summonses

## Source
- **Patrol Guide Section:** 209
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide1.pdf
- **Priority:** Medium
- **Status:** Completed

## Learning Objectives
- Understand the conditions under which a summons may be issued instead of an arrest (age, capacity, eligible offenses)
- Know the VTL and Penal Law exceptions that require arrest rather than summons issuance
- Identify the correct returnable agency for each summons type (Criminal Court, TVB, OATH, TAB, DOF)
- Master CJRA civil summons eligibility, OATH Recidivist criteria, and NYPD Codes 1-4
- Know the 5 mandatory identification elements for parking summonses and the consequences of omission
- Understand abandoned and derelict vehicle definitions and associated timeframes
- Recognize the Patrol Supervisor's responsibilities for Code 3 verification, voided summons review, and summons accountability
- Know the procedures for suspended/revoked license stops, ignition key removal, and department vehicle summonses
- Distinguish between Urgent Police Action, Necessary Police Action, and convenience for department vehicle summons defenses
- Understand TAB return date calculation (28 days) and Criminal Court return date sourcing (FINEST message)

## Procedures

| Procedure | Title | Status |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| P.G. 209-01 | Conditions of Service | completed |
| P.G. 209-02 | Returnable Agencies | completed |
| P.G. 209-03 | CJRA Civil Summons (OATH) | completed |
| P.G. 209-04 | Return Date/Time | completed |
| P.G. 209-07 | Parking Summonses | completed |
| P.G. 209-08 | Parking Summons — Additional Procedures | completed |
| P.G. 209-09 | Personal Service of Summonses (TVB/Criminal Court) | completed |
| P.G. 209-10 | TVB Summons Procedures | completed |
| P.G. 209-11 | Criminal Court Traffic Summons | completed |
| P.G. 209-12 | OATH Summonses (Non-CJRA) | completed |
| P.G. 209-15 | Transit Adjudication Bureau (TAB) Summonses | completed |
| P.G. 209-16 | TAB Summons — Additional Procedures | completed |
| P.G. 209-17 | E-Bike and E-Scooter Summonses | completed |
| P.G. 209-18 | Bicycle Summonses | completed |
| P.G. 209-19 | Pedestrian Summonses | completed |
| P.G. 209-20 | Environmental Control Board (ECB) Procedures | completed |
| P.G. 209-21 | Summons Accountability | completed |
| P.G. 209-22 | Summons Records | completed |
| P.G. 209-23 | Summons Dispositions | completed |
| P.G. 209-24 | Mandatory Driver's License Checks | completed |
| P.G. 209-26 | Suspended/Revoked License | completed |
| P.G. 209-27 | Removal of Ignition Key | completed |
| P.G. 209-28 | Unlicensed Tow Truck Operators | completed |
| P.G. 209-30 | Summons on Department Vehicle | completed |
| P.G. 209-31 | Voiding a Summons — General Procedures | completed |
| P.G. 209-32 | Voiding a Parking Summons | completed |
| P.G. 209-33 | Voiding a TVB Summons | completed |
| P.G. 209-34 | Voiding a Criminal Court Summons | completed |
| P.G. 209-35 | GPS/Electronic Tracking Devices | completed |
| P.G. 209-37 | Speed Detection Devices | completed |
| P.G. 209-39 | Red Light Camera Violations | completed |
| P.G. 209-40 | Speed Camera Violations | completed |

## Sergeant Focus Areas

### Key Supervisor Procedures
- **P.G. 209-01 — Conditions of Service:** Verify officers check age (16+), capacity (able to care for self), and offense eligibility before issuing summonses; ensure VTL exceptions (Leaving Scene, Dangerous Articles, False Statements, Stolen Vehicle, DWI) result in arrest, not summons
- **P.G. 209-03 — CJRA Civil Summons:** Patrol supervisor MUST respond to verify Code 3 (law enforcement exception); desk officer makes FINAL determination on identity/residence; verify OATH Recidivist screening before civil summons issuance
- **P.G. 209-07 — Parking Summonses:** Monitor 5 mandatory ID elements (plate number, type, expiration, make, model) — omission = dismissal; enforce 3-summons max per vehicle per day; ensure no summonses issued to abandoned/derelict vehicles
- **P.G. 209-09 — Personal Service:** Verify Vehicle Reports completed for ALL vehicle/bicycle/e-scooter/e-bike stops; ensure one violation per summons; RTR notation when violator records activity
- **P.G. 209-24 — License Checks:** Mandatory at traffic collisions, car stops, and investigatory situations; do NOT detain solely for license check; Activity Log entry when DMV system is inoperative
- **P.G. 209-26 — Suspended License:** Verify proper confiscation and PD634-152 preparation; accept 14-day correction proof (validated license, temporary license, DMV receipt); park vehicle legally for registered owner
- **P.G. 209-27 — Ignition Key:** 3-minute threshold for removal; PD571-121 in duplicate; 48-hour deadline for Property Clerk invoicing
- **P.G. 209-30 — Dept Vehicle Summons:** Review Summons Plea Forms within 30 days; scrutinize "necessary police action" claims — convenience is NOT a valid defense; know the safety violation list
- **P.G. 209-31 to 209-34 — Voided Summonses:** Supervisory review required for all voided summonses; monitor voiding patterns for training deficiencies or integrity concerns
chapters.keyTerms:
# Section 209 — Key Terms, Forms, and Abbreviations

---

## Core Summons Definitions

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Summons** | A written directive ordering a person to appear before a court or administrative tribunal at a specified date and time to answer for a violation or offense | P.G. 209-01 |
| **Civil Summons (OATH)** | A summons issued under the CJRA for one of 6 specified violations, returnable to OATH instead of Criminal Court | P.G. 209-03 |
| **Personal Service** | Direct hand-delivery of a summons to the violator, with verbal notification of the offense charged | P.G. 209-09 |

## CJRA and OATH Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **CJRA (Criminal Justice Reform Act of 2016)** | Legislation allowing 6 specified violations to be processed as civil summonses returnable to OATH rather than criminal summonses returnable to Criminal Court | P.G. 209-03 |
| **OATH (Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings)** | NYC administrative tribunal that adjudicates civil violations including CJRA offenses, vendor violations, health code violations, noise complaints, and environmental offenses | P.G. 209-03, 209-12 |
| **OATH Recidivist** | A person ineligible for CJRA civil summons due to: (1) 2+ felony arrests in past 2 years, (2) 3+ unanswered OATH summonses in past 8 years, OR (3) currently on parole or probation | P.G. 209-03 |
| **ECB (Environmental Control Board)** | Now part of OATH; adjudicates environmental, building, and zoning violations | P.G. 209-20 |

## CJRA Violations — The 6 Qualifying Offenses

| # | Violation | Code Section | Source |
|---|-----------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | Open Container (alcohol) | AC 10-125(b) | P.G. 209-03 |
| 2 | Public Urination | AC 16-118(6) | P.G. 209-03 |
| 3 | Littering | AC 16-118(1)(a) | P.G. 209-03 |
| 4 | Spitting | AC 16-118(1)(b) | P.G. 209-03 |
| 5 | Excessive Noise | AC 24-218 | P.G. 209-03 |
| 6 | NYC Park Rules | Title 56 | P.G. 209-03 |

## NYPD Codes (CJRA Processing)

| Code | Meaning | Supervisor Involvement | Source |
|------|---------|----------------------|--------|
| **Code 1** | OATH Recidivist — issue Criminal Court summons | None required | P.G. 209-03 |
| **Code 2** | Active warrant or I-card — issue Criminal Court summons | None required | P.G. 209-03 |
| **Code 3** | Law enforcement exception — issue Criminal Court summons | **Patrol supervisor MUST respond** to verify | P.G. 209-03 |
| **Code 4** | Non-CJRA violation or multiple violations | None required | P.G. 209-03 |

## Vehicle Definitions

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Abandoned Vehicle** | A vehicle with: (1) no plates for 6+ hours, (2) on public street for 7+ consecutive days, OR (3) on another's property for 96+ hours | P.G. 209-07 |
| **Derelict Vehicle** | A vehicle that is dilapidated, burned, or stripped, with no value except as scrap metal | P.G. 209-07 |

## Department Vehicle Summons Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Urgent Police Action** | Response to an **emergency**, active **pursuit**, or moving **surveillance** | P.G. 209-30 |
| **Necessary Police Action** | Less than urgent; **enhances safety** of an operation; NOT solely for the member's **convenience** | P.G. 209-30 |
| **Valid Verifiable Defense** | A legitimate, verifiable defense to a summons; safety violations are NOT eligible unless urgent or necessary police action occurred | P.G. 209-30 |
| **Safety Violation** | Specific parking violations (double parking, fire hydrant, sidewalk, bus stop, crosswalk, obstruction of traffic, no standing, snow emergency, no parking-authorized vehicles, no stopping, no parking-taxi stand) requiring urgent/necessary police action defense | P.G. 209-30 |

## License-Related Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Suspended License** | A driver's license that has been temporarily withdrawn by DMV; operator is prohibited from driving | P.G. 209-26 |
| **Revoked License** | A driver's license that has been permanently withdrawn by DMV; operator must reapply | P.G. 209-26 |
| **14-Day Correction Window** | Period within which a violator may claim suspension/revocation was corrected; accept validated license, temporary license, or DMV receipt | P.G. 209-26 |
| **False Personation** | PL 190.23; providing a false identity, particularly relevant during suspended/revoked license stops | P.G. 209-26 |

## Returnable Agencies

| Agency | Abbreviation | Handles | Source |
|--------|-------------|---------|--------|
| NYC Department of Finance — Parking Violations Operations | DOF/PVO | Parking summonses | P.G. 209-02 |
| NYS DMV Traffic Violations Bureau | TVB | Non-parking traffic infractions | P.G. 209-02 |
| NYC Criminal Court | — | Traffic misdemeanors, pedestrian offenses, Criminal Court summonses | P.G. 209-02 |
| Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings | OATH | CJRA civil summonses, vendor/health/noise/sanitation/air/graffiti violations | P.G. 209-02, 209-03 |
| Transit Adjudication Bureau | TAB | Transit system violations (28-day return) | P.G. 209-04, 209-15 |

## DAT vs. Summons Distinction

| Feature | DAT (Desk Appearance Ticket) | Summons |
|---------|------------------------------|---------|
| **Issued after** | Custodial arrest and processing | Field encounter (no arrest) |
| **Requires arrest** | Yes — person is arrested, processed, then released with DAT | No — issued in lieu of arrest |
| **Fingerprinted** | Yes | No |
| **Eligible offenses** | Class E felonies, misdemeanors, violations | Violations, infractions, certain misdemeanors |
| **Reference** | P.G. 208-27 | P.G. 209-01 |

---

## Forms

| Form Name | Form Number | Purpose | Reference |
|-----------|-------------|---------|-----------|
| Parking Summons | — | Issued for parking violations; returnable to DOF/PVO | P.G. 209-07 |
| TVB Summons | — | Issued for non-parking traffic infractions; returnable to TVB | P.G. 209-10 |
| Criminal Court Summons | — | Issued for traffic misdemeanors, pedestrian offenses, other Criminal Court matters | P.G. 209-11 |
| OATH Civil Summons | — | Issued for CJRA violations and other OATH-eligible offenses | P.G. 209-03, 209-12 |
| TAB Summons | — | Issued for transit system violations; 28-day return | P.G. 209-15 |
| REMOVAL OF IGNITION KEY | PD571-121 | Prepared in duplicate when key removed from unattended vehicle; one copy on steering wheel, one attached to key | P.G. 209-27 |
| SEIZED DRIVER'S LICENSE RECEIPT/REPORT | PD634-152 | Prepared when confiscating a suspended/revoked license | P.G. 209-26 |
| SUMMONS PLEA FORM | — | Used to contest a summons issued to a department vehicle; must be forwarded within 30 days | P.G. 209-30 |
| VEHICLE REPORT | — | Completed in FORMS for every vehicle/bicycle/e-scooter/e-bike stop | P.G. 209-09 |

---

## Abbreviations

| Abbreviation | Full Name | Context |
|-------------|-----------|---------|
| **AC** | Administrative Code (NYC) | Source of CJRA violations and other city offenses |
| **BWC** | Body-Worn Camera | Activated during enforcement encounters |
| **CJRA** | Criminal Justice Reform Act (2016) | Allows 6 violations as civil summonses to OATH |
| **DMV** | Department of Motor Vehicles (NYS) | Operates the TVB; license status checks |
| **DOF** | Department of Finance (NYC) | Adjudicates parking violations, red light/speed camera violations |
| **ECB** | Environmental Control Board | Now part of OATH; environmental/building/zoning violations |
| **NYSPIN** | New York Statewide Police Information Network | Used to check stolen vehicle status |
| **OATH** | Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings | Adjudicates civil summonses |
| **PL** | Penal Law (NYS) | Source of criminal violations |
| **PVO** | Parking Violations Operations | Division within DOF handling parking summonses |
| **RTR** | Right to Record | Notation on summons when violator records police activity |
| **TAB** | Transit Adjudication Bureau | Adjudicates transit system violations; 28-day return |
| **TVB** | Traffic Violations Bureau | Adjudicates non-parking traffic infractions |
| **VTL** | Vehicle and Traffic Law (NYS) | Source of traffic offenses |

---

## Critical Numbers

| Number | What It Represents | Source |
|--------|-------------------|--------|
| **3 minutes** | Threshold for unattended vehicle with key in ignition before removal | P.G. 209-27 |
| **6 hours** | No plates displayed = abandoned vehicle | P.G. 209-07 |
| **7 days** | On public street = abandoned vehicle | P.G. 209-07 |
| **96 hours** | On another's property = abandoned vehicle | P.G. 209-07 |
| **48 hours** | Unclaimed ignition key invoiced to Property Clerk | P.G. 209-27 |
| **14 days** | Correction window for suspended/revoked license claims | P.G. 209-26 |
| **28 days** | TAB summons return date (calendar days from issuance) | P.G. 209-04 |
| **30 days** | Deadline to forward Summons Plea Form for dept vehicle summons | P.G. 209-30 |
| **2 years** | Lookback period for felony arrests (OATH Recidivist — 2+ arrests) | P.G. 209-03 |
| **8 years** | Lookback period for unanswered OATH summonses (Recidivist — 3+ unanswered) | P.G. 209-03 |
| **3** | Maximum parking summonses to same vehicle at same location per day | P.G. 209-07 |
| **5** | Mandatory ID elements on parking summons (omission = dismissal) | P.G. 209-07 |
| **6** | Number of CJRA-qualifying violations | P.G. 209-03 |
| **16** | Minimum age to receive a summons | P.G. 209-01 |

questions.text: An officer stops a 16-year-old who committed a non-VTL misdemeanor. Can the officer issue a Criminal Court summons?
options: A) Yes, 16-year-olds may receive summonses for all offenses
B) Yes, but only with supervisor approval
C) No, 16-year-olds may receive Criminal Court summonses for violations and VTL misdemeanors only
D) No, 16-year-olds are never eligible for summonses
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-01, 16-year-olds may receive Criminal Court summonses for ALL violations and for VTL misdemeanors. Non-VTL misdemeanors are NOT eligible for summons issuance to 16-year-olds.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-01, 16-year-olds may receive Criminal Court summonses for ALL violations and for VTL misdemeanors. Non-VTL misdemeanors are NOT eligible for summons issuance to 16-year-olds.

questions.text: Which of the following VTL offenses may be handled by summons rather than arrest?
options: B) VTL 600 — Leaving the Scene of an Accident
D) VTL 392 — False Statements regarding vehicle registration
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-01, VTL 511 (Aggravated Unlicensed Operation) is NOT among the VTL exceptions. The five VTL exception categories requiring arrest are: Section 600 (Leaving Scene), Section 380 (Dangerous Articles), Sections 392/392-a (False Statements), Sections 421/422/423/426 (Stolen Vehicle), and Section 1192 (DWI/DWAI).
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-01, VTL 511 (Aggravated Unlicensed Operation) is NOT among the VTL exceptions. The five VTL exception categories requiring arrest are: Section 600 (Leaving Scene), Section 380 (Dangerous Articles), Sections 392/392-a (False Statements), Sections 421/422/423/426 (Stolen Vehicle), and Section 1192 (DWI/DWAI).

questions.text: A person is stopped for public urination and is found to have 2 felony arrests in the past 18 months and 1 unanswered OATH summons. What type of summons should be issued?
options: A) OATH civil summons — the person does not meet recidivist criteria
B) Criminal Court summons — the person is an OATH Recidivist
C) No summons — the person must be arrested
D) TAB summons — public urination falls under transit jurisdiction
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 209-03, an OATH Recidivist is defined as someone with 2 or more felony arrests in the past 2 years, 3 or more unanswered OATH summonses in the past 8 years, OR currently on parole/probation. This person has 2 felony arrests in 18 months (within 2 years), meeting the first criterion. A Criminal Court summons must be issued instead of a CJRA civil summons.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-03, an OATH Recidivist is defined as someone with 2 or more felony arrests in the past 2 years, 3 or more unanswered OATH summonses in the past 8 years, OR currently on parole/probation. This person has 2 felony arrests in 18 months (within 2 years), meeting the first criterion. A Criminal Court summons must be issued instead of a CJRA civil summons.

questions.text: An officer issues a Criminal Court summons under NYPD Code 3 (law enforcement exception) for an open container violation. What supervisory action is required?
options: A) No supervisory action is required for Code 3
B) The desk officer must approve the Code 3 designation
C) A patrol supervisor must respond to the scene to verify the exception
D) The commanding officer must be notified within 24 hours
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-03, when a Criminal Court summons is issued under Code 3 (law enforcement exception), a patrol supervisor MUST respond to the scene to verify the exception. This is a mandatory supervisory response.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-03, when a Criminal Court summons is issued under Code 3 (law enforcement exception), a patrol supervisor MUST respond to the scene to verify the exception. This is a mandatory supervisory response.

questions.text: An officer is preparing a parking summons and records the license plate number, plate type, make, and model, but forgets to include the expiration date. What is the likely outcome?
options: A) The summons will be processed normally with a minor notation
B) The summons will be DISMISSED due to the missing mandatory element
C) The summons will be returned for correction within 30 days
D) The court will obtain the expiration date from DMV records
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 209-07, there are 5 mandatory identification elements on a parking summons: license plate number, plate type, expiration date, make, and model. Omission of ANY one of these elements results in automatic DISMISSAL of the summons.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-07, there are 5 mandatory identification elements on a parking summons: license plate number, plate type, expiration date, make, and model. Omission of ANY one of these elements results in automatic DISMISSAL of the summons.

questions.text: A vehicle has been parked on a public street without moving for 8 consecutive days. Under the Patrol Guide, this vehicle is classified as:
options: C) A nuisance vehicle requiring towing
D) A legally parked vehicle unless in a restricted zone
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 209-07, a vehicle on a public street for more than 7 consecutive days is classified as an abandoned vehicle. A derelict vehicle is one that is dilapidated, burned, or stripped with no value except as scrap. Parking summonses should NOT be issued to abandoned or derelict vehicles.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-07, a vehicle on a public street for more than 7 consecutive days is classified as an abandoned vehicle. A derelict vehicle is one that is dilapidated, burned, or stripped with no value except as scrap. Parking summonses should NOT be issued to abandoned or derelict vehicles.

questions.text: Where are pedestrian violation summonses returnable?
options: A) NYS DMV Traffic Violations Bureau
D) Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings (OATH)
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-02, pedestrian offenses are returnable to NYC Criminal Court. The TVB handles non-parking traffic infractions. The Department of Finance handles parking violations. OATH handles CJRA civil summonses and other administrative violations.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-02, pedestrian offenses are returnable to NYC Criminal Court. The TVB handles non-parking traffic infractions. The Department of Finance handles parking violations. OATH handles CJRA civil summonses and other administrative violations.

questions.text: What is the return date for a summons issued through the Transit Adjudication Bureau (TAB)?
options: A) 14 calendar days from date of issuance
B) 21 calendar days from date of issuance
C) 28 calendar days from date of issuance, with weekend/holiday adjustment
D) Per the daily FINEST message
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-04, TAB summonses have a return date of 28 calendar days from the date of issuance. If the 28th day falls on a weekend or holiday, the return date moves to the next business day. Criminal Court summons dates are set per the daily FINEST message.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-04, TAB summonses have a return date of 28 calendar days from the date of issuance. If the 28th day falls on a weekend or holiday, the return date moves to the next business day. Criminal Court summons dates are set per the daily FINEST message.

questions.text: During a car stop, the officer discovers the driver's license has been suspended. The driver claims the suspension was corrected 10 days ago and shows a DMV receipt. What should the officer do?
options: A) Confiscate the license and issue a summons — only a validated license is acceptable
B) Accept the DMV receipt as proof of correction within the 14-day window
C) Radio dispatch to verify the DMV receipt before accepting it
D) Arrest the driver for operating with a suspended license
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 209-26, if a violator claims the suspension/revocation was corrected within the past 14 days, officers should accept a newly validated license, a temporary license, OR a DMV receipt showing correction. A DMV receipt within 10 days falls within the 14-day window.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-26, if a violator claims the suspension/revocation was corrected within the past 14 days, officers should accept a newly validated license, a temporary license, OR a DMV receipt showing correction. A DMV receipt within 10 days falls within the 14-day window.

questions.text: An officer removes the ignition key from an unattended vehicle. The owner does not return. After what period does the desk officer invoice the key to the Property Clerk?
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 209-27, if the ignition key is not claimed within 48 hours, the desk officer invoices it to the Property Clerk. The ignition key is removed only after the vehicle has been unattended with the key in the ignition for more than 3 minutes.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-27, if the ignition key is not claimed within 48 hours, the desk officer invoices it to the Property Clerk. The ignition key is removed only after the vehicle has been unattended with the key in the ignition for more than 3 minutes.
