questions.text: A member parked the department vehicle at a fire hydrant while responding to an emergency call. A parking summons was issued. What type of defense applies?
options: A) Valid Verifiable Defense — fire hydrant is not a safety violation
B) No defense — department vehicles are not exempt from parking rules
C) Urgent Police Action — response to an emergency qualifies
D) Necessary Police Action — the parking enhanced safety of the operation
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-30, response to an emergency constitutes Urgent Police Action (along with active pursuit and moving surveillance). Fire hydrant parking is a safety violation, which requires proof of urgent or necessary police action. Since the member was responding to an emergency, Urgent Police Action applies.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-30, response to an emergency constitutes Urgent Police Action (along with active pursuit and moving surveillance). Fire hydrant parking is a safety violation, which requires proof of urgent or necessary police action. Since the member was responding to an emergency, Urgent Police Action applies.

questions.text: A member parks on the sidewalk to pick up lunch from a restaurant. A summons is issued. Is "Necessary Police Action" a valid defense?
options: A) Yes, because the member was in uniform and on duty
B) Yes, because sidewalk parking is not classified as a safety violation
C) No, Necessary Police Action specifically excludes actions taken solely for the member's convenience
D) No, only Urgent Police Action can be used for sidewalk violations
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-30, Necessary Police Action is defined as less than urgent but enhancing safety of an operation — and specifically excludes actions taken solely for the member's convenience. Picking up lunch is a personal convenience, not a police operation.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-30, Necessary Police Action is defined as less than urgent but enhancing safety of an operation — and specifically excludes actions taken solely for the member's convenience. Picking up lunch is a personal convenience, not a police operation.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the 6 CJRA violations eligible for OATH civil summons?
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 209-03, Disorderly Conduct (PL 240.20) is NOT one of the 6 CJRA violations. The 6 CJRA violations are: Open Container, Public Urination, Littering, Spitting, Excessive Noise, and NYC Park Rules (Title 56).
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-03, Disorderly Conduct (PL 240.20) is NOT one of the 6 CJRA violations. The 6 CJRA violations are: Open Container, Public Urination, Littering, Spitting, Excessive Noise, and NYC Park Rules (Title 56).

questions.text: A tow truck registered in New Jersey enters NYC to pick up a broken-down vehicle and tow it back to a repair shop in New Jersey. The tow truck does not have an NYC tow truck license. Should the officer issue a summons?
options: A) Yes, all tow trucks operating in NYC must be licensed under AC 20-496(b)
B) No, out-of-city tow trucks picking up a vehicle for transport outside the city are exempt
C) Yes, but only a warning summons since it is a first offense
D) No, New Jersey tow trucks are covered under interstate commerce exemptions
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 209-28, the NYC tow truck licensing requirement does NOT apply to out-of-city tow trucks that are: (1) passing through NYC, (2) picking up a vehicle to transport outside the city, or (3) dropping off a vehicle that originated from outside the city. This tow truck from NJ picking up a vehicle to tow back to NJ falls under exemption (2).
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-28, the NYC tow truck licensing requirement does NOT apply to out-of-city tow trucks that are: (1) passing through NYC, (2) picking up a vehicle to transport outside the city, or (3) dropping off a vehicle that originated from outside the city. This tow truck from NJ picking up a vehicle to tow back to NJ falls under exemption (2).

questions.text: A person stopped for littering has no valid ID. The officer confirms the violation is CJRA-eligible. What is the correct course of action?
options: A) Issue the OATH civil summons using the person's verbal identification
B) Release the person with a warning since no ID is available
C) Remove the person to the command for identification
D) Issue a Criminal Court summons instead, since OATH requires valid ID
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-03, if a violator has no valid ID, the officer must remove the person to the command for identification. The desk officer makes the FINAL determination on identity and residence verification. The summons type (OATH civil or Criminal Court) is determined after identity is established.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-03, if a violator has no valid ID, the officer must remove the person to the command for identification. The desk officer makes the FINAL determination on identity and residence verification. The summons type (OATH civil or Criminal Court) is determined after identity is established.

questions.text: A UMOS writes a "C" summons instead of an OATH for an open container violation and selects Code 1 (OATH Recidivist). Must a patrol supervisor respond to the scene?
options: A) Yes, a supervisor must respond any time a "C" is issued instead of an OATH
B) Yes, the desk officer must verify all "C" summons codes
C) No, a patrol supervisor must respond to the scene ONLY for Code 3 (law enforcement exception)
D) No, but the commanding officer must be notified within 24 hours
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-03, a patrol supervisor must respond to the scene and verify the exception ONLY when Code 3 (law enforcement exception) is used. Codes 1, 2, and 4 do not require a supervisory response. This is a frequently tested distinction.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-03, a patrol supervisor must respond to the scene and verify the exception ONLY when Code 3 (law enforcement exception) is used. Codes 1, 2, and 4 do not require a supervisory response. This is a frequently tested distinction.

questions.text: When a UMOS finishes a summons pack, the served summonses should be given to:
options: A) The Desk Officer for review and filing
B) The Operations Coordinator for data entry
C) Clerical staff, with the appropriate copy of served summonses
D) The Integrity Control Officer for accountability review
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-36, when the summons pack is finished, the UMOS signs the Certification Card and gives the summonses to **clerical staff** (not the D.O.), along with the appropriate copy of served summonses.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-36, when the summons pack is finished, the UMOS signs the Certification Card and gives the summonses to **clerical staff** (not the D.O.), along with the appropriate copy of served summonses.

questions.text: TABs and OATHs come in packs of how many summonses? And A, B, and C summonses?
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 209-36, TABs and OATHs come in packs of **25**, while A (Parking), B (Moving), and C (Criminal Court) summonses come in packs of **20**.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-36, TABs and OATHs come in packs of **25**, while A (Parking), B (Moving), and C (Criminal Court) summonses come in packs of **20**.

questions.text: The ICO Ageing Report monitors summonses that have been distributed but not served for how many days?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-36, the ICO Ageing Report monitors summonses distributed but not served for **90 days**. If any UMOS appears on the ICO Ageing Report, the ICO must conduct an **IMMEDIATE investigation**. The exam commonly tests 30 days as a distractor.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-36, the ICO Ageing Report monitors summonses distributed but not served for **90 days**. If any UMOS appears on the ICO Ageing Report, the ICO must conduct an **IMMEDIATE investigation**. The exam commonly tests 30 days as a distractor.

questions.text: An OATH summons is written for a non-CJRA violation based on a witness statement. The witness refuses to sign the Statement of Witness section. What should the officer write in the witness signature caption?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-12, if the witness refuses to ID themselves and/or sign the Statement of Witness, the officer writes **"REFUSED"** in the witness signature caption. Note: CJRA violations (209-03) must be personally observed and cannot be based on witness statements — only non-CJRA OATH summonses (209-12) can use "informed by."
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-12, if the witness refuses to ID themselves and/or sign the Statement of Witness, the officer writes **"REFUSED"** in the witness signature caption. Note: CJRA violations (209-03) must be personally observed and cannot be based on witness statements — only non-CJRA OATH summonses (209-12) can use "informed by."

questions.text: Missing summonses are discovered when an officer is being transferred from the Department. Who must the Desk Officer notify?
options: B) IAB and the Commanding Officer
C) IAB and the ICO immediately; if ICO unavailable, the CO or XO
D) IAB and the Duty Captain
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 209-36, when an officer is transferred or separated from the Department and summonses are missing, the D.O. must call **IAB AND** notify the **ICO immediately**. If the ICO is unavailable, notify the **CO or XO** — NOT the Duty Captain.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-36, when an officer is transferred or separated from the Department and summonses are missing, the D.O. must call **IAB AND** notify the **ICO immediately**. If the ICO is unavailable, notify the **CO or XO** — NOT the Duty Captain.

questions.text: During a mandatory driver's license check under PG 209-24, the operator intentionally provides a false identity. What charge applies?
options: A) Criminal Impersonation — Class A Misdemeanor
B) False Personation — Class B Misdemeanor
C) Obstructing Governmental Administration — Class A Misdemeanor
D) Identity Theft — Class E Felony
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 209-24, when an operator intentionally lies regarding their identification to deceive a police officer, they can be charged with **False Personation (PL 190.23)**, which is a **Class B Misdemeanor**.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 209-24, when an operator intentionally lies regarding their identification to deceive a police officer, they can be charged with **False Personation (PL 190.23)**, which is a **Class B Misdemeanor**.

sergeantFocus.text: Supervisors must verify that officers correctly distinguish between violations (summons-eligible for 16-year-olds) and non-VTL misdemeanors (not summons-eligible for 16-year-olds). This is a common error in the field.

When a Criminal Court summons is issued under **Code 3** (law enforcement exception), a **patrol supervisor MUST respond to the scene** to verify the exception. This is a mandatory supervisory response — failure to respond is a procedural violation. Ensure officer details reason in digital Activity Log.
Supervisors should ensure officers correctly route OATH-eligible offenses to OATH rather than Criminal Court. Misrouting creates unnecessary court appearances and administrative burden.
Supervisors should periodically review parking summons issuance to verify all 5 mandatory elements are present. A pattern of dismissals due to missing elements indicates a training deficiency.

The desk officer is responsible for tracking unclaimed keys and ensuring timely invoicing to the Property Clerk after 48 hours. Supervisors should include ignition key removals in their review of daily activity.
Supervisors should monitor that officers conduct mandatory license checks at every qualifying stop. The Activity Log entry when DMV is inoperative creates the audit trail.

Verify that officers park the violator's vehicle in a legal area rather than leaving it in traffic. The vehicle remains until the registered owner (not the violator with the suspended license) arranges removal.

Supervisors must review Summons Plea Forms for accuracy and ensure they are forwarded within the 30-day deadline. Verify that officers claiming "necessary police action" can articulate how the action enhanced safety — "convenience" is explicitly excluded.

Supervisors play a critical role in the summons voiding process. Every voided summons requires supervisory review to prevent abuse. Monitor voiding patterns — an officer with an unusually high void rate may indicate a training issue or integrity concern.
Supervisors must verify that Vehicle Reports are completed for every stop. Missing reports indicate either unreported stops or incomplete documentation — both require corrective action.

chapters.title: Prisoners
chapters.readme:
# Section 210 — Prisoners

## Source
- **Patrol Guide Section:** 210
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide2.pdf
- **Priority:** High
- **Status:** Completed

## Learning Objectives
- Understand general procedures for safeguarding prisoners in police custody
- Know the Desk Officer's responsibilities for prisoner intake, detention, meals, and release
- Identify proper prisoner search, transport, and monitoring protocols
- Recognize holdover prisoner procedures and borough court section coordination
- Master transport rules including rear cuffing, transport chain thresholds, and leg restraint requirements
- Know high risk prisoner factors and the additional precautions they trigger
- Understand pregnant prisoner restraint procedures and the sergeant's assessment role
- Know attorney interview and parent/guardian visit rules (location, duration, supervision)
- Understand bedside arraignment procedures, MLD requirements, and bail posting rules
- Know prisoner meal rules ($1.80 max, three meal periods, no hot liquids)
- Master holding pen guidelines (30-minute inspections, 3-hour max, separation rules, item removal)
- Distinguish between voided arrests and decline prosecutions — procedures, documentation, and authority
- Understand warrant check requirements before release and record sealing for voided arrests
- Know inter-agency procedures for hospitalized prisoners and release conflict resolution

## Procedures

| Procedure | Title | Status |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| P.G. 210-01 | Prisoners General Procedure | completed |
| P.G. 210-02 | Hospitalized Prisoners | completed |
| P.G. 210-03 | Hospitalized Prisoners — Arrests by Other Agencies | completed |
| P.G. 210-04 | Medical Treatment of Prisoners / Psychiatric Treatment | completed |
| P.G. 210-05 | Bedside Arraignment | completed |
| P.G. 210-06 | Prisoner Meals | completed |
| P.G. 210-07 | Unusual Occurrences Involving Prisoners | completed |
| P.G. 210-08 | Guidelines for Prisoner Holding Pens | completed |
| P.G. 210-13 | Release of Prisoners | completed |
| P.G. 210-15 | Release of Prisoners Arrested by Other Agencies | completed |
| P.G. 210-16 | Release at Complaint Room by Direction of ADA | completed |
| P.G. 210-17 | Special Category Prisoners | completed |

## Key Terms
- **Holdover Prisoner** — A prisoner who has entered the court process but has not yet been arraigned and has been returned to police custody for overnight detention
- **Voided Arrest** — Release when reasonable cause to believe prisoner did not commit the offense, or no offense was committed
- **Decline Prosecution** — DA exercises discretion not to prosecute; requires Decline Prosecution Letter from ADA
- **High Risk Prisoner** — Assessed based on 7 factors: seriousness of offense, refusal to identify, forcible resistance, threats, violence history, weapons, escape history
- **Prisoner Security Guidelines** — All prisoners searched on entry/exit from any police, detention, DA, or court facility; searched before transport and upon arrival
- **Mobile Livescan Device (MLD)** — Portable fingerprint scanner for hospitalized prisoners; requires 2 MOS present; prohibited for other purposes
- **Transport Chains** — Required for more than 2 prisoners; cuffs must be double locked

## Sergeant Focus Areas

### Key Supervisor Procedures
- **P.G. 210-01 — General Procedure:** Desk officer must step from behind desk to verify prisoner security before transport; immediate supervisor determines pregnant prisoner restraint method; authorize and monitor attorney/parent visits
- **P.G. 210-04 — Medical Treatment:** Patrol supervisor must be notified when prisoner requires medical treatment; verify documentation and guard assignment
- **P.G. 210-05 — Bedside Arraignment:** Monitor 6-day timeline for borough court section notification; verify 2 MOS present for MLD fingerprinting
- **P.G. 210-06 — Meals:** Desk officer authorizes additional meals for medical/unusual conditions; expense report due by 15th of month
- **P.G. 210-08 — Holding Pens:** Sergeant must personally and periodically inspect prisoners, recording condition and position; verify 30-minute MOS inspections; enforce 3-hour maximum with Command Log entries for extensions
- **P.G. 210-13 — Release:** Patrol supervisor is primary authority for consent to release; verify voided arrest documentation (5 elements on OLBS Arrest Worksheet); ensure BCS intake supervisor seals fingerprint records
- **P.G. 210-15 — Other Agency Release:** Escalate inter-agency conflicts to precinct commander/duty captain
- **P.G. 210-16 — ADA Release:** Verify warrant check before release; process on warrant only if active warrant found
chapters.keyTerms:
# Section 210 — Key Terms, Forms, and Critical Numbers

---

## Core Prisoner Definitions

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Holdover Prisoner** | A prisoner who has entered the court process, has NOT yet been arraigned, and has been returned to police custody for overnight detention | P.G. 210-01 |
| **Voided Arrest** | Release of a prisoner when reasonable cause exists to believe the prisoner did NOT commit the offense, or that no offense was committed | P.G. 210-13 |
| **Decline Prosecution** | DA exercises discretion not to prosecute a validly arrested prisoner; requires a Decline Prosecution Letter from an ADA | P.G. 210-13 |
| **High Risk Prisoner** | A prisoner assessed as high risk based on: seriousness of offense, refusal to identify, forcible resistance, threats of violence/escape, history of violence, weapons possession, or escape/attempted escape history | P.G. 210-01 |
| **Prisoner Security Guidelines** | All prisoners will be searched on entry to and exit from any police facility, detention facility, DA facility, or court facility; also searched before transport departure and upon arrival | P.G. 210-01 |

## Medical and Arraignment Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Mobile Livescan Device (MLD)** | Portable fingerprint scanner that transmits via cellular to DCJS; used exclusively for fingerprinting hospitalized prisoners; requires at least 2 MOS present | P.G. 210-05 |
| **Bedside Arraignment** | Arraignment conducted at the hospital to reduce manpower required to guard hospitalized prisoners | P.G. 210-05 |
| **Prison Ward** | Designated area within a hospital for the treatment and detention of prisoners under guard | P.G. 210-02 |

## Transport and Restraint Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Transport Chains** | Restraint system used when transporting more than 2 prisoners; cuffs must be double locked | P.G. 210-01 |
| **Leg Restraints** | Additional restraint applied to legs; MUST be used when prisoner refused fingerprinting and for ALL hospital transports; may NOT be used for punitive purposes; does not replace rear handcuffing | P.G. 210-01 |
| **Rear Cuff** | Standard restraint for ALL prisoners during transport; hands cuffed behind the back | P.G. 210-01 |

## Police Action Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Urgent Police Action** | Response to an emergency, active pursuit, or moving surveillance | P.G. 209-30 |
| **Necessary Police Action** | Less than urgent; enhances safety of an operation; NOT solely for the member's convenience | P.G. 209-30 |

## Hospitalized Prisoner Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **ACR (Ambulance Call Report)** | Report number obtained from ambulance attendant when prisoner is transported by EMS; recorded in Section I of MTP | P.G. 210-04 |
| **PCR (Patient Care Report)** | Report number obtained from ambulance attendant when prisoner is transported by RMP or other means (not EMS); recorded in Section I of MTP | P.G. 210-04 |
| **PIN (Patient Identification Number)** | Number obtained from hospital intake people when prisoner is transferred to Bellevue/Elmhurst for psychiatric admission; NOT the same as ACR or PCR | P.G. 210-04 |
| **UF-50** | A memo with the Official Seal of NYC preprinted in the upper left corner; used for communications outside the Department; issued to authorize family visits to hospitalized prisoners | P.G. 210-02 |
| **Plastic Flex Cuffs** | Additional data restraint used when a hospitalized prisoner admitted to ICU is assessed as having escape potential | P.G. 210-02 |
| **Long Chain Cuffs** | Restraint used when prisoner is comatose, paralyzed, or in last stage of pregnancy | P.G. 210-02 |
| **Prisoner Unusual** | An unarraigned adult prisoner or juvenile under 18 in NYPD custody or Corrections who: assaults MOS, is sexually assaulted/harassed (or alleges such), escapes/attempts, or other unusual occurrence | P.G. 210-07 |
| **Typed Letterhead (49)** | Report prepared by the C.O./Duty Captain for prisoner unusual occurrences; forwarded to Chief of Department (3 copies adult, 2 copies child DOA, 1 copy child in custody) | P.G. 210-07 |

## Other Agency Terms

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Other Police Agencies** | DOC, NYS DOCCS, Court Officers, Port Authority Police, MTA Police, Amtrak Police, Sea Gate Police, FDNY Fire Marshals, DEP Police, TBTA Police | P.G. 210-03 |

---

## Forms

| Form Name | Form Number | Purpose | Reference |
|-----------|-------------|---------|-----------|
| Prisoner Roster | **PD244-145** | Tracks all prisoners in custody at a command | P.G. 210-01 |
| Prisoner Holding Pen Roster | **PD244-1410** | Tracks all prisoners placed in holding pens; includes 30-minute inspection records | P.G. 210-08 |
| Medical Treatment of Prisoner | **PD244-150** | Documents medical treatment provided to or requested by a prisoner | P.G. 210-04 |
| Transport Dispatch | **PD171-132** | Records prisoner transport details including destination and escort | P.G. 210-01 |
| Meal Voucher | **PD144-051** | Documents prisoner meal purchases; max $1.80 per meal | P.G. 210-06 |
| Arrest Report Supplement | **PD244-157** | Records attorney visit details: name, address, phone, who retained, interview status, arrival/departure times | P.G. 210-01 |
| Medical Treatment of Prisoner (MTP) | **PD244-150** | Documents medical treatment; use "Reserve" function in FORMS app; copy accompanies prisoner until no longer in NYPD custody | P.G. 210-04 |
| Decline Prosecution Letter | — | Issued by ADA when DA declines to prosecute; required before prisoner release | P.G. 210-16 |
| OLBS Arrest Worksheet | — | Must include reason for void, property returned, witnesses, authorizing supervisor, and time of release for voided arrests | P.G. 210-13 |
| Expense Report | — | Submitted by the 15th of each month for prisoner meal expenditures | P.G. 210-06 |

---

## Abbreviations

| Abbreviation | Full Name | Context |
|-------------|-----------|---------|
| **BCS** | Borough Court Section | Coordinates cell space, intake processing, court appearances |
| **DCJS** | Division of Criminal Justice Services | Receives fingerprint transmissions; records sealed for voided arrests |
| **DOC** | Department of Correction (NYC) | Listed as "other police agency" for hospitalized prisoner guarding |
| **DOCCS** | Department of Corrections and Community Supervision (NYS) | State corrections; listed as other police agency; also authorized to interview prisoners |
| **MLD** | Mobile Livescan Device | Portable fingerprint scanner for hospitalized prisoners |
| **MOS** | Member of Service | NYPD personnel |
| **OLBS** | On-Line Booking System | Used to process arrest worksheets including voided arrests |
| **TBTA** | Triborough Bridge and Tunnel Authority | Listed as other police agency |
| **ACR** | Ambulance Call Report | Number for EMS transport; recorded on MTP Section I |
| **PCR** | Patient Care Report | Number for RMP/other transport; recorded on MTP Section I |
| **PIN** | Patient Identification Number | Number from hospital intake for psych transfer; NOT same as ACR/PCR |
| **MTP** | Medical Treatment of Prisoner | Form documenting medical treatment; "Reserve" function in FORMS app |
| **HHC** | NYC Health and Hospitals Corporation | Fiscal Affairs/General Accounting handles billing for non-HHC hospitals |
| **NP** | Nurse Practitioner | At BCS; administers medication given by doctor for prisoner to take |
| **ESU** | Emergency Service Unit | Supervisors may consider ESU for extraordinary hospital transport circumstances |
| **IAB** | Internal Affairs Bureau | Notified via IAB Command Center for prisoner escape/attempt |
| **CJB** | Criminal Justice Bureau | C.O. receives copy of unusual occurrence report |

---

## Critical Numbers

| Number | What It Represents | Source |
|--------|-------------------|--------|
| **$1.80** | Maximum cost per meal per prisoner | P.G. 210-06 |
| **15 minutes** | Maximum duration for parent/guardian visit (prisoner age 18-21) | P.G. 210-01 |
| **30 minutes** | Inspection interval for prisoners in holding pens | P.G. 210-08 |
| **1 hour** | Follow-up notification deadline if other agency does not provide guard relief | P.G. 210-03 |
| **3 hours** | Maximum detention time in holding pen (standard; extension requires approval) | P.G. 210-08 |
| **4 hours** | Minimum detention before parent/guardian visit is permitted (prisoner age 18-21) | P.G. 210-01 |
| **6 days** | Notification threshold — notify borough court section if bedside arraignment delayed beyond this | P.G. 210-05 |
| **2 prisoners** | Threshold — transport chains MUST be used for more than this number | P.G. 210-01 |
| **9 prisoners** | Threshold — additional escort required for more than this number on transport chains | P.G. 210-01 |
| **2 MOS** | Minimum staffing for MLD fingerprinting of hospitalized prisoners | P.G. 210-05 |
| **3 meal periods** | 0500-0600, 1200-1300, 1800-1900 | P.G. 210-06 |
| **15th of month** | Deadline for prisoner meal expense report submission | P.G. 210-06 |
| **September 30th** | Due date for annual Prisoner Security Survey report | P.G. 210-01 |
| **Last week of September** | Timing for annual Prisoner Security Survey | P.G. 210-01 |
| **7** | Number of high risk prisoner factors | P.G. 210-01 |
| **6** | Items removed from prisoners in holding pens (belts, neckties, shoelaces, drawstrings, jackets, overcoats) | P.G. 210-08 |
| **5** | Required elements on OLBS Arrest Worksheet for voided arrests | P.G. 210-13 |
| **10** | Number of agencies classified as "other police agencies" | P.G. 210-03 |
| **4 hours** | Deadline for EO to respond to DOC prison ward to take custody of released prisoner | P.G. 210-04 |
| **3** | Copies of typed letterhead (49) to Chief of Dept for adult prisoner unusual occurrence | P.G. 210-07 |
| **2** | Copies of typed letterhead (49) to Chief of Dept for child in custody DOA | P.G. 210-07 |
| **1** | Copy of typed letterhead (49) to Chief of Dept for child in custody (not DOA) | P.G. 210-07 |
| **1 tour** | Maximum time Pct of Hospitalization may guard a Housing/Transit prisoner if A/O has other arrests | P.G. 210-02 |
| **Wednesday** | Day of weekly telephone message to BCS when guard removed from hospitalized prisoner | P.G. 210-02 |
| **3 notifications** | Times PS and Communications Section notified when prisoner ingests narcotics/dangerous substance | P.G. 210-04 |

questions.text: What is the maximum amount that may be spent per meal per prisoner?
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 210-06, the maximum cost is **$1.80 per meal per prisoner**. The three meal periods are 0500-0600, 1200-1300, and 1800-1900. No hot liquids (coffee, tea, hot chocolate) are permitted.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-06, the maximum cost is **$1.80 per meal per prisoner**. The three meal periods are 0500-0600, 1200-1300, and 1800-1900. No hot liquids (coffee, tea, hot chocolate) are permitted.

questions.text: How often must a designated MOS inspect prisoners in a holding pen?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 210-08, a designated MOS must inspect prisoners every **30 minutes**. In addition, a supervisor must personally and periodically inspect prisoners, recording their condition (sleeping, alert) and position (sitting, lying down).
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-08, a designated MOS must inspect prisoners every **30 minutes**. In addition, a supervisor must personally and periodically inspect prisoners, recording their condition (sleeping, alert) and position (sitting, lying down).

questions.text: What is the standard maximum detention time for a prisoner in a holding pen?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 210-08, the standard maximum detention in a holding pen is **3 hours**. Extensions require desk officer or detective squad supervisor approval, and the extension must be recorded in the Command Log.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-08, the standard maximum detention in a holding pen is **3 hours**. Extensions require desk officer or detective squad supervisor approval, and the extension must be recorded in the Command Log.

questions.text: When transporting prisoners, at what number must transport chains be used?
options: C) 3 or more prisoners — only if high risk
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 210-01, transport chains MUST be used when transporting **more than 2 prisoners**. Transport chain cuffs must be double locked. Two prisoners may be individually rear cuffed; three or more require transport chains.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-01, transport chains MUST be used when transporting **more than 2 prisoners**. Transport chain cuffs must be double locked. Two prisoners may be individually rear cuffed; three or more require transport chains.

questions.text: Under what TWO circumstances MUST leg restraints be used on a prisoner?
options: A) When the prisoner is combative and when transporting to court
B) When the prisoner refused fingerprinting and for ALL hospital transports
C) When the prisoner is high risk and when transporting more than 2 prisoners
D) When the prisoner threatened violence and when the prisoner has a weapons history
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 210-01, leg restraints MUST be used when (1) the prisoner **refused fingerprinting** and (2) for **ALL hospital transports**. Leg restraints may NOT be used for punitive purposes and do NOT replace rear handcuffing.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-01, leg restraints MUST be used when (1) the prisoner **refused fingerprinting** and (2) for **ALL hospital transports**. Leg restraints may NOT be used for punitive purposes and do NOT replace rear handcuffing.

questions.text: Where must attorney interviews with prisoners be conducted at the precinct?
options: D) Any private room designated by the desk officer
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 210-01, attorney interviews at the precinct must be conducted in the **muster room ONLY**. At borough court, the interview is conducted at a location designated by the supervisor. The attorney visit must be documented on the Arrest Report Supplement (PD244-157).
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-01, attorney interviews at the precinct must be conducted in the **muster room ONLY**. At borough court, the interview is conducted at a location designated by the supervisor. The attorney visit must be documented on the Arrest Report Supplement (PD244-157).

questions.text: A prisoner is 19 years old and has been detained for 5 hours. The prisoner's parent requests a visit. What are the rules for this visit?
options: A) The visit is not permitted — parent visits are only for juveniles under 18
B) Maximum 15 minutes, in the muster room, in the presence of the desk officer or BCS supervisor
C) Maximum 30 minutes, in any designated area, no supervision required
D) Maximum 15 minutes, in the cellblock, in the presence of the arresting officer
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 210-01, parent/guardian visits are permitted for prisoners aged **18-21** who have been detained **more than 4 hours**. The visit is limited to **15 minutes**, must take place in the **muster room**, and must be in the presence of the **desk officer or BCS supervisor**. This prisoner (age 19, detained 5 hours) meets all criteria.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-01, parent/guardian visits are permitted for prisoners aged **18-21** who have been detained **more than 4 hours**. The visit is limited to **15 minutes**, must take place in the **muster room**, and must be in the presence of the **desk officer or BCS supervisor**. This prisoner (age 19, detained 5 hours) meets all criteria.

questions.text: A pregnant prisoner is brought into the command. What is the initial restraint method, and who determines if a less restrictive method is appropriate?
options: A) Front cuff initially; the desk officer determines if less restrictive method is needed
B) Rear cuff initially; the immediate supervisor (sergeant) determines if less restrictive method is appropriate
C) No restraints initially; the commanding officer determines restraint level
D) Rear cuff initially; the arresting officer determines if less restrictive method is needed
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 210-01, pregnant prisoners are initially **rear cuffed**, absent exceptional circumstances. The **immediate supervisor** (sergeant) determines if a less restrictive method is appropriate (front cuff, or one hand cuffed to a stationary post) based on factors including safety, escape likelihood, crime circumstances, demeanor, late-stage pregnancy/fall risk, and labor.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-01, pregnant prisoners are initially **rear cuffed**, absent exceptional circumstances. The **immediate supervisor** (sergeant) determines if a less restrictive method is appropriate (front cuff, or one hand cuffed to a stationary post) based on factors including safety, escape likelihood, crime circumstances, demeanor, late-stage pregnancy/fall risk, and labor.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the seven high risk prisoner factors?
options: D) History of escape or attempted escape
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 210-01, the 7 high risk factors are: (1) seriousness of offense, (2) unwillingness to identify self, (3) forcible resistance, (4) threats of violence or escape, (5) history of violence, (6) weapons possession, and (7) escape/attempted escape history. "Prior criminal record length" is NOT one of the factors.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-01, the 7 high risk factors are: (1) seriousness of offense, (2) unwillingness to identify self, (3) forcible resistance, (4) threats of violence or escape, (5) history of violence, (6) weapons possession, and (7) escape/attempted escape history. "Prior criminal record length" is NOT one of the factors.

questions.text: An arresting officer wants to void an arrest. With whom must the officer confer to obtain consent?
options: C) The patrol supervisor; if unavailable, the desk officer or BCS supervisor
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 210-13, the arresting officer must confer with the **patrol supervisor** for consent to release. If the patrol supervisor is unavailable, the officer confers with the **desk officer or BCS supervisor**. The authority to release rests solely with the Police Department.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-13, the arresting officer must confer with the **patrol supervisor** for consent to release. If the patrol supervisor is unavailable, the officer confers with the **desk officer or BCS supervisor**. The authority to release rests solely with the Police Department.

questions.text: An ADA issues a Decline Prosecution Letter. Before releasing the prisoner, the desk officer conducts a warrant check and discovers an active warrant. What is the correct action?
options: A) Release the prisoner on the decline prosecution and issue a new summons for the warrant
B) Hold the prisoner on both the original charges and the warrant
C) Process the prisoner solely on the warrant authority, NOT on the original charges
D) Contact the ADA to request prosecution on the original charges
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 210-16, if a warrant check reveals an active warrant during a decline prosecution release, the prisoner is processed **solely on the warrant authority** — NOT on the original charges. The ADA has already declined prosecution on the original charges.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-16, if a warrant check reveals an active warrant during a decline prosecution release, the prisoner is processed **solely on the warrant authority** — NOT on the original charges. The ADA has already declined prosecution on the original charges.

questions.text: How many MOS must be present when using the Mobile Livescan Device (MLD) to fingerprint a hospitalized prisoner?
options: D) The number depends on the prisoner's risk level
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 210-05, at least **2 MOS** must be present during MLD fingerprinting of a hospitalized prisoner. The MLD is prohibited for any purpose other than fingerprinting hospitalized prisoners. If bedside arraignment is delayed more than 6 days, the borough court section must be notified.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-05, at least **2 MOS** must be present during MLD fingerprinting of a hospitalized prisoner. The MLD is prohibited for any purpose other than fingerprinting hospitalized prisoners. If bedside arraignment is delayed more than 6 days, the borough court section must be notified.

questions.text: When is the annual Prisoner Security Survey conducted, and when is the report due?
options: A) First week of January; report due January 7th
B) Last week of September; report due September 30th
C) Last week of December; report due December 31st
D) First week of June; report due June 7th
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 210-01, the Prisoner Security Survey is conducted annually during the **last week of September**, with the report due by **September 30th**.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-01, the Prisoner Security Survey is conducted annually during the **last week of September**, with the report due by **September 30th**.

questions.text: Which of the following prisoners may be placed in a holding pen?
options: A) An intoxicated prisoner who is cooperative
B) An emotionally disturbed prisoner under observation
C) A prisoner with a minor laceration who has been treated and cleared by EMS
D) An ill prisoner awaiting transport to the hospital
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 210-08, ill, intoxicated, and emotionally disturbed prisoners are NOT permitted in holding pens — they must be removed to a hospital under P.G. 210-04. A prisoner who has been **treated and medically cleared** by EMS may be placed in a holding pen, as they are no longer in the ill/injured category requiring hospital treatment.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-08, ill, intoxicated, and emotionally disturbed prisoners are NOT permitted in holding pens — they must be removed to a hospital under P.G. 210-04. A prisoner who has been **treated and medically cleared** by EMS may be placed in a holding pen, as they are no longer in the ill/injured category requiring hospital treatment.

questions.text: A voided arrest was processed and the prisoner was fingerprinted. Who is responsible for faxing DCJS to seal the fingerprint record?
questions.answerFull: D) Per P.G. 210-13, when a voided arrest involves a fingerprinted prisoner, the **BCS intake supervisor** faxes DCJS to seal the record. This step is critical to ensure that an invalid arrest does not create a permanent fingerprint record.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-13, when a voided arrest involves a fingerprinted prisoner, the **BCS intake supervisor** faxes DCJS to seal the record. This step is critical to ensure that an invalid arrest does not create a permanent fingerprint record.

questions.text: A hospitalized prisoner's doctor requests that the guarding officer remove the prisoner's handcuffs. What is the correct procedure?
options: A) Remove the cuffs immediately as directed by the doctor
B) Explain the circumstances of the arrest; if the doctor insists, request the Patrol Supervisor to respond — do NOT remove without the PS present and authorizing
C) Refuse the request and document it in the Activity Log
D) Contact the desk officer for permission to remove the cuffs
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 210-04, if a doctor requests cuffs be removed, the guarding officer explains the circumstances of arrest. If the doctor still wants them off, the officer requests the **Patrol Supervisor** to respond. Cuffs are NOT removed without the PS on scene and authorizing. Note: this is different from a doctor asking the officer to **leave the room**, which does NOT require the PS.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-04, if a doctor requests cuffs be removed, the guarding officer explains the circumstances of arrest. If the doctor still wants them off, the officer requests the **Patrol Supervisor** to respond. Cuffs are NOT removed without the PS on scene and authorizing. Note: this is different from a doctor asking the officer to **leave the room**, which does NOT require the PS.

questions.text: A prisoner ingests narcotics during arrest. How many times must the Patrol Supervisor and Communications Section be notified?
options: A) Once — when the ingestion is observed
B) Twice — when observed and when arriving at the hospital
C) Three times — when observed, when transport initiated, and when arrived at hospital
D) Four times — when observed, when transport initiated, when arrived, and when treated
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 210-04, the Patrol Supervisor AND Communications Section are notified **three times**: (1) upon observing or suspecting ingestion, (2) when transport to hospital is initiated, and (3) when 84 (arrived) at hospital. The prisoner goes DIRECTLY to the nearest hospital — never to the stationhouse first.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-04, the Patrol Supervisor AND Communications Section are notified **three times**: (1) upon observing or suspecting ingestion, (2) when transport to hospital is initiated, and (3) when 84 (arrived) at hospital. The prisoner goes DIRECTLY to the nearest hospital — never to the stationhouse first.

questions.text: In P.G. 210-02, a prisoner's lawyer requests to interview the prisoner at the hospital. Under the Patrol Guide, the lawyer is classified as:
options: A) An Interviewer — same as at the precinct under 210-01
B) A Visitor — "if requested" by the prisoner
C) Not permitted to visit hospitalized prisoners
D) An Interviewer only if approved by the Commanding Officer
questions.answerFull: B) Under P.G. 210-02, a lawyer drops from the "Interviewer" list (where they appear in 210-01 at the precinct) to the **"Visitor"** list at the hospital. The lawyer may visit "if requested" by the prisoner. This is one of the key differences between the 210-01 and 210-02 interviewer/visitor lists.
questions.explanation: Under P.G. 210-02, a lawyer drops from the "Interviewer" list (where they appear in 210-01 at the precinct) to the **"Visitor"** list at the hospital. The lawyer may visit "if requested" by the prisoner. This is one of the key differences between the 210-01 and 210-02 interviewer/visitor lists.

questions.text: A prisoner requires psychiatric admission. Generally, male prisoners are transported to which facility, and by what means?
options: A) Elmhurst General by FDNY EMS
C) Bellevue by private ambulance contract
D) Kings County Hospital by RMP
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 210-04, male prisoners requiring psychiatric admission are generally transported to **Bellevue** (women to Elmhurst General). Transport is by hospital via **private contract** — NOT FDNY EMS. The escorting officer rides in the private ambulance with the prisoner.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-04, male prisoners requiring psychiatric admission are generally transported to **Bellevue** (women to Elmhurst General). Transport is by hospital via **private contract** — NOT FDNY EMS. The escorting officer rides in the private ambulance with the prisoner.

questions.text: How many copies of the typed letterhead (49) report does the Commanding Officer forward to the Chief of Department for an unusual occurrence involving an adult prisoner?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 210-07, the number of copies sent to the Chief of Department varies: **3 for an adult prisoner**, 2 for a child in custody who is DOA, and 1 for a child in custody (not DOA). One additional copy goes to each: Chief of Patrol, Chief of IAB, Chief of Training, C.O. CJB, C.O. Performance Monitoring Unit, each intermediate command, and C.O. of UMOS if different.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-07, the number of copies sent to the Chief of Department varies: **3 for an adult prisoner**, 2 for a child in custody who is DOA, and 1 for a child in custody (not DOA). One additional copy goes to each: Chief of Patrol, Chief of IAB, Chief of Training, C.O. CJB, C.O. Performance Monitoring Unit, each intermediate command, and C.O. of UMOS if different.

questions.text: A family member brings prescription medication to the precinct for a prisoner who has already been transferred to another facility. What documentation is required?
options: A) Single Command Log entry at the receiving facility
B) Dual Command Log entries — both the notifying supervisor and the receiving D.O. make entries
D) Activity Log entry by the desk officer
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 210-04, when a prisoner is not at the facility where family brought medication, the supervisor contacts the D.O. at the facility where the prisoner is located. **Both** the notifying supervisor AND the receiving D.O. make Command Log entries, including time of notification, prisoner's name, and name of supervisor receiving/making notification.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-04, when a prisoner is not at the facility where family brought medication, the supervisor contacts the D.O. at the facility where the prisoner is located. **Both** the notifying supervisor AND the receiving D.O. make Command Log entries, including time of notification, prisoner's name, and name of supervisor receiving/making notification.

questions.text: Who decides whether to remove handcuffs entirely from a hospitalized prisoner when restraint is deemed "impractical or inexpedient" (e.g., prisoner is comatose)?
options: D) The Desk Officer, Precinct of Hospitalization
questions.answerFull: D) Per P.G. 210-02, the **Desk Officer, Precinct of Hospitalization** decides whether cuffs are removed when deemed "impractical or inexpedient." The Patrol Supervisor confers with the doctor and makes a recommendation, but the D.O. makes the final decision. Do not confuse with pregnant prisoner restraint (where the immediate supervisor decides).
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 210-02, the **Desk Officer, Precinct of Hospitalization** decides whether cuffs are removed when deemed "impractical or inexpedient." The Patrol Supervisor confers with the doctor and makes a recommendation, but the D.O. makes the final decision. Do not confuse with pregnant prisoner restraint (where the immediate supervisor decides).

sergeantFocus.text: The desk officer is the central authority for prisoner management at the command level. Sergeants must ensure desk officers are actively managing cellblock assignments, custody records, and attendant assignments — not delegating these responsibilities.

Before ANY prisoner is removed from the command, the desk officer must **step from behind the desk** and **personally check** that prisoners are properly secured. This is a physical, hands-on duty — not a visual check from behind the desk.

The **immediate supervisor** (sergeant) makes the determination on whether to use less restrictive restraints for pregnant prisoners. This is a direct supervisory decision — not a desk officer or commanding officer decision. Document the assessment and rationale.

The desk officer must actively track whether the arresting agency has provided relief guards. If no relief arrives within **1 hour**, a follow-up notification is required. Do not allow NYPD resources to be used as indefinite guard replacements for other agencies.

The patrol supervisor must respond to ALL unusual occurrences involving prisoners. Document the incident thoroughly in the Command Log, prepare required reports, and notify the commanding officer for serious incidents (death, escape, serious injury).

Sergeants have a dual responsibility: (1) ensure the designated MOS is conducting 30-minute inspections, and (2) personally and periodically inspect prisoners, recording their condition (sleeping, alert) and physical position (sitting, lying down). This documentation can be critical in liability situations.

Menstrual hygiene products must be provided upon request — do not wait for the prisoner to ask repeatedly. A female MOS must be assigned to escort the prisoner to the restroom. Failure to provide these basic accommodations is a supervisory failure.

When a prisoner requires medical treatment, the patrol supervisor must be notified. The sergeant should verify that the desk officer has properly documented the condition, arranged transport, and assigned a guard. Prisoners with medical conditions cannot simply be held in the cellblock. The supervisor must also evaluate the need for additional personnel, ESU, and ensure leg restraints are applied for hospital transport.

If a hospitalized prisoner is discharged before arraignment, the guarding member must obtain a signed medical release and escort the prisoner to court in rear cuffs. The sergeant should verify this process is followed — prisoners should not be released from the hospital without proper documentation and escort.

The desk officer has authority to authorize additional meals beyond the standard three periods. This includes situations where a prisoner missed a scheduled meal due to processing activities. Sergeants should ensure meal requests are handled promptly — denying food to a prisoner who missed a meal period is not acceptable.

For unusual occurrences, the CO/Duty Captain must personally investigate and prepare typed letterhead reports. The distribution is extensive — 3 copies DIRECT to Chief of Dept, plus 7 additional individual copies. For escapes, IAB must be notified in addition to Operations Unit.

The patrol supervisor is the first-line authority for consent to release a prisoner. This conferral is mandatory — the arresting officer cannot unilaterally void an arrest or release a prisoner. If the patrol supervisor is unavailable, the desk officer or BCS supervisor serves as the alternate authority.

When a voided arrest involves a fingerprinted prisoner, the BCS intake supervisor must fax DCJS to seal the record. Sergeants should verify this step is completed — failure to seal creates a permanent record from an invalid arrest.

When there is a disagreement between the desk officer or BCS supervisor and an arresting officer from another agency regarding prisoner release, the matter is escalated to the precinct commander or duty captain. Sergeants should not attempt to resolve inter-agency disputes at their level — escalate promptly.

The BCS Supervisor is the key decision-maker for Special Category designation, working in conjunction with EMS. The sergeant must ensure all 8 handling steps are completed, especially the MTP preparation and the "Recommend Prisoner Be Separated" checkbox.

The Patrol Supervisor ensures the A/O-E/O accompanies the arrestee during transport (step 8). The desk officer is responsible for arranging both transports and conferring with BCS. The van operator must be **qualified and trained** in wheelchair lift operation.

chapters.title: Court & Agency Appearances
chapters.readme:
# Section 211 — Court & Agency Appearances

## Source

- **Patrol Guide Section:** 211
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide2.pdf
- **Priority:** Medium

## Overview

This chapter covers the procedures governing member appearances at courts, governmental agencies, and private organizations, including the systems used to monitor and control those appearances, subpoena processing, records requests, and conferral with Department attorneys.

---

## Learning Objectives

After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

1. Explain the role of the CPR System in monitoring court and agency appearances
2. Describe the roll call clerk's responsibilities for accessing the CPR System (3 times daily)
3. Identify valid reasons for cancelling a court appearance and the explicit concurrence requirement
4. Apply the 4th Platoon Rule for members with next-day court appearances
5. Describe the criminal and civil court telephone alert procedures, including the 1300 hours cutoff
6. Explain the procedure for preventing court appearances on scheduled days off
7. Identify when and how to notify the Police Commissioner of governmental/private organization appearances
8. Distinguish between voluntary and subpoenaed appearances and their approval requirements
9. Describe TVB appearance procedures and the training sergeant's monthly review process
10. Explain FOIL requirements including the 5 business day response deadline
11. Distinguish the validity of subpoenas from NYC courts, NYS courts, Federal courts, and out-of-state courts
12. Apply the Brady obligation to ADA/ACC document requests
13. Describe the procedure for conferring with a Department attorney and the contrary advice rule

---

## Procedures

| Procedure | Title | Study Guide File | Status |
|-----------|-------|-----------------|--------|
| P.G. 211-05 | Processing Notifications to Appear at Court and Other Government Agencies |  | completed |
| P.G. 211-06 | Criminal/Civil Court Telephone Alert System |  | completed |
| P.G. 211-07 | Prevention of Court Appearance on Scheduled Day Off |  | completed |
| P.G. 211-09 | Appearances Before Governmental Agencies or Private Organizations |  | completed |
| P.G. 211-11 | Traffic Violations Bureau Appearances / Computerized Time Slips |  | completed |
| P.G. 211-15 | Processing Subpoenas for Police Department Records and Testimony |  | completed |
| P.G. 211-16 | Subpoenas Issued by Courts Outside New York City |  | completed |
| P.G. 211-17 | Processing Legal Bureau Requests Including FOIL |  | completed |
| P.G. 211-18 | Processing Requests from ADAs and Assistant Corporation Counsels |  | completed |
| P.G. 211-20 | Conferral with Department Attorney |  | completed |

---

## Additional Resources

| File | Contents |
|------|----------|
|  | Definitions of critical terms, acronyms, and frequently tested numbers |
|  | 15 multiple-choice questions with hidden answers covering all procedures |

---

## Sergeant Focus Areas

- Tracking and approving subordinates' court appearance schedules via CPR System
- Ensuring timely notification and proper documentation for all court-related absences
- Roll call clerk supervision (3 daily CPR checks)
- Mediating court appearance conflicts through ACU
- Training sergeant monthly TVB dismissal review (10 members)
- Ensuring compliance with the explicit concurrence cancellation rule
- Following Department attorney advice over contrary supervisor/ADA guidance
- FOIL response compliance within 5 business days
- Brady obligation awareness for document production
chapters.keyTerms:
# Key Terms — Section 211: Court & Agency Appearances

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **CPR System** | Centralized Personnel Resource System — monitors and controls all court and agency appearances |
| **Short Date Notification** | Notification to appear the next day (or Friday for Monday) — time-sensitive, triggers email notifications to member, roll call, operations coordinator, and integrity control officer |
| **Telephone Alert** | System to prevent unnecessary court appearances; member assigned to 2nd platoon, no meal before 1300 hours; if not called by 1300, alert NOT activated |
| **Appearance Control Unit (ACU)** | Processes all court appearance notifications, mediates conflicts between appearances, monitors member attendance |
| **Officer Time Slip** | Computerized slip from TVB showing hearing disposition; replaces Court Attendance Record (PD468-141) for TVB appearances |
| **FOIL** | Freedom of Information Law — NYS Public Officers Law Sections 84-89; all government records presumed available for public inspection; 5 business day response required |
| **Records Access Officer** | Supervisor of the FOIL Unit within the Legal Bureau's Subpoena Litigation Unit |
| **Command Liaison Officer** | Every commanding officer — designated official liaison for tracking records requests from the Subpoena Litigation Unit |
| **Subpoena Litigation Unit** | Legal Bureau sub-unit located at One Police Plaza, Room 110C; processes all subpoenas and FOIL requests |
| **4th Platoon Rule** | Members on 4th platoon (1800x0200) with next-day court are reassigned to 3rd platoon (1600x2400); members not notified in advance are excused at 2400 (no OT for 2400-0200) |
| **5 Business Days** | (1) Time before testimony to notify Police Commissioner of governmental/private organization appearances; (2) FOIL response deadline |
| **5 Days Maximum** | Temporary DA assignment limit without CO endorsement to Chief of Department |
| **1300 Hours** | Telephone alert cutoff — if member on alert is not called to court by 1300, the alert is NOT activated that day |
| **25 Cents Per Page** | FOIL photocopy fee |
| **30 Days** | Time limit to appeal a FOIL denial to the Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters |
| **0700-2300 Mon-Fri** | Hours to contact a Department attorney for legal advice; outside these hours, contact CO/duty captain |
| **Brady Obligation** | Prosecution's duty to disclose exculpatory evidence; NYPD possession of records = prosecutor possession; failure to produce = potential conviction reversal |
| **Court Attendance Record (PD468-141)** | Standard court attendance form — notably NOT used for TVB appearances (Officer Time Slip used instead) |
| **Fee Receipt (PD122-017)** | Issued per P.G. 212-79 for out-of-NYC subpoenas |
| **Subpoena Record Book** | Command-level log for tracking all subpoenas and DA/Corporation Counsel requests |
| **Typed Letterhead** | Required format for written notifications to the Police Commissioner, reports to Subpoena Litigation Unit, and Federal criminal subpoena reports |

---

## Critical Numbers at a Glance

| Number | Context |
|--------|---------|
| **3** | Times daily roll call clerk must access CPR System |
| **5 business days** | FOIL response deadline AND PC notification deadline for governmental appearances |
| **5 days** | Maximum temporary DA assignment without CO endorsement |
| **6-30 days** | DA assignment extension requiring CO endorsement to Chief of Department |
| **10** | Members selected monthly by Training Sergeant for TVB dismissal review |
| **25 cents** | FOIL photocopy fee per page |
| **30 days** | FOIL denial appeal period |
| **1300 hours** | Telephone alert cutoff time |
| **0700-2300** | Department attorney contact hours (Mon-Fri) |
| **Triplicate** | Copies required for PC notification of governmental appearances |

---

**Reference: P.G. 211-05, 211-06, 211-07, 211-09, 211-11, 211-15, 211-16, 211-17, 211-18, 211-20**

questions.text: According to P.G. 211-05, how many times daily must the roll call clerk access the CPR System to check for court appearance notifications?
options: A) Once, at the beginning of the business day
B) Twice — beginning and end of the business day
C) Three times — beginning, midpoint, and 30 minutes before close
D) Four times — at regular intervals throughout the day
questions.answerFull: C) The roll call clerk must access the CPR System 3 times daily: (1) at the beginning of the business day, (2) at the midpoint of roll call office hours, and (3) 30 minutes before the close of roll call office hours.
questions.explanation: The roll call clerk must access the CPR System 3 times daily: (1) at the beginning of the business day, (2) at the midpoint of roll call office hours, and (3) 30 minutes before the close of roll call office hours.

questions.text: Which of the following is a valid reason to cancel a court appearance under P.G. 211-05?
options: A) Member has a personal appointment
B) Member is sick, after conferral with the district surgeon
C) Member left a voicemail with the DA's office requesting cancellation
D) Member's supervisor determines the appearance is unnecessary
questions.answerFull: B) Cancellation is accepted ONLY if the member is: sick (after conferral with district surgeon), on annual vacation selection, military leave, bereavement leave, or terminal leave. A voicemail is never sufficient — the requesting agency must explicitly concur.
questions.explanation: Cancellation is accepted ONLY if the member is: sick (after conferral with district surgeon), on annual vacation selection, military leave, bereavement leave, or terminal leave. A voicemail is never sufficient — the requesting agency must explicitly concur.

questions.text: A "short date notification" under P.G. 211-05 is defined as:
options: A) A notification for a member to appear within 72 hours
B) A notification for a member to appear the next day, or a notification delivered on Friday for a Monday appearance
C) A notification received less than one week before the appearance date
D) Any notification that bypasses the normal CPR System process
questions.answerFull: B) A short date notification is a notification for a member to appear the next day, or a notification delivered on Friday for a Monday appearance. It is time-sensitive and triggers immediate email notifications to the member, roll call personnel, operations coordinator, and integrity control officer.
questions.explanation: A short date notification is a notification for a member to appear the next day, or a notification delivered on Friday for a Monday appearance. It is time-sensitive and triggers immediate email notifications to the member, roll call personnel, operations coordinator, and integrity control officer.

questions.text: Under the 4th Platoon Rule in P.G. 211-05, a member normally assigned to the 4th platoon (1800x0200) who is subpoenaed to appear in court the next day shall be:
options: A) Excused from the court appearance
B) Placed on overtime for the court appearance
questions.answerFull: C) Members normally assigned to 4th platoon (1800x0200) who are subpoenaed or directed to appear the next day are reassigned to the 3rd platoon (1600x2400). Members not notified in advance who are already on the 4th platoon are excused at 2400 hours, with no overtime authorized for the 2400-0200 period.
questions.explanation: Members normally assigned to 4th platoon (1800x0200) who are subpoenaed or directed to appear the next day are reassigned to the 3rd platoon (1600x2400). Members not notified in advance who are already on the 4th platoon are excused at 2400 hours, with no overtime authorized for the 2400-0200 period.

questions.text: Under the criminal court telephone alert system (P.G. 211-06), a member on alert shall not be given a meal period before what time?
questions.answerFull: C) Under the criminal court telephone alert, NO meal period is authorized before 1300 hours. The member must remain available for a call to court.
questions.explanation: Under the criminal court telephone alert, NO meal period is authorized before 1300 hours. The member must remain available for a call to court.

questions.text: If a member on criminal court telephone alert is not directed to report to court by 1300 hours, what happens?
options: A) The member is placed on standby until end of tour
B) The alert is NOT activated for that day
C) The member is assigned to administrative duties
D) The member is directed to contact ACU for further instructions
questions.answerFull: B) If a member on telephone alert is not directed to court by 1300 hours, the alert is NOT activated for that day. The 1300 hours cutoff is absolute.
questions.explanation: If a member on telephone alert is not directed to court by 1300 hours, the alert is NOT activated for that day. The 1300 hours cutoff is absolute.

questions.text: Under P.G. 211-06, the ACU supervisor must notify the commanding officer IN WRITING when:
options: A) A member requests a schedule change due to court
B) A member fails to appear when called OR appears in court without being called
C) A member's court case is adjourned
D) A member is placed on telephone alert for three consecutive days
questions.answerFull: B) The ACU supervisor must notify the CO in writing when: (a) a member fails to appear when called to court, OR (b) a member appears in court without being called. Both scenarios trigger mandatory written notification.
questions.explanation: The ACU supervisor must notify the CO in writing when: (a) a member fails to appear when called to court, OR (b) a member appears in court without being called. Both scenarios trigger mandatory written notification.

questions.text: When a member intends to testify before a governmental agency about Department operations (P.G. 211-09), written notification must be forwarded to the Police Commissioner no later than:
options: A) 3 business days before the appearance
B) 5 business days before the appearance
C) 7 calendar days before the appearance
D) 10 business days before the appearance
questions.answerFull: B) Written notification must be forwarded no later than 5 business days before the testimony. If unable to meet this deadline, the member must immediately notify the Office of the Police Commissioner or Legal Bureau by telephone.
questions.explanation: Written notification must be forwarded no later than 5 business days before the testimony. If unable to meet this deadline, the member must immediately notify the Office of the Police Commissioner or Legal Bureau by telephone.

questions.text: Under P.G. 211-09, the written request to the Police Commissioner for appearances before governmental agencies is prepared in how many copies?
questions.answerFull: B) The written request is prepared in triplicate: the original goes direct to the Police Commissioner, one copy goes through channels to the Police Commissioner, and one copy goes direct to the Deputy Commissioner, Public Information.
questions.explanation: The written request is prepared in triplicate: the original goes direct to the Police Commissioner, one copy goes through channels to the Police Commissioner, and one copy goes direct to the Deputy Commissioner, Public Information.

questions.text: A member who wishes to voluntarily testify before a private organization about Department policies must obtain:
options: A) Verbal approval from their commanding officer
B) Prior written approval from the Police Commissioner
C) Approval from the Legal Bureau only
D) No prior approval, but must file a report afterward
questions.answerFull: B) Voluntary appearances require PRIOR WRITTEN APPROVAL from the Police Commissioner. This is distinct from subpoenaed appearances, which require notification but not prior approval (since the member is legally compelled to appear).
questions.explanation: Voluntary appearances require PRIOR WRITTEN APPROVAL from the Police Commissioner. This is distinct from subpoenaed appearances, which require notification but not prior approval (since the member is legally compelled to appear).

questions.text: At the Traffic Violations Bureau, which form is NOT used to record officer court attendance?
options: D) Officer's Notes copy of summons
questions.answerFull: C) The Court Attendance Record (PD468-141) is NOT used for TVB appearances. Instead, the computerized Officer Time Slip is used to record TVB hearing attendance and dispositions.
questions.explanation: The Court Attendance Record (PD468-141) is NOT used for TVB appearances. Instead, the computerized Officer Time Slip is used to record TVB hearing attendance and dispositions.

questions.text: Under P.G. 211-11, the training sergeant's monthly review of TVB dispositions involves selecting how many members for potential retraining?
options: A) 5 members with the most dismissals
B) 10 members with the highest percentage of dismissals
C) All members with any dismissals
D) 15 members with the most court appearances
questions.answerFull: B) The training sergeant monthly selects 10 members with the highest percentage of dismissals for failure to establish prima facie case or Not Guilty due to unclear evidence. These members are retrained and/or subject to corrective action.
questions.explanation: The training sergeant monthly selects 10 members with the highest percentage of dismissals for failure to establish prima facie case or Not Guilty due to unclear evidence. These members are retrained and/or subject to corrective action.

questions.text: Under the Freedom of Information Law (FOIL), the Department must respond to a records request within:
questions.answerFull: B) FOIL requires a response within 5 business days. The response must either: (1) make the record available, (2) deny the request in writing, or (3) acknowledge receipt with an approximate date of availability.
questions.explanation: FOIL requires a response within 5 business days. The response must either: (1) make the record available, (2) deny the request in writing, or (3) acknowledge receipt with an approximate date of availability.

questions.text: A subpoena issued by a court in another state (not New York) is:
options: A) Always valid and must be complied with immediately
B) Valid only if served within New York City
C) NOT valid unless presented to and endorsed by a New York State judge
D) Valid only for criminal matters, not civil
questions.answerFull: C) Out-of-state subpoenas (criminal or civil) are NOT valid unless presented to and endorsed by a New York State judge. However, the Department should make every reasonable effort to assist other jurisdictions, with permission obtained through channels.
questions.explanation: Out-of-state subpoenas (criminal or civil) are NOT valid unless presented to and endorsed by a New York State judge. However, the Department should make every reasonable effort to assist other jurisdictions, with permission obtained through channels.

questions.text: Under P.G. 211-20, if a member receives advice from a supervisor or ADA that contradicts the Department attorney's prior advice, the member MUST:
options: A) Follow the supervisor's or ADA's advice as the most recent guidance
B) Use personal judgment to determine the best course of action
C) Follow the Department attorney's advice and inform the other party of the prior conferral
D) Request a meeting between the Department attorney and the supervisor/ADA before taking action
questions.answerFull: C) The member MUST inform the individual giving contrary advice of the prior conferral with the Department attorney. If there is a suggestion to disregard the Department attorney's advice, the member or supervisor MUST notify the Department attorney and MUST FOLLOW THE DEPARTMENT ATTORNEY'S ADVICE. Disregarding Department attorney advice may result in disciplinary action and civil liability.
questions.explanation: The member MUST inform the individual giving contrary advice of the prior conferral with the Department attorney. If there is a suggestion to disregard the Department attorney's advice, the member or supervisor MUST notify the Department attorney and MUST FOLLOW THE DEPARTMENT ATTORNEY'S ADVICE. Disregarding Department attorney advice may result in disciplinary action and civil liability.

sergeantFocus.text: The roll call clerk **shall** access the CPR System **3 times daily**:
The desk officer shall:
The roll call clerk shall:
The ACU supervisor shall notify the CO **in writing** when:
The supervisor determines if conferral with a Department attorney is necessary. This is a judgment call that falls directly on the sergeant.
A Command Log entry is required with the following information:

Sergeants must understand that they cannot override Department attorney advice. If a sergeant believes the attorney's advice is incorrect, the proper course is to notify the Department attorney of the conflicting opinion — not to direct the member to disregard the attorney's guidance. Following this protocol protects both the member and the supervisor from disciplinary action and civil liability.

Sergeants must ensure that civil lawsuit papers are handled promptly — failure to forward the REQUEST FOR LEGAL ASSISTANCE in time can result in a default judgment against the member. The CO must check both IAB Records Unit and Department Advocate's Office before completing Part B. The WHITE copy goes DIRECT to Legal Bureau, not through channels. Follow up after 10 business days.
Supervisors should ensure members understand the mandatory nature of cooperation with the AG's Misconduct Investigative Office. There is no discretion here — full cooperation is required.
When a member's court appearance is scheduled on an RDO:
Sergeants should be aware that the Integrity Control Officer monitors RDO court appearances for patterns that may indicate integrity issues.

Supervisors must ensure members understand that unilateral cancellation attempts do not relieve them of the obligation to appear. Only explicit concurrence from the requesting agency cancels the appearance.

Supervisors must understand that ANY Department document relevant to a case is considered to be in the prosecutor's possession. Failure to produce it is a Brady violation that can overturn a conviction.
For summary arrests, the arresting/assigned officer shall:
Have the member logged into the computer system **IMMEDIATELY**.
This is a direct sergeant responsibility and a likely exam topic.

Codes **5** (Officer No-Show), **6** (No Prima Facie case), **7** (Cannot identify motorist), and **8** (No recollection) are the codes most likely to trigger training sergeant review and corrective action. These reflect officer performance issues.

chapters.title: Command Operations
chapters.readme:
# Section 212 — Command Operations

## Source
- **Patrol Guide Section:** 212
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide2.pdf
- **Priority:** High

## Overview

Section 212 is the **largest section** in the Patrol Guide, covering 56 procedures (212-03 through 212-129) that govern day-to-day operational procedures for command-level activities. This chapter spans tour management, transit and housing operations, special incidents, media relations, firearms licensing, election duties, technology systems, and interaction procedures.

## Learning Objectives

After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

1. Explain tour expiration procedures, Activity Log requirements, and the 4th platoon rule
2. Describe transit evacuation types (reach train vs. rescue train) and power removal criteria
3. Apply proper procedures for critical transit situations and subway power removal
4. Identify rules governing service animals, photography, and radio malfunction on transit
5. Conduct proper inspections of NYCHA facilities and transit stations
6. Handle incidents involving former/retired MOS, diplomats, and off-duty law enforcement
7. Apply media access rules including MOME press card privileges and First Amendment protections
8. Verify pistol licenses and process rifle/shotgun permit surrenders
9. Evaluate and initiate Extreme Risk Protection Orders (ERPOs)
10. Execute election-day duties before, during, and after polls
11. Activate and cancel AMBER Alerts with proper authority
12. Understand facial recognition limitations and VGTOF handling codes
13. Ensure effective communication with persons who are deaf or hard of hearing
14. Manage auxiliary police deployments and coordinator requirements

## Chapter Files

### Operational Procedures

| File | Procedures Covered | Topic |
|------|-------------------|-------|
|  | 212-03, 212-08, 212-10 | Tour expiration, Activity Logs, Interrupted Patrol Log |
|  | 212-04, 212-11 | Crime scene procedures, investigative encounters (Stop, Question & Frisk), three levels, RTKBC, Stop Report review |
|  | 212-16, 212-17, 212-19, 212-21, 212-22, 212-23, 212-24 | Train evacuations, critical situations, animals, photography, radio malfunction, station inspection, power removal |
|  | 212-26, 212-28, 212-60 | NYCHA facility inspection, disabled elevators, interior patrol |
|  | 212-36, 212-43, 212-45, 212-47, 212-54, 212-55, 212-56 | Former/retired MOS, vacate orders, asylum, counterfeit money, jumpers, fireworks injuries, diplomatic incidents |

|  | 212-44, 212-46, 212-84, 212-85, 212-118, 212-128 | Firearms dealers, unlawful possession, pistol license verification, rifle/shotgun permits, ERPO |
|  | 212-86, 212-87, 212-88 | Before polls, during polls, after polls |
|  | 212-12, 212-78, 212-99, 212-108, 212-121, 212-123, 212-127, 212-129 | Intelligence reporting, RTCC, AMBER Alert, VGTOF, unauthorized radio, BWC, gunshot detection, facial recognition |
|  | 212-06, 212-41, 212-48, 212-50, 212-51, 212-52, 212-65, 212-66, 212-69, 212-70, 212-73, 212-74, 212-81, 212-82, 212-79, 212-91, 212-100 | Reserve surgeon, alarms, labor disputes, bicycle patrol, immigration policy, civilian commendations, subpoena fees, auxiliary police |
|  | 212-104 | Deaf/hard of hearing communication, ADA compliance |

### Study Materials

| File | Contents |
|------|----------|
|  | 60+ critical terms, numbers, handling codes, radio codes, and mnemonics |
|  | 35 multiple-choice questions with detailed answer explanations |

**Note:** This is the largest section (56 procedures). Study sessions are organized into 10 logical sub-groups above.

## Sergeant Focus Areas

- Daily command operations and supervision (tour expiration, Activity Logs, Interrupted Patrol Log)
- Transit emergency management (evacuations, power removal, critical situations)
- Housing facility inspections and interior patrols
- Media relations and press access rules
- Firearms licensing verification and ERPO evaluation
- Election-day duties and poll security
- Technology system limitations (facial recognition, AMBER Alert authority)
- Auxiliary police coordinator requirements

chapters.keyTerms:
# Section 212 — Key Terms and Numbers

## Critical Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **CPR System** | Centralized Personnel Resource — attendance and roll call tracking system |
| **Digital Activity Log** | Primary activity recording tool on Department smartphone; required for all uniformed MOS below captain rank |
| **Interrupted Patrol Log** | Desk officer log tracking time members spend in the stationhouse during tour |
| **Rosario Material** | Documents, notes, or logs potentially discoverable in criminal proceedings |
| **Reach Train** | Train brought in front of or behind a stalled train so passengers can walk through |
| **Rescue Train** | Train brought alongside for cross-platform transfer via emergency evacuation devices |
| **RTOCC** | Rapid Transit Operations Command Center — primary transit coordination hub |
| **Emergency Alarm Box** | Located 400-600 feet apart under blue lights; removes power on ALL tracks in vicinity |
