| **MOME** | Mayor's Office of Media and Entertainment — issues press credentials |
| **DCPI** | Deputy Commissioner, Public Information — notified for all press incidents (24/7) |
| **ERPO** | Extreme Risk Protection Order — court order prohibiting firearm purchase/possession |
| **VGTOF** | Violent Gang and Terrorist Organization File within NCIC |
| **RTCC-FIS** | Real Time Crime Center — Facial Identification Section |
| **Probe Image** | Image of unidentified person submitted for facial recognition comparison |
| **Photo Repository** | Approved facial recognition database containing ONLY arrest and parole photographs |
| **Possible Match Candidate** | Investigative lead ONLY — does NOT establish probable cause |
| **AMBER Alert** | Child abduction alert for victims 17 or under in imminent danger of serious harm/death |
| **ALPS** | Automated License and Permit System — electronic pistol license verification |
| **Public Counter** | Scanner machine counter that MUST read zero before polls open |
| **Protective Counter** | Scanner counter that is NOT expected to be zero — do not confuse with public counter |
| **TRS** | Telecommunications Relay Service — dial 711 for deaf/hard of hearing communication |
| **4th Platoon Rule** | Members on 1800x0200 subpoenaed for next-day court reassigned to 3rd platoon (1600x2400) |

## Investigative Encounter Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Level 1 Encounter** | Request for Information — based on objective credible reason; no detention, no search request |
| **Level 2 Encounter** | Common Law Right of Inquiry — based on founded suspicion; may ask accusatory questions and request consent to search |
| **Level 3 Encounter** | Terry Stop — based on reasonable suspicion of felony or PL misdemeanor; may detain, use force, frisk |
| **DeBour** | NYS Court of Appeals case articulating the 4-level encounter framework (request for info, CLRI, forcible stop, arrest) |
| **Terry v. Ohio** | Federal Supreme Court case providing authority for Level 3 forcible stop and possible frisk |
| **RTKBC** | Right to Know Business Card — pre-printed card given to persons at Level 2 and 3 encounters |
| **RTKBC-G** | Right to Know Business Card — General — blank version used when regular RTKBCs are exhausted |
| **CLRI** | Common Law Right of Inquiry — Consent Search Report; prepared when consent to search is requested at Level 2 |
| **Stop Report** | Documentation prepared for each Level 3 (Terry) stop; submitted through FORMS; reviewed by supervisor |
| **TACTICS** | Training Attendance Certification Transcript Integration Collection System — records training sessions for investigative encounter compliance |
| **Protective Measures** | Tactics short of a frisk (order hands from pockets, instruct person to put down object) used when officer safety is at risk |
| **Frisk** | Carefully running hand down outside of person's clothing to check for weapons — NOT to locate evidence |

## Crime Scene and Incident Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Police Line Do Not Cross** | White tape with blue letters; establishes secure area for police operations |
| **Crime Scene Do Not Cross** | Yellow tape with black letters; safeguards area where evidence may be present |
| **Crime Scene Evidence Cone** | 5-inch orange cone marked "NYPD" in black; placed next to small evidence items |
| **HARBOR** | Mnemonic for mandatory CSU response: Homicide, Aggravated assault (likely to die), Rape (forcible), Burglary (forced safe/circumvented alarm), Other (CSU assists), Robbery/hijacking (firearm caused injury) |
| **IACC** | Inter-Agency Command Center — separate from police Command Post; established at hazmat/SECTAR incidents |
| **SECTAR** | Significant fire, Explosion, Chemical burn, Toxic Air Release, Radiation hazard — triggers full hazmat response |
| **MDV** | Mobile Decontamination Vehicle — requested through Operations by on-scene coordinator |
| **FRUITS** | Mnemonic for when D.O. notifies Ops AND Borough for fires: Forced entry by FD, Relocation of tenants, Unusual fire, Injury/death, Three alarms+, Suspicious fire |
| **RATCOP** | Mnemonic for info released only after conferral with DCPI AND Chief of Detectives: Reputation, Anticipated testimony, Test results, Credibility, Opinion/speculation, Photos/films/mug shot |

## BWC and Technology Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **BWC** | Body Worn Camera — Dept property; must be personally issued; activate for all police action |
| **Police Action** | Police service, law enforcement, or investigative activity in furtherance of official duties — triggers mandatory BWC activation |
| **GO 15 (A.G. 318-11)** | Rule allowing member to view own BWC video as subject/witness in Dept investigation prior to making statement |
| **Gunshot Detection** | Alert system (Shotspotter) that detects potential gunfire; generates confirmed or unconfirmed alerts |
| **ECMS** | Enterprise Case Management System — NCO opens companion case for confirmed gunshot alerts |

## Critical Numbers

| Number | Significance |
|--------|-------------|
| **1 hour** | Contact desk officer before end of tour (foot patrol needing relief); arrive at poll site before opening |
| **30 minutes** | Dispatch another unit if Transit call not finalized; radio check intervals during malfunction |
| **100 feet** | No-electioneering zone around poll site entrance |
| **400-600 feet** | Spacing of emergency alarm boxes (under blue lights) in subway |
| **2100 hours** | Poll closing time — persons on line may vote, no new entries |
| **72 hours** | HPD deadline to seal vacated premises |
| **60 days** | Rifle/shotgun surrender investigation completion deadline |
| **2 years** | Pistol license validity period; minimum firearm retention under ERPO |
| **8 hours** | Window to cancel AMBER Alert via fax to NYS Police |
| **30 days** | ERPO Unit reviews before expiration for renewal determination |
| **$15** | Subpoena fee — Civil/State |
| **$40** | Subpoena fee — Civil/Federal |
| **$5** | Medical records fee for outside parties |
| **711** | TRS dial code for deaf/hard of hearing relay service |
| **(212) 504-4115** | TTY number for 311 system |
| **0900-1700** | License Division hours (Mon-Fri); Legal Bureau ERPO Unit hours (Mon-Fri) |
| **300 feet** | Minimum outdoor hazmat distance for spills and most fires |
| **1,000 feet** | Minimum outdoor hazmat distance for explosions/suspected devices/military/tanker fires; bomb evacuation distance |
| **20 minutes** | Discontinue bomb search before threatened explosion time |
| **1 hour** | Do not resume bomb search after threatened explosion time |
| **39 months** | BWC video automatic deletion period |
| **PD Code 080** | Investigate Gunshot Detection Alert (Shotspotter, no ballistics) |
| **PD Code 083** | Investigate Shots Fired (no ballistics) |
| **PD Code 088** | Investigate Shot Fired, Ballistics Recovered |
| **PD Code 089** | Investigate Gunshot Detection Alert, Ballistics Recovered |

## Handling Codes (VGTOF)

| Code | Action | Key Detail |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **1** | ARREST | Warrant exists — approach with caution |
| **2** | DETAIN | Reasonable suspicion — active investigation |
| **3** | INVESTIGATION | Do not alert — possible terrorism ties |
| **4** | INFORMATION GATHERING | Do not alert individual |

## Transit Radio Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **10-75T** | Transit patrol by non-Transit member |
| **10-75S** | Station inspection by Transit member |
| **10-75O** | TOMS |
| **10-75M** | Mobile order maintenance |
| **10-90N** | Unnecessary Alarm (DOE facilities, no forced entry) |

## Train Crew Signal

| Signal | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| **LONG-SHORT-LONG-SHORT** | Train crew requesting police assistance |

questions.text: Who is required to use a paper Activity Log (PD112-145) instead of the digital Activity Log?
options: D) Members above the rank of captain
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-08, undercovers use the paper Activity Log (PD112-145) and shall NOT use the digital version.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-08, undercovers use the paper Activity Log (PD112-145) and shall NOT use the digital version.

questions.text: Entries in the Interrupted Patrol Log are made in whose handwriting?
options: A) The member reporting to the stationhouse
B) The desk officer's own handwriting
C) The patrol supervisor on duty
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-10, the desk officer makes all entries in the Interrupted Patrol Log in their own handwriting and maintains the log under direct control.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-10, the desk officer makes all entries in the Interrupted Patrol Log in their own handwriting and maintains the log under direct control.

questions.text: Activity Logs are considered potential what type of material?
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-08, Activity Logs are potential Rosario material and may be reviewed by the Department, outside agencies, DAs, and defense attorneys.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-08, Activity Logs are potential Rosario material and may be reviewed by the Department, outside agencies, DAs, and defense attorneys.

questions.text: Under the 4th platoon rule, members subpoenaed for next-day court are reassigned to which platoon?
options: D) They remain on the 4th platoon
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-03, members on the 4th platoon (1800x0200) who are subpoenaed for next-day court (2nd platoon) are reassigned to the 3rd platoon. If not notified in advance, they are excused at 2400 hours with no OT for the 2400-0200 period.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-03, members on the 4th platoon (1800x0200) who are subpoenaed for next-day court (2nd platoon) are reassigned to the 3rd platoon. If not notified in advance, they are excused at 2400 hours with no OT for the 2400-0200 period.

questions.text: Before evacuating transit passengers to the benchwall or roadbed, what must happen first?
options: A) A rescue train must be positioned
C) The duty captain must approve
D) ESU must arrive on scene
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-16, when evacuating passengers to the benchwall or roadbed, power MUST be removed first. Power is NOT removed for reach trains or rescue trains.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-16, when evacuating passengers to the benchwall or roadbed, power MUST be removed first. Power is NOT removed for reach trains or rescue trains.

questions.text: What distinguishes a "reach train" from a "rescue train"?
options: A) Reach trains use emergency devices; rescue trains do not
B) Reach trains involve walking through the stalled train; rescue trains involve cross-platform transfer via emergency devices
C) Reach trains require power removal; rescue trains do not
D) There is no difference; the terms are interchangeable
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-16, neither requires power removal.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-16, neither requires power removal.

questions.text: The transit radio code 10-75T indicates what?
options: A) Transit station inspection by Transit member
B) Transit patrol by a non-Transit member
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-23, 10-75T is for transit patrol by non-Transit personnel. 10-75S is station inspection by Transit, and 10-75M is mobile order maintenance.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-23, 10-75T is for transit patrol by non-Transit personnel. 10-75S is station inspection by Transit, and 10-75M is mobile order maintenance.

questions.text: Emergency alarm boxes in the subway are spaced how far apart and located where?
options: A) 200-400 feet apart, under red lights
B) 400-600 feet apart, under blue lights
C) 600-800 feet apart, under yellow lights
D) 100-200 feet apart, under green lights
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-24, emergency alarm boxes are located 400-600 feet apart beneath blue lights. Pulling the lever removes power on ALL tracks in the vicinity.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-24, emergency alarm boxes are located 400-600 feet apart beneath blue lights. Pulling the lever removes power on ALL tracks in the vicinity.

questions.text: Can members require proof of disability or documentation for a service animal on public transportation?
options: A) Yes, written documentation is required
B) Yes, but only a verbal confirmation
C) No, but members may inquire if the animal is providing disability assistance
D) No, members may not ask any questions
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-19, members CANNOT require proof of disability or documentation. They may only ask if the animal is providing disability assistance.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-19, members CANNOT require proof of disability or documentation. They may only ask if the animal is providing disability assistance.

questions.text: What photography/recording is permitted on the transit system without a press ID?
options: A) No photography of any kind
B) Camera alone with no ancillary equipment
C) Camera with a tripod only
D) Camera with lights and reflectors
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-21, photography and recording are permitted, but ancillary equipment (lights, reflectors, tripods) may NOT be used without a valid MOME press ID.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-21, photography and recording are permitted, but ancillary equipment (lights, reflectors, tripods) may NOT be used without a valid MOME press ID.

questions.text: MOME Press Card holders are entitled to what access?
options: A) Access to interior crime scenes
B) Access to cross police and fire lines
D) Access to all areas without restriction
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-49, MOME press card holders are entitled to cross police and fire lines. This does NOT include interior crime scenes or security-restricted areas.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-49, MOME press card holders are entitled to cross police and fire lines. This does NOT include interior crime scenes or security-restricted areas.

questions.text: During First Amendment Activity, if NYPD orders persons to leave an area, what happens to MOME press?
options: A) They must leave with everyone else
B) They are arrested if they refuse to leave
C) They are NOT required to leave but should move to a safe location
D) They may stay only with incident commander approval
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-49, during First Amendment Activity, MOME press are not required to leave even if NYPD orders persons to leave — they should be directed to a safe location.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-49, during First Amendment Activity, MOME press are not required to leave even if NYPD orders persons to leave — they should be directed to a safe location.

questions.text: Before polls open, the scanner machine public counter must read what?
options: B) The same as the protective counter
D) The number of registered voters
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-86, ALL scanner machine public counters must read zero before polls open. If not zero, the desk officer/supervisor must be notified immediately and the scanner shall NOT be used. The protective counter will likely NOT be zero — do not confuse the two.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-86, ALL scanner machine public counters must read zero before polls open. If not zero, the desk officer/supervisor must be notified immediately and the scanner shall NOT be used. The protective counter will likely NOT be zero — do not confuse the two.

questions.text: No-electioneering signs must be posted within how many feet of the poll site entrance?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-86, the Board of Elections must post no-electioneering signs within 100 feet of the poll site entrance.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-86, the Board of Elections must post no-electioneering signs within 100 feet of the poll site entrance.

questions.text: Polls close at what time, and what is the rule for persons on line?
options: A) 2000 hours; all persons must leave immediately
B) 2100 hours; persons on line may vote, no new entries after 2100
C) 2200 hours; persons on line may vote
D) 2100 hours; no one may vote after 2100
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-87, polls close at 2100 hours. Persons already on line at closing may vote, but NO new persons may join the line after 2100.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-87, polls close at 2100 hours. Persons already on line at closing may vote, but NO new persons may join the line after 2100.

questions.text: Temporary Extreme Risk Protection Orders (ERPOs) are served by whom?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-128, temporary ERPOs are served by the NYC Sheriff's Office, NOT by NYPD.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-128, temporary ERPOs are served by the NYC Sheriff's Office, NOT by NYPD.

questions.text: Firearms seized under an ERPO are retained for a minimum of how long?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-128, firearms are retained for a minimum of 2 years. No release is permitted without written authorization from CO, License Division (and CO, Medical Division if the subject is a MOS).
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-128, firearms are retained for a minimum of 2 years. No release is permitted without written authorization from CO, License Division (and CO, Medical Division if the subject is a MOS).

questions.text: What is the maximum age for an AMBER Alert subject?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-99, the child must be 17 or younger and police must believe the child is in imminent danger of serious bodily harm or death.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-99, the child must be 17 or younger and police must believe the child is in imminent danger of serious bodily harm or death.

questions.text: Who may request AMBER Alert activation?
options: C) A Captain or above from the Detective Bureau
D) The Operations Unit duty officer
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-99, only a Captain or above from the Detective Bureau may request activation through the NYS Police Communications Section.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-99, only a Captain or above from the Detective Bureau may request activation through the NYS Police Communications Section.

questions.text: Within what timeframe can an AMBER Alert be cancelled by fax to NYS Police?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-99, cancellation within 8 hours is by fax to NYS Police. After 8 hours, cancellation is through DCPI media notification.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-99, cancellation within 8 hours is by fax to NYS Police. After 8 hours, cancellation is through DCPI media notification.

questions.text: Facial recognition technology establishes what level of evidence?
options: B) Reasonable suspicion for a stop
C) Investigative lead only — NOT probable cause
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-129, facial recognition does not establish probable cause by itself. A possible match candidate is an investigative lead only.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-129, facial recognition does not establish probable cause by itself. A possible match candidate is an investigative lead only.

questions.text: The facial recognition photo repository contains only what type of images?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-129, the photo repository contains ONLY arrest and parole photographs. Use of images outside this repository is prohibited unless approved by the Chief of Detectives, Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau, or Chief of Intelligence.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-129, the photo repository contains ONLY arrest and parole photographs. Use of images outside this repository is prohibited unless approved by the Chief of Detectives, Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau, or Chief of Intelligence.

questions.text: VGTOF Handling Code 1 means what?
options: B) Arrest — warrant exists, approach with caution
C) Investigation — do not alert individual
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-108, Handling Code 1 = Arrest (warrant exists), Code 2 = Detain, Code 3 = Investigation (do not alert), Code 4 = Information gathering (do not alert).
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-108, Handling Code 1 = Arrest (warrant exists), Code 2 = Detain, Code 3 = Investigation (do not alert), Code 4 = Information gathering (do not alert).

questions.text: Can a diplomat with full immunity be arrested?
options: B) Yes, but only for violent felonies
C) No, but may be issued a summons
D) No, and may not be issued a summons
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-56, diplomats with immunity shall NOT be arrested but MAY be issued a summons. Pending verification, they may be detained if a danger to self or public.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-56, diplomats with immunity shall NOT be arrested but MAY be issued a summons. Pending verification, they may be detained if a danger to self or public.

questions.text: In a Level 2 investigative encounter, the UMOS requests consent to search. What document must be prepared?
options: B) Common Law Right of Inquiry — Consent Search Report (CLRI)
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-11, when consent to search is requested at Level 2, the UMOS prepares a CLRI. At Level 3, the request is documented on the **Stop Report** instead.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-11, when consent to search is requested at Level 2, the UMOS prepares a CLRI. At Level 3, the request is documented on the **Stop Report** instead.

questions.text: What does the HARBOR mnemonic stand for regarding when Crime Scene Unit must respond?
options: A) Homicide, Assault (any), Robbery, Burglary (any), Officer-involved, Rape
B) Homicide, Aggravated assault (likely to die), Rape (forcible), Burglary (forced safe/circumvented alarm), Other (CSU will assist), Robbery/hijacking (firearm caused injury)
C) Homicide, Arson, Robbery, Burglary, Obstruction, Rape
D) Homicide, Assault (SPI), Robbery (any), Burglary (safe involved), Other crimes, Rape
questions.answerFull: B) Per P.G. 212-04, these are the specific criteria for mandatory CSU response.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-04, these are the specific criteria for mandatory CSU response.

questions.text: At a hazmat scene, what is the minimum outdoor evacuation distance for an explosion or suspected device?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-37, the minimum outdoor distance for any explosion or suspected device is 1,000 feet. For spills and most fires, the distance is 300 feet. The 1,000-foot distance also applies to fires involving military shipments or tanker trucks.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-37, the minimum outdoor distance for any explosion or suspected device is 1,000 feet. For spills and most fires, the distance is 300 feet. The 1,000-foot distance also applies to fires involving military shipments or tanker trucks.

questions.text: During a bomb threat search, teams must discontinue searching how long before the threatened explosion time?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-40, search teams must discontinue 20 minutes prior to the threatened explosion time, regardless of whether the premise is evacuated or occupied. Search must NOT resume for at least 1 hour after the threatened time.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-40, search teams must discontinue 20 minutes prior to the threatened explosion time, regardless of whether the premise is evacuated or occupied. Search must NOT resume for at least 1 hour after the threatened time.

questions.text: At a post-explosion bomb scene, who removes injured persons to safe locations for medical treatment?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-40, the patrol supervisor/ranking officer directs **ESU** to remove injured to safe locations — not EMS. EMS administers medical treatment at the safe locations, but the physical removal is an ESU responsibility.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-40, the patrol supervisor/ranking officer directs **ESU** to remove injured to safe locations — not EMS. EMS administers medical treatment at the safe locations, but the physical removal is an ESU responsibility.

questions.text: The RATCOP mnemonic identifies information that may only be released after conferral with DCPI AND which other official?
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-77, RATCOP items (Reputation, Anticipated testimony, Test results/refusal, Credibility, Opinion/speculation, Photos/films/mug shots) require conferral with both DCPI and Chief of Detectives before release.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-77, RATCOP items (Reputation, Anticipated testimony, Test results/refusal, Credibility, Opinion/speculation, Photos/films/mug shots) require conferral with both DCPI and Chief of Detectives before release.

questions.text: Under PG 212-123, who prepares the Typed Letterhead (49) when a member fails to record a mandatory BWC event vs. when a member records a prohibited incident?
options: C) Fail to record: Patrol Supervisor; Prohibited recording: Commanding Officer
D) Fail to record: Commanding Officer; Prohibited recording: Patrol Supervisor
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-123, know who writes and who receives in each scenario.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-123, know who writes and who receives in each scenario.

questions.text: BWC video is automatically deleted after how many months?
questions.answerFull: D) Per P.G. 212-123, BWC video is automatically deleted after 39 months. Certain categories (arrest, homicide) may be retained longer. The C.O. may request extended retention through channels to DC Information Technology.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-123, BWC video is automatically deleted after 39 months. Certain categories (arrest, homicide) may be retained longer. The C.O. may request extended retention through channels to DC Information Technology.

questions.text: At what alarm level does FDNY notify NYPD Operations, and at what alarm level does the Desk Officer notify Operations?
options: A) FD notifies at 2 alarms; D.O. notifies at 3 alarms
B) FD notifies at 3 alarms; D.O. notifies at 2 alarms
questions.answerFull: A) Per P.G. 212-58, this is a commonly tested distinction about who initiates the Operations notification at each alarm level.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-58, this is a commonly tested distinction about who initiates the Operations notification at each alarm level.

questions.text: In PG 212-52 (Labor Disputes), certain harassment charges will NOT be enforced. Which type of harassment WILL be enforced?
options: C) Any violence or physical harassment (strike/shove/kick)
D) Following a person in a public place
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-52, Harassment 1st degree (PL 240.25) and Harassment 2nd degree (PL 240.20) will NOT be enforced at labor disputes. However, any actual violence or physical harassment — striking, shoving, kicking, or attempting/threatening same — WILL be enforced.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-52, Harassment 1st degree (PL 240.25) and Harassment 2nd degree (PL 240.20) will NOT be enforced at labor disputes. However, any actual violence or physical harassment — striking, shoving, kicking, or attempting/threatening same — WILL be enforced.

questions.text: Can an MOS request a breathalyzer test from a diplomat suspected of DWI?
options: A) No, this would violate diplomatic immunity
B) Yes, and the diplomat must comply
C) Yes, the MOS may ask, but the diplomat may refuse
D) Only with approval from the Intelligence Division
questions.answerFull: C) Per P.G. 212-56, MOS may ASK a diplomat to take a sobriety test (breathalyzer), but the diplomat is entitled to REFUSE. The request and response must be documented in the Activity Log and Unusual Occurrence Report.
questions.explanation: Per P.G. 212-56, MOS may ASK a diplomat to take a sobriety test (breathalyzer), but the diplomat is entitled to REFUSE. The request and response must be documented in the Activity Log and Unusual Occurrence Report.

sergeantFocus.text: Supervisors must ensure officers understand the guardrail boundaries, do not participate in the election process, and properly handle disputes about voting rights by referring to the county Supreme Court.
Ensure all election materials are accounted for and properly secured. Verify that the transition from active voting to post-closing procedures occurs smoothly and without interference.
Sergeants are the preferred rank for conducting rifle/shotgun permit surrender investigations.
When a subordinate MOS becomes the subject of an ERPO, the supervisor must ensure immediate CO notification and initiation of the Modified Assignment process.

Patrol supervisors shall inspect Activity Logs to ensure NYCHA facilities are being inspected by members on post. Supervisors should recommend Departmental Recognition for burglary arrests at NYCHA facilities.
Patrol supervisor should respond to ensure proper coordination between NYPD and NYCHA maintenance staff, particularly when occupants include elderly or medically vulnerable individuals.
Supervisors shall review Activity Logs to verify interior patrols are being conducted and documented. Ensure members are varying patrol times and routes to maximize deterrent effect.

Supervisors must ensure that communication accommodations are provided promptly and that the individual's preferred method of communication is the primary consideration. For pre-planned events, arrange interpreter services in advance through the Chief of Personnel.
Patrol supervisor must investigate immediately when a press member is assaulted or harassed. Notification to DCPI must be made without delay. DCPI is available via phone **24/7**.

Supervisors must ensure members understand that press access cannot be restricted through police lines or frozen zones designed to block recording. Any incident involving the press requires immediate DCPI notification. The patrol supervisor must conduct an immediate investigation if press members are assaulted or harassed.
Ensure subordinates understand that all grant/equipment/funding offers must be forwarded to the Police Commissioner — members have no authority to accept directly.
Preparation of a Traffic Intelligence Report does not relieve precinct personnel from taking other corrective action to expedite traffic flow and ensure pedestrian safety.
Patrol supervisor must respond personally when forced entry is detected at a DOE facility and assign a member to guard until the premises are secured.
Supervisors must verify coordinator availability before any auxiliary patrol deployment. No exceptions to the coordinator requirement.
Patrol supervisor shall respond and approve resolution (arrest, summons, court referral, or no action).
Patrol supervisor shall consult the duty captain when:

Patrol supervisor shall take command of the scene, coordinate with ESU, and ensure the area below is secured. Consider requesting additional resources (negotiators, mental health professionals) early.

Ensure desk officers are maintaining the Order of Protection Log properly and that service is attempted at least once per tour. The Domestic Violence Investigator is responsible for entering service information into the State Order of Protection Registry via FINEST.
Do NOT detain a properly identified and verified diplomat. All reports must still be prepared even when immunity applies.

The PDS/BRAM Supervisor is responsible for reviewing all burglary/robbery complaints to determine case status (ACTIVE or CLOSED) and ensuring all investigative steps have been taken. Major case prints must be taken for every burglary arrest.

Supervisors conduct the final review in the facial recognition process. Ensure all subordinates understand that a possible match is an investigative lead only and must never be presented as establishing probable cause.
Only authorized/approved protective vests will be worn.

Supervisors must ensure meal periods are properly scheduled and documented. Key responsibilities include: assigning meal periods prior to posting details, ensuring members are not deprived of meals, and properly documenting any exigent circumstances that prevent granting meals.
Desk officer shall check CPR System Roll Call to account for all members from previous tour. Conduct **immediate investigation** if any member failed to scan out.
The desk officer shall maintain the Interrupted Patrol Log under **direct control** at all times and review it frequently during the tour to ensure member presence is being expedited.

The supervisor shall review every Transit System Ejection Report for accuracy and completeness before approving it in FORMS. If the report is deficient, it must be returned to the member for correction.
During radio malfunction:

chapters.title: Mobilization/Emergency Incidents
chapters.readme:
# Section 213 — Mobilization/Emergency Incidents

## Overview

Section 213 of the NYPD Patrol Guide covers mobilization, emergency response, special events, and First Amendment activities. This chapter is HIGH PRIORITY for the Sergeant Promotional Exam, particularly the procedures governing First Amendment activities (213-06, 213-20) and special events management (213-15). Sergeants must know authorization hierarchies, specific numerical thresholds, processing time limits, and the rights of demonstrators and prisoners.

This study guide covers 17 procedures from Section 213. Six procedures have full extracted text; the remaining 11 are summarized from cross-references.

## Priority Topics for Sergeant Exam

1. **Mass arrest authorization** -- Only Senior Executive (deputy chief+) or designee (captain+) can order mass arrests at First Amendment events (213-20)
2. **MAPC activation and prisoner limits** -- Chief of Department activates; 20 per wagon, 5 per officer, 4 officers per supervisor (213-06)
3. **7 hours 30 minutes processing time limit** for First Amendment violations/misdemeanors (213-06)
4. **Incident Commander hierarchy** -- Precinct CO / Patrol Borough Commander / Chief of Patrol (213-15)
5. **Legal observer identification** -- NLG = green hats, NYCLU = blue hats/vests; free access through police lines (213-20)
6. **Barrier exclusion distances** -- 100 feet on through streets, 25 feet from corners (213-15)
7. **Security alert levels** -- GREEN/BLUE/YELLOW/ORANGE/RED; NYC at ORANGE; changes via FINEST Message (213-18)
8. **Body-worn cameras NOT activated** at demonstrations unless per P.G. 212-123 (213-20)
9. **Handcuff checks at three points** -- entering van, exiting van, regularly at processing (213-20)
10. **De-escalation required** before enforcement action at First Amendment activities (213-20)

## Study Tips

- Start with **** -- it contains the highest-yield exam material in this chapter
- Use **** as a quick-reference sheet and self-test on the critical numbers
- Work through all 20 review questions without looking at the answers first, then check yourself
- Pay special attention to authorization levels: who can order what, and at what rank
- Memorize the security alert level colors and order (G-B-Y-O-R)
- The numerical limits in 213-06 (20/5/4, 7:30, 2 hours) are classic exam question material
chapters.keyTerms:
# Key Terms — Section 213: Mobilization/Emergency Incidents

## Definitions

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| First Amendment Activity | Exercise of rights protected by the 1st Amendment: speech, assembly, petition, press | 213-20 |
| Senior Executive | Deputy chief or higher rank | 213-20 |
| Designee | Captain or above designated by a Senior Executive | 213-20 |
| De-escalation | Reducing intensity of conflict or potential conflict | 213-20 |
| Incident Commander | MOS in charge of the police response at an event or incident | 213-15, 213-20 |
| Nonviolent Participant | Person engaged in lawful First Amendment activity | 213-20 |
| Violent Behavior | Conduct involving force causing or intended to cause physical injury or property damage | 213-20 |
| Protest Liaison | Community Affairs Bureau (CAB) member assigned to communicate with demonstrators | 213-20 |
| Mass Arrest Processing Center (MAPC) | Facility activated to process large numbers of arrests during mass arrest situations | 213-06 |
| Legal Observer | Individual (NLG or NYCLU) present at demonstrations to observe police-protester interactions; granted free access through police lines | 213-20 |
| Handschu Consent Decree | Legal ruling requiring written approval from Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau for political activity investigations | 213-20 |
| FINEST Message | Department-wide communication method used to announce changes in citywide security alert levels | 213-18 |
| Traffic Brief | Daily compilation of traffic-related notifications from events affecting traffic flow | 213-17 |
| Unusual Disorder Kit | Standardized kit of supplies maintained by each command for use during unusual disorders | 213-09 |

## Critical Numbers

| Item | Value | Source |
|------|-------|--------|
| Maximum prisoners per wagon | 20 | 213-06 |
| Maximum prisoners per arresting officer | 5 | 213-06 |
| Maximum arresting officers per arrest supervisor | 4 | 213-06 |
| First Amendment arrest processing time limit (violations/misdemeanors) | 7 hours 30 minutes | 213-06 |
| Food required for prisoners held longer than | 2 hours | 213-06 |
| Barrier exclusion distance — through streets from parade route | 100 feet | 213-15 |
| Barrier exclusion distance — corners on parade route | 25 feet | 213-15 |
| Sector maps in Unusual Disorder Kit | 75 | 213-09 |
| Index cards in Unusual Disorder Kit | 200 | 213-09 |
| Complaint Report Worksheets in Unusual Disorder Kit | 200 | 213-09 |
| Aided Report Worksheets in Unusual Disorder Kit | 200 | 213-09 |
| Detail Roster sheets in Unusual Disorder Kit | 200 | 213-09 |
| Tags in Unusual Disorder Kit | 300 | 213-09 |
| Unusual Disorder Kit inspection frequency | Semiannual (January and July) | 213-09 |
| Citywide Events Planning availability | 1000-1800, Mon-Fri | 213-20 |
| NYC default security alert level | ORANGE (High) | 213-18 |
| Number of security alert levels | 5 | 213-18 |
| Number of handcuff check points | 3 | 213-20 |

## Incident Commander Hierarchy

| Event Scope | Incident Commander |
|------------|-------------------|
| Single command | Precinct Commanding Officer |
| Multi-command | Patrol Borough Commander |
| Multi-borough | Chief of Patrol |
| Tier One First Amendment | Sergeant (may serve as IC) |

## Security Alert Levels (Low to High)

| Order | Color | Name | Risk |
|-------|-------|------|------|
| 1 | GREEN | Low | Low risk |
| 2 | BLUE | Guarded | General risk |
| 3 | YELLOW | Elevated | Significant risk |
| 4 | ORANGE | High | High risk |
| 5 | RED | Severe | Severe risk |

## Legal Observer Identification

| Organization | Identifier |
|-------------|-----------|
| National Lawyers Guild (NLG) | Bright green hats |
| NYCLU | Blue hats/vests |

## Authorization Requirements

| Action | Minimum Authority Required |
|--------|--------------------------|
| Activate MAPC | Chief of Department |
| Authorize MAPC at First Amendment event | Senior Executive or designee |
| Order mass arrests at First Amendment activity | Senior Executive (deputy chief+) or designee (captain+) |
| Individual arrest for criminal conduct | Any supervisor |
| Political activity investigation (Handschu) | Written approval from Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau |

questions.text: Who has the authority to activate a Mass Arrest Processing Center (MAPC)?
questions.explanation: The **Chief of Department** activates the MAPC. At First Amendment events specifically, only the **First Amendment Activity Senior Executive or designee** can authorize MAPC activation. (P.G. 213-06)

questions.text: What is the maximum number of prisoners that can be transported per wagon during mass arrest processing?
questions.explanation: **20 prisoners** per wagon. (P.G. 213-06)

questions.text: What is the maximum arrest processing time for violations and misdemeanors at a First Amendment activity?
questions.explanation: **7 hours 30 minutes.** (P.G. 213-06)

questions.text: An arresting officer during mass arrest processing can be assigned no more than how many prisoners? And an arrest supervisor can be assigned no more than how many arresting officers?
questions.explanation: An arresting officer processes no more than **5 prisoners**. An arrest supervisor is assigned no more than **4 arresting officers**. (P.G. 213-06)

questions.text: At the MAPC, prisoners must be provided food after being held for how long?
questions.explanation: Food must be provided if a prisoner is held **more than 2 hours**. (P.G. 213-06)

questions.text: Who is the Incident Commander at a special event that spans multiple patrol boroughs?
questions.explanation: The **Chief of Patrol** is the Incident Commander for multi-borough events. For multi-command (single borough) events, it is the **Patrol Borough Commander**. For single command events, it is the **Precinct Commanding Officer**. (P.G. 213-15)

questions.text: What are the barrier exclusion distances at a parade route?
questions.explanation: **100 feet** exclusion from the parade route on through streets, and **25 feet** on the parade route from corners. Additionally, barrier configuration must NOT unreasonably restrict pedestrian access. (P.G. 213-15)

questions.text: What is the current citywide security alert level for New York City, and how are changes communicated?
questions.explanation: NYC is maintained at **ORANGE (High)**, which is the fourth of five levels. Changes are communicated via **FINEST Message**. (P.G. 213-18)

questions.text: List the five citywide security alert levels in order from lowest to highest, including their color and name.
questions.explanation:
1. **GREEN** — Low
2. **BLUE** — Guarded
3. **YELLOW** — Elevated
4. **ORANGE** — High
5. **RED** — Severe

(P.G. 213-18)

questions.text: How are NLG legal observers and NYCLU legal observers identified at demonstrations?
questions.explanation: **NLG (National Lawyers Guild)** legal observers wear **bright green hats**. **NYCLU** legal observers wear **blue hats/vests**. Both must be permitted **free access through police lines**. (P.G. 213-20)

questions.text: Who has the authority to order mass arrests at a First Amendment activity? Can a sergeant order mass arrests?
questions.explanation: Only a **Senior Executive (deputy chief or higher)** or a **designee (captain or above)** can order mass arrests at First Amendment activities. A sergeant **cannot** order mass arrests. However, individual arrests for criminal conduct are still permitted by any supervisor. (P.G. 213-20)

questions.text: What are the three required handcuff check points during First Amendment arrest processing?
questions.explanation:
1. **Entering** the transport van
2. **Exiting** the transport van
3. **Regularly** at arrest processing

(P.G. 213-20)

questions.text: Under what circumstances can body-worn cameras be activated at demonstrations?
questions.explanation: Body-worn cameras are **NOT activated** at demonstrations **unless** per P.G. 212-123. The mere occurrence of a protest does not authorize camera activation. (P.G. 213-20)

questions.text: What is the Handschu Consent Decree, and what does it require?
questions.explanation: The Handschu Consent Decree requires **written approval from the Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau** for political activity investigations. It is a legal constraint limiting investigative authority at First Amendment events. (P.G. 213-20)

questions.text: What role can a sergeant play in a Tier One response to a First Amendment activity?
questions.explanation: At a **Tier One** response, a sergeant may serve as the **Incident Commander**. Higher-tier responses require higher-ranking Incident Commanders. (P.G. 213-20)

questions.text: Regarding protest liaisons from the Community Affairs Bureau (CAB), what are the uniform and assignment restrictions?
questions.explanation: CAB protest liaisons **must** wear uniform clearly displaying rank, name, shield, and command. They may **NOT** wear civilian attire. CAB personnel are **NOT** assigned to patrol or enforcement duties except in **exceptional circumstances**. (P.G. 213-20)

questions.text: How often must the Unusual Disorder Kit be inspected, and in which months?
questions.explanation: The Unusual Disorder Kit requires **semiannual inspection** in **January** and **July**. (P.G. 213-09)

questions.text: How many sector maps, index cards, and tags must be in an Unusual Disorder Kit?
questions.explanation:
- **75** sector maps
- **200** index cards
- **300** tags

The kit also contains 200 Complaint Report Worksheets, 200 Aided Report Worksheets, and 200 Detail Roster sheets. (P.G. 213-09)

questions.text: Which unit must be notified for all events affecting traffic, and which additional unit must be notified for unscheduled events?
questions.explanation: The **Traffic Management Center (TMC)** must be notified of **all** events affecting traffic. The **Operations Unit** must also be notified for **unscheduled** events. (P.G. 213-17)

questions.text: What steps must be taken before enforcement action at a First Amendment activity?
questions.explanation:
Before enforcement action at a First Amendment activity:
1. **De-escalation** attempts are required
2. **Warnings** must be given (when feasible)
3. **Sufficient resources** must be assembled before taking enforcement action
4. Only a **Senior Executive or designee** can order mass arrests

Individual arrests for criminal conduct may still be made by any supervisor without these prerequisites. (P.G. 213-20)

sergeantFocus.text: A sergeant can serve as Incident Commander at a Tier One response. However, any escalation beyond Tier One requires a higher-ranking IC. De-escalation is mandatory before enforcement -- this is not optional. Warnings must be given when feasible, and sufficient resources must be assembled before acting.

CAB protest liaisons may NOT wear civilian attire and may NOT be assigned to enforcement duties except in exceptional circumstances. If you see a CAB member in plainclothes at a demonstration, this is a procedure violation.

The arrest processing supervisor has direct responsibility for prisoner welfare: food, medical care, restrooms, and telephone access. Failure to provide these is a procedure violation. Track the clock -- 7 hours 30 minutes is the hard ceiling for First Amendment violation/misdemeanor processing.

While the full text is unavailable, know that at **Tier One** a sergeant can be the Incident Commander. Higher tiers require higher-ranking ICs. This tiered approach ensures proportional response to First Amendment events.
All supervisors must know the kit's location. If you are newly assigned to a command, learn the kit location immediately. Ensure inspections occur on schedule in January and July.

Be prepared for exam questions that test the order of alert levels or ask which color corresponds to which threat description. A common distractor is confusing BLUE (Guarded/General risk) with GREEN (Low risk).
A critique report with overtime reporting is required after every special event. The sergeant must ensure the detail roster is complete and accurate.
Do not assume someone else has made traffic notifications. Verify that TMC has been contacted, especially for unscheduled events where the Operations Unit must also be informed.

chapters.title: Quality of Life Matters
chapters.readme:
# Section 214 — Quality of Life Matters

## Overview

Section 214 covers 26 Patrol Guide procedures related to quality of life enforcement, from alarm response and evictions to animal control and 311 service requests. This is **medium priority** material for the sergeant promotional exam. The procedures emphasize the supervisory role in interagency coordination, the limits of police authority in civil matters, and specific numeric thresholds that are frequently tested.

## Source

- **Patrol Guide Section:** 214
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide3.pdf
- **Priority:** Medium

## Procedures

| Procedure | Title | Study File | Status |
|-----------|-------|-----------|--------|
| P.G. 214-02 | Narcotics Eviction Program | enforcement-programs | completed |
| P.G. 214-03 | Graffiti Reward Program | enforcement-programs | completed |
| P.G. 214-08 | Chronic Abuser Alarm Procedures | alarm-procedures | completed |
| P.G. 214-09 | TLC Padlock Enforcement | miscellaneous-qol | completed |
| P.G. 214-10 | Unlawful Posting of Signs | miscellaneous-qol | completed |
| P.G. 214-11 | Obscenity/Offensive Material | miscellaneous-qol | completed |
| P.G. 214-12 | Unlawful Evictions | evictions-civil | completed |
| P.G. 214-13 | Evictions, Repossessions, Civil Process | evictions-civil | completed |
| P.G. 214-16 | Construction Sites | buildings-construction | completed |
| P.G. 214-17 | Vacant Buildings | buildings-construction | completed |
| P.G. 214-18 | Stop Work Orders | buildings-construction | completed |
| P.G. 214-19 | Chains/Padlocks Commercial Premises | buildings-construction | completed |
| P.G. 214-20 | Repair of Damaged Entrances | buildings-construction | completed |
| P.G. 214-24 | OATH Summons Vehicle Alarms | alarm-procedures | completed |
| P.G. 214-25 | Deactivation Motor Vehicle Alarms | alarm-procedures | completed |
| P.G. 214-26 | Snow Emergency Regulations | vehicles-snow | completed |
| P.G. 214-27 | Snow Removal | vehicles-snow | completed |
| P.G. 214-29 | Derelict Vehicles with Registration Plates | vehicles-snow | completed |
| P.G. 214-30 | Removal of Expired Registration Plates | vehicles-snow | completed |
| P.G. 214-31 | Removal of Vehicles from Parkways | vehicles-snow | completed |
| P.G. 214-32 | Discarded Refrigerators/Freezers | miscellaneous-qol | completed |
| P.G. 214-33 | Care and Disposition of Animals | miscellaneous-qol | completed |
| P.G. 214-35 | Processing 311 Service Requests | miscellaneous-qol | completed |
| P.G. 214-37 | DOB Access Warrants | buildings-construction | completed |
| P.G. 214-39 | C.U.R.E. Operations | enforcement-programs | completed |
| P.G. 214-40 | Mediation Referral Program | evictions-civil | completed |

## Priority Topics for Sergeant Exam

1. **311 Service Requests (214-35)** — Multiple supervisory frequencies (hourly, daily, weekly, monthly), chronic condition thresholds
2. **Unlawful Evictions (214-12)** — 30-day occupancy threshold, borough-specific return dates, NO DAT, not fingerprintable
3. **Chronic Abuser Alarm (214-08)** — 3 alarms in 3 months, three service methods, 48-hour reinstatement delay
4. **Vehicle Alarms (214-24/25)** — 3-minute observation, vehicle vs. building alarm authority, $280/$2,100 fines
5. **Care of Animals (214-33)** — Bite report in every instance, firearm use criteria, Dept of Health notifications
6. **C.U.R.E. Operations (214-39)** — 30-day notice, insufficient grounds, Chief of Patrol approval
7. **Evictions/Civil Process (214-13)** — Police role limited to peacekeeping, no property on sidewalk, no door breaking
8. **Obscenity (214-11)** — Judicial determination vs. summary enforcement distinction

## Study Tips

- Focus on numeric thresholds and timeframes — these are the most common exam question targets in Section 214
- Learn the distinction between police authority and other agency authority (DOB, TLC, city marshals) — many procedures limit MOS to peacekeeping only
- Know which supervisor level is responsible for each action (desk officer, patrol supervisor, Special Ops Lt, CO)
- Pay attention to form numbers — the exam may reference specific PD forms by number
- The vehicle alarm vs. building alarm distinction (can deactivate vehicles, cannot deactivate buildings) is a classic exam question
chapters.keyTerms:
# Key Terms — Section 214: Quality of Life Matters

## Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| C.U.R.E. | Coordinating a United Resolution with Establishments — multi-agency operation targeting problem commercial establishments |
| M.E.N.D. Program | Free mediation offered by the Mayor's Office of Nightlife as an alternative to C.U.R.E. operations |
| Chronic 311 Condition | 5 or more 311 requests in a 28-day period for the same condition at the same location |
| Chronic Abuser Alarm | Location with 3 or more unnecessary/unfounded alarms in a 3-month period |
| Dangerous Animal | An animal that menaces, threatens, attacks, or bites a person |
| Derelict Vehicle | A vehicle so dilapidated, burned, or stripped as to have no value except scrap |
| Door Service | Method of serving alarm notice by placing in mailbox or under door when no qualified person is available |
| Deferred Service | Method where CPO serves alarm notice the next business day when service cannot be made at the scene |
| Qualified Person (alarm service) | Owner, corporate officer, manager, supervisor, or adult employee/resident |
| Unlawful Eviction | Eviction through force, denial of essential services, removing possessions, changing locks, or interfering with comfort |
| Access Warrant | Court Order signed by a Supreme Court judge allowing DOB to inspect premises |
| OMAP | Designated liaison between NYPD and NYC 311 |
| CVTF | Citywide Vandals Task Force — handles graffiti vandal debriefing |
| ACC | Animal Care and Control of NYC — responds to animal complaints |
| ICAD Number | Identifier entered on Aided and Dangerous Animal/Bite Report |
| LBB | Legal Bureau Bulletin — supplemental legal guidance that modifies or clarifies PG procedures |
| Dwelling Unit | Any building or portion used as a home, residence, or sleeping place (includes private hotels with gov't funding; excludes hospitals, convents, NYCHA) |
| Legitimate Occupancy | Tenant has a lease, occupied 30+ consecutive days, SRO requesting lease (verbal is sufficient), or sublet tenant |
| Stay of Eviction | Court order to halt eviction — unenforceable once the eviction is already complete |
| 72-Hour Notice of Service | Required notice before executing Warrant of Eviction; starts day after service, excludes weekends/holidays; NOT required for Repossession Orders |
| Telephone Record | Document where 311 deferrals and notifications are recorded (not the Telephone Dispatch Log) |
| Chronic 311 Condition | 5+ service requests in 28 days for the same specified condition at the same location |
| NCO Sergeant (311) | Directed by CO to make daily callbacks to identified 311 callers; maintains 28-day chronic conditions list |

## Critical Numbers

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| Graffiti reward maximum | $500 |
| Chronic abuser alarm trigger | 3+ alarms in 3 months |
| Alarm reinstatement delay | Minimum 48 hours |
| Vehicle alarm observation period | 3 minutes |
| Vehicle alarm auto-deactivation | 10 minutes |
| Building alarm summons threshold | 15 minutes continuous |
| OATH vehicle alarm mail-in fine | $280 |
| OATH vehicle alarm maximum fine | $2,100 |
| Unlawful eviction occupancy threshold | 30+ consecutive days |
| Unlawful eviction summons return date minimum | 21 days out |
| TLC follow-up padlocking | 10 days after initial sealing |
| Snow removal summons threshold | 4 hours after snow stops |
| Expired registration plate threshold | More than 2 months expired |
| Derelict vehicle plate pickup deadline | 5 days |
| Narcotics evidence retention (eviction) | Minimum 3 months |
| C.U.R.E. NOTICE lead time | At least 30 days before operation |
| C.U.R.E. CO determination deadline | Within 30 days of notice |
| Chronic 311 condition | 5+ requests in 28 days |
| 311 immediate response trigger | 5+ calls in 8 hours |
| 311 desk officer refresh interval | At least every hour |
| 311 daily review time | 0800 by Second Platoon Desk Officer |
| 311 callback attempts | Minimum 2 per caller |
| 311 Special Ops Lt audit frequency | Monthly |
| 311 chronic conditions list update | Weekly |
| Warrant of Eviction execution hours | Mon-Fri 0800-1700 |
| Search Warrant execution hours | Mon-Fri 0600-2100 |
| 72-hour Notice of Service | Starts day after service, excludes Sat/Sun/Holidays |
| ACC Brooklyn Control Center | 24/7 |
| ACC Rescue Units | Mon-Fri 0800-2000 |
| ACC Other Control Centers | Tues-Sat (business hours) |

## Key Forms

| Form | Number | Used For |
|------|--------|----------|
| Complaint Report | PD313-152 | Narcotics eviction documentation |
| Notice of Unnecessary Alarm | PD310-120 | Chronic abuser alarm notification |
| Termination Notice (alarm) | PD310-121 | Terminating police alarm response |
| Registration Plate Removal Notice (NY) | PD571-120 | Derelict/expired NY plates |
| Expired Registration Plate Removal (out-of-state) | PD571-124 | Derelict/expired out-of-state plates |
| Dangerous Animal/Bite Report | PD311-152 | Every animal incident |
| C.U.R.E. Notice | PD635-121 | Notification to problem establishments |
| Referral to Mediation | PD613-181 | Non-criminal dispute referral |
| Notification to Owner | MISC. 3930 | Commercial premises secured by ESU |

## Key Radio Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 10-11 | Alarm Condition |
| 10-42 | Notify Communications Section (311 receipt) |
| 10-90N | Notice Served (alarm) |
| 10-95 | Refer to Another Agency (311 disposition) |

## Key Legal Bureau Bulletins

| LBB | Topic | Key Point |
|-----|-------|-----------|
| Vol 49 #1 (Aug 2019) | Unlawful Evictions | Adds Building/Health Code violations as eviction method; acts must be done for PURPOSE of eviction; police cannot break in to let tenant re-enter unless medicine vital to life is inside |
| Vol 14 #8 | Warrant of Eviction | 72-hour Notice of Service required; starts day after service excluding weekends/holidays; no 72-hour notice for Repossession Orders; Order to Seize Property does not authorize forced entry unless explicitly stated |

## Summons Return Dates — Unlawful Eviction

| Borough | Day |
|---------|-----|
| Manhattan/Bronx | Tuesday |
| Brooklyn/Queens | Thursday |
| Staten Island | First Wednesday of the month |

questions.text: Under the Chronic Abuser Alarm Procedures (P.G. 214-08), what triggers a location being classified as a chronic abuser, and what is the minimum delay before police response can be reinstated after termination?
questions.explanation: A location is classified as a chronic abuser when **3 or more alarms** in a **3-month period** are unnecessary or unfounded. After a TERMINATION NOTICE (PD310-121) is served, the minimum delay before reinstatement takes effect is **48 hours**. The CO may also require an alarm system or premises security survey before reinstatement. (P.G. 214-08)

questions.text: A vehicle alarm has been sounding continuously. At what point can MOS take action, and what is the time of violation recorded on the OATH summons?
questions.explanation: MOS can take action after the vehicle alarm has been audible for **3 minutes**. The time of violation recorded on the OATH summons is the **end** of the 3-minute observation period. The mail-in fine is $280 and the maximum penalty is $2,100. If the alarm resets and sounds again, it is considered **one continuous action** — only one summons is issued. (P.G. 214-24)

questions.text: What is the critical distinction between MOS authority to deactivate vehicle alarms versus building alarms?
questions.explanation: MOS **may** attempt to deactivate **vehicle alarms** (within the first 10 minutes, without intentionally damaging the vehicle). However, MOS are **NOT permitted** to deactivate **building alarms**. For building alarms that continue for **15 or more minutes**, MOS may issue a summons. ESU should be requested if MOS have difficulty accessing the engine or passenger compartment of a vehicle. (P.G. 214-25)

questions.text: A tenant claims they were unlawfully evicted. They have no lease but have lived in the unit for five weeks. The person who changed the locks is present but refuses to let the tenant back in. What actions should the sergeant direct?
questions.explanation: The law applies because the tenant occupied the unit for **30 or more consecutive days** (5 weeks exceeds 30 days), even without a lease. Since the violator is present but **refuses to permit reentry**, the sergeant should direct an **arrest**. The subject must be brought to criminal court for **prompt arraignment — NO DAT** is permitted. Unlawful eviction is a **Class A misdemeanor** but is **NOT fingerprintable**. The sergeant should also confirm there is no valid Warrant of Eviction or Government Order to Vacate. (P.G. 214-12)

questions.text: What are the summons return dates for unlawful eviction cases by borough, and what is the minimum lead time?
questions.explanation:
All return dates must be at least **21 days** out:
- **Manhattan/Bronx:** Tuesday
- **Brooklyn/Queens:** Thursday
- **Staten Island:** First Wednesday of the month

(P.G. 214-12)

questions.text: A dog has bitten a pedestrian but has since fled the scene. The animal is no longer present. What documentation and notifications are required?
questions.explanation: The **DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT (PD311-152)** must be prepared in **every instance**, even if the animal is gone. The **desk officer** must notify the **Department of Health and Mental Hygiene by phone** because the animal bit a human. If the offender (owner) is identified, process for **DAT** if eligible. The animal is never placed in the trunk of a Department vehicle. (P.G. 214-33)

questions.text: Under P.G. 214-33, when is a member of the service authorized to use a firearm against a dangerous animal?
questions.explanation: A firearm may be used ONLY to protect from **imminent physical injury** when there is **no opportunity to retreat**. General use of force against animals is limited to situations necessary to prevent harm or injury. (P.G. 214-33)

questions.text: What defines a "Chronic 311 Condition," and what triggers an immediate response? Describe the different supervisory review frequencies.
questions.explanation:
- **Chronic 311 Condition:** **5 or more** requests in a **28-day period** for the same condition at the same location
- **Immediate response trigger:** **5 or more** calls within an **8-hour period**

Supervisory review frequencies:
- **Hourly:** Desk officer manually refreshes the 311 system at least every hour
- **Daily:** Second Platoon Desk Officer reviews at **0800** for the previous 24 hours; CO directs daily callbacks (minimum **2 attempts** per caller)
- **Weekly:** Special Ops Lt updates the 28-day chronic conditions list
- **Monthly:** Special Ops Lt conducts an audit of 311 callers

Command Log entries are required at the **beginning and end** of each tour with the number of pending assignments. (P.G. 214-35)

questions.text: A C.U.R.E. operation is being planned against a nightclub. The nightclub owner's only issue is refusing to attend community nightlife meetings. Is this sufficient grounds for the operation?
questions.explanation: **No.** Refusal to attend nightlife meetings is specifically listed as **NOT sufficient grounds** for a C.U.R.E. operation. Other insufficient grounds include: property crimes beyond the establishment's control, and crimes where security acted properly. Valid grounds include complaint reports, summonses, arrests, 911/311 calls, general disorder, aided cases from intoxication/overdose, and a documented pattern of problems. (P.G. 214-39)

questions.text: What is the required lead time for a C.U.R.E. NOTICE, and what are the three possible outcomes after the notice period?
questions.answerFull: C) The must be served at least **30 days** prior to the operation. Prior notice is NOT required when it would compromise an active investigation or pose a health/safety risk.
questions.explanation:
The must be served at least **30 days** prior to the operation. Prior notice is NOT required when it would compromise an active investigation or pose a health/safety risk.

Within **30 days** of the notice, the CO determines one of three outcomes:
1. **Conduct the operation**
2. **Additional monitoring**
3. **No further action**

The **Chief of Patrol** must approve or disapprove through written endorsement. The operation is coordinated by the **Legal Bureau's Civil Enforcement Unit** and overseen by the **Mayor's Office of Nightlife**. (P.G. 214-39)

questions.text: During an eviction executed by a city marshal, the marshal asks MOS to help break down a door. Property is being placed on the sidewalk. What should the sergeant direct?
questions.explanation:
The sergeant should direct that:
1. MOS will **NOT assist** in breaking the door or damaging property — the police role is to **preserve peace and protect life/property ONLY**
2. The marshal/sheriff or landlord is **NOT permitted** to place property on the sidewalk — a **vehicle must be on scene** and property loaded **immediately**
3. Execution of the warrant is the **sole responsibility** of the city marshal/sheriff
4. The marshal must force entry in the **least disruptive way**

The sergeant should ensure the Activity Log records: marshal's name/shield, time of entry, location, tenant name, and who is responsible for property. (P.G. 214-13)

questions.text: MOS discover a derelict vehicle with New York plates. It is not needed as evidence. What is the step-by-step process, and what are the key timelines?
questions.explanation:
1. Remove the registration plates
2. Prepare the **REGISTRATION PLATE REMOVAL NOTICE (PD571-120)** (for NY plates)
3. Notify the **Stolen Property Inquiry Unit** (required in ALL cases)
4. Notify the owner to pick up plates within **5 days**
5. If unclaimed after 5 days: forward plates and typed letterhead to **DMV**
6. Desk officer assigns a check **5 days later** to verify if the vehicle is still present
7. If still there: notify the **Department of Sanitation** for removal

The vehicle is NOT taken into police custody because it is not needed as evidence. The Department of Sanitation removes all derelict vehicles not required as evidence. (P.G. 214-29)

questions.text: MOS encounter potentially obscene material at a storefront. What is the correct chain of action, and what legal distinction must the supervisor understand?
questions.explanation:
MOS must **NOT** take enforcement action. They must **immediately notify the patrol supervisor**. The patrol supervisor must:
1. Respond to the scene
2. Determine if the material meets legal criteria
3. Make an Activity Log entry
4. **Consult the Legal Bureau**

**No enforcement** is permitted without specific direction from the Legal Bureau.

The critical legal distinction:
- **PL Article 235 (Obscenity):** Requires a **judicial determination** that the item is obscene — no summary enforcement
- **PL 245.11 (Public Display of Offensive Sexual Material):** A **Class A misdemeanor** — summary enforcement is allowed under appropriate circumstances

(P.G. 214-11)

questions.text: What are the eligibility requirements for the Mediation Referral Program, and what types of disputes are NOT eligible?
questions.explanation:
**Eligibility requirements:**
- No threat of violence
- No criminal allegation
- No Order of Protection between the parties
- Parties are not family or intimate partners
- Likely an ongoing dispute

**NOT eligible:**
- Criminal conduct
- Unlawful evictions (handled under P.G. 214-12)
- Intimate partners or family members
- Child abuse or neglect
- Parties with an Order of Protection between them

The program uses the **REFERRAL TO MEDIATION (PD613-181)** with a serial number in **YYYY-XXX-NNNN** format. It must NOT be used in lieu of enforcement action — only when there is no basis to take enforcement action. The **Special Ops Lt** conducts a weekly inspection of the precinct mediation file. (P.G. 214-40)

questions.text: A DOB inspector arrives at the precinct with an Access Warrant to inspect a commercial building. What must the desk officer verify, and what are the limits on MOS authority during the inspection?
questions.explanation:
The desk officer must verify:
1. The warrant is **signed by a Supreme Court judge**
2. The warrant is **not expired**

Limits on MOS authority:
- MOS role is to **preserve peace and prevent crime ONLY**
- MOS will **NOT** assist in breaking a door or damaging property
- If access is not granted by the occupant, **DOB reschedules** — this is not a police responsibility

The same principle applies to other civil enforcement (P.G. 214-13, 214-18): police preserve peace but do not participate in the execution of civil process. (P.G. 214-37)

questions.text: A landlord has cut off hot water to a tenant who has lived in the apartment for two months without a lease. The tenant calls police claiming unlawful eviction. Is this an unlawful eviction?
questions.explanation: **No.** While the tenant has legitimate occupancy (30+ consecutive days), cutting off **hot water** alone is NOT an unlawful eviction. The exam has tested this directly. Essential services that qualify as unlawful eviction when denied include **heat**, **water**, and **electricity** — but **hot water** by itself does not meet the threshold. The acts must also be done for the **purpose** of eviction (per LBB Vol 49 #1). (P.G. 214-12)

questions.text: A city marshal arrives to execute a Warrant of Eviction at 1830 hours on a Wednesday. The tenant has a Stay of Eviction. The eviction was already completed earlier that day. What should the sergeant consider?
questions.explanation:
Two key issues:

1. **Time:** Warrants of Eviction may only be executed **Monday-Friday 0800-1700**. At 1830 hours, the execution window has passed. (Do NOT confuse with Search Warrant hours of 0600-2100.)

2. **Stay of Eviction:** Once the eviction is **complete**, a Stay of Eviction is **unenforceable**. Attempts to pay rent or produce a stay does NOT allow re-entry after the eviction is complete.

Additionally, per LBB Vol 49 #1, police **cannot break in** to let the tenant re-enter **UNLESS** there is medicine inside that is vital to life. (P.G. 214-13)

questions.text: During a 311 assignment review, you notice a condition has been deferred. Where should the deferral be indicated — in the Telephone Dispatch Log, the Telephone Record, or the 311 system?
questions.explanation: Deferrals should be indicated in the **311 system** or the **Telephone Record**, as appropriate. The deferred assignment gets an interim disposition of "Deferred" or "Open." Do **NOT** confuse the Telephone Record with the Telephone Dispatch Log — the exam specifically tests this distinction. Notifications are documented in the Telephone Record. (P.G. 214-35)

questions.text: Under P.G. 214-33, ESU is called to capture a dangerous dog. How is the animal transported, and what specific unit should be contacted if a search warrant is planned at a location known to have a dangerous dog?
questions.explanation:
**Transport:** **ESU bags the animal** and then an **RMP transports** the animal to an ACC facility. The kennel is placed in the **backseat** of the RMP (rear seat may be removed if necessary). An animal is **NEVER** placed in the trunk of a Department vehicle.

**Search warrant:** If there is advance information of a dog at a warrant location, confer with the **Canine Unit of ESU** — not just any ESU team. (P.G. 214-33)

questions.text: Who directs the NCO Sergeant to make daily callbacks to identified 311 callers — the Special Ops Lt or the CO? How many attempts are required per caller?
questions.explanation: The **CO personally directs** the NCO Sergeant to make daily callbacks to identified 311 callers. This breaks the normal chain of command — it sounds like a Special Ops Lt responsibility, but the PG specifically assigns this to the CO. A minimum of **2 attempts** per caller is required, and all contacts and attempts must be documented. The NCO Sgt then interviews callers to determine if assignments were resolved or need further action, and confers with the SOL. (P.G. 214-35)

questions.text: A complainant in an unlawful eviction case refuses to sign the summons. Can the UMOS still proceed? What modifications are needed when a civilian is the complainant?
questions.explanation:
**Yes.** If the complainant refuses to sign, the **UMOS can sign** "based on information and belief" — but only if the information is **complete**.

When a **civilian is the complainant**, the following modifications are required on the summons:
- Cross out "Complainant's" and "Complainant" and substitute "Officer's" and "Officer" on the front
- Cross out "I personally observed..." above the complainant's name
- On the rear of the original summons, enter the complainant's full name, rank/full signature, and "Date Affirmed" under the appropriate sections
- Complainant's name, address, and phone number go on the bottom

(P.G. 214-12)

questions.text: When referring a 311 condition to another city agency, what is the correct procedure? Can the referral be made anonymously?
questions.explanation:
The referral **cannot** be made anonymously. The correct procedure is:

1. Call **311 directly**
2. Provide: member's **name**, **Dept email**, and **smartphone number** (or TS phone number)
3. Obtain a **new Service Request number**
4. Make the original request disposition **10-95** ("Refer to Other Agency")
5. Put the new service request number in **"Notes to Customer"** before finalizing the old request

(P.G. 214-35)

sergeantFocus.text: Know the three service methods: personal, door service, and deferred (CPO serves next business day). No NOTICE is served if a crime was actually committed at the premises.
Issuance is NOT mandatory. A repeated alarm cycle counts as one continuous action, not multiple violations. The white cardboard (agency) copy is affixed to the vehicle.

The critical distinction is vehicle vs. building: MOS may deactivate vehicle alarms but are NOT permitted to deactivate building alarms. For building alarms, a summons may be issued after 15 minutes of continuous sounding.
The supervisor must respond, investigate, and make the determination about whether the condition involves safety or is solely a civil penalty matter.
Determine the degree of hazard and decide whether barriers, posted officers, or ESU response is appropriate.
The patrol supervisor is the decision-maker on whether to secure the premises. The chain of custody for the key runs from ESU to the desk officer to the assigned member when the owner appears.
The supervisor's first action is to ask the owner/tenant their preference for repair. An MOS must be assigned to secure the location until repairs are complete.

The desk officer is responsible for reviewing the warrant for proper judicial signature and expiration. Police authority is strictly limited to preserving peace — DOB handles all enforcement and rescheduling.

The Special Ops Lt is responsible for packaging and forwarding information to the DA's Narcotics Eviction Program Coordinator. The CO/XO/Special Ops Lt must review all narcotics arrest reports for eviction potential.
Ensure MOS obtain the CVTF log number and enter it in the "Notifications" section of the Complaint Report Worksheet.

Refusal to attend nightlife meetings is NOT sufficient grounds for a C.U.R.E. operation. Know the three possible outcomes after the 30-day notice period: conduct operation, additional monitoring, or no further action.

Class A misdemeanor but NOT fingerprintable. If DV potential exists between family/household members, follow DV guidelines and do NOT demand entrance. Arrest only when the violator cannot be identified OR refuses to permit reentry. If complainant refuses to sign summons, UMOS may sign "based on information and belief."

The police role is exclusively peacekeeping. When arrest is made for unlawful eviction: OLBS only — no Complaint Report, no fingerprints, no photo, no DAT. If subject refuses entry to the marshal, the patrol supervisor should consider making them an EDP. Police cannot break in to let a tenant re-enter unless medicine vital to life is inside.

Special Ops Lt conducts a weekly inspection of the precinct mediation referral file. This program is reserved for situations where there is no basis to take enforcement action — it is not a substitute for proper enforcement.
The patrol supervisor is the decision-maker. Always consult Legal Bureau for obscenity matters. Know the distinction: PL 235 requires judicial determination; PL 245.11 allows summary enforcement.

The desk officer must notify the Dept of Health and Mental Hygiene by phone for three situations: human bitten/scratched/abraded, human exposed to rabid animal, or owner located after form forwarded. ESU bags the animal, RMP transports to ACC. Know all four dangerous dog categories and the Agriculture & Markets Law sections. ACC Brooklyn Control Center is open **24/7**; Rescue Units are Mon-Fri 0800-2000.

Multiple supervisory layers with different frequencies: hourly (desk officer refresh), daily (0800 review, callbacks), weekly (chronic conditions list), monthly (311 caller audit). The 10-42 code is used to notify Communications Section with location and type of job. Disposition 10-95 for referrals to other agencies. The CO breaks the chain of command by personally directing the NCO Sgt. Give special attention to 311 assignments with media files (photos).
The activation mechanism is the FINEST message. For vehicles that can still move, the response is a warning and direction to leave, not immediate enforcement.

The desk officer must assign the 5-day follow-up check and then contact the Department of Sanitation if the vehicle remains. Derelict vehicles are only taken into police custody when needed as evidence.
The 2-month expiration threshold is the trigger. Know the distinction between unoccupied vehicles (remove plates on scene) and occupied vehicles (remove to stationhouse per P.G. 218-12).
Two key notifications: the owner about towing fees, and the Stolen Property Inquiry Unit about the vehicle. Highway vehicles can push to safe parking only — never to repair shops or private property.

chapters.title: Juvenile Matters
chapters.readme:
# Section 215 — Juvenile Matters

## Chapter Overview

Section 215 of the NYPD Patrol Guide covers procedures related to juveniles, including lost and runaway children, juvenile delinquency and arrest processing, school-related incidents, child protection and advocacy, and child safety. These procedures are among the most exam-critical for the Sergeant promotion exam due to the complexity of age thresholds, Miranda requirements, notification chains, and supervisor responsibilities.

## Source

- **Patrol Guide Section:** 215
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide3.pdf
- **Priority:** High

## Learning Objectives

After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

1. Distinguish between Juvenile Delinquent, Juvenile Offender, and Adolescent Offender by age and offense type
2. Explain the Miranda warning requirements unique to juvenile interrogations, including the role of parents/guardians
3. Identify the correct notification sequence for lost children, runaway children, and child abuse cases
4. Describe supervisor responsibilities during juvenile arrests, school-related incidents, and ACS coordination
5. Recall critical age thresholds (under 11, under 16, under 18) and their procedural significance
6. Articulate school arrest protocols including School Safety Division notification and on-campus vs. off-campus enforcement
7. Explain ACS Child Protection Specialist assistance procedures and the 10-68A radio code
8. State child car seat requirements by age group

## Procedure Index

| Procedure | Title | Coverage |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| P.G. 215-01 | Children/Minors Requiring Care and/or Shelter | covered in section files |
| P.G. 215-02 | Lost Child | covered in  |

| P.G. 215-05 | Runaway Children | covered in  |
