| P.G. 215-08 | On-Line Juvenile Report | covered in section files |
| P.G. 215-09 | Offense by Juvenile Under 18 | covered in  |
| P.G. 215-10 | Arrest of JO/AO | covered in  |
| P.G. 215-11 | Arrest in Licensed Premises | covered in  |
| P.G. 215-12 | Personal Recognizance | covered in  |
| P.G. 215-13 | Handcuffing in Schools | covered in  |
| P.G. 215-14 | Child Advocacy Referrals | covered in  |
| P.G. 215-16 | Reporting School Incidents | covered in  |
| P.G. 215-17 | Police Actions at Schools | covered in  |
| P.G. 215-18 | Search Protocols SSA | covered in  |

| P.G. 215-23 | JO/AO Weekend Processing | covered in  |

| P.G. 215-28 | Child Car Seats | covered in  |
| P.G. 215-29 | Electronic Recording | covered in  |

## Sergeant Focus Areas

- Supervisory approval requirements for juvenile arrests
- Proper detention conditions for juveniles
- Mandatory notifications (parents, ACS, Family Court)
- Time limits on juvenile detention
- Miranda requirements specific to juveniles (parent/guardian role)
- School arrest protocols and notification chains
- ACS coordination and 10-68A radio code
- Child car seat inspection responsibilities

## Chapter Files

chapters.keyTerms:
# Section 215 — Key Terms and Critical Numbers

## Definitions

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Juvenile Delinquent (JD)** | A person at least 12 but less than 18 years of age who commits an act that would be a crime if committed by an adult. A child under 12 may qualify for homicide offenses. | P.G. 215-09 |
| **Juvenile Offender (JO)** | A juvenile charged with specific serious felonies who is prosecuted in the criminal justice system rather than Family Court. Age and offense-specific. | P.G. 215-10 |
| **Adolescent Offender (AO)** | A category under Raise the Age legislation for certain offenders aged 16-17 charged with felonies, processed through a Youth Part of Superior Court. | P.G. 215-10 |
| **PINS (Person in Need of Supervision)** | A person under 18 who is truant, incorrigible, ungovernable, or habitually disobedient and beyond the control of a parent/guardian. Processed through Family Court. | General PG |
| **ACS (Administration for Children's Services)** | NYC agency responsible for child welfare, protective services, foster care, and adoption services. | Multiple |
| **IRT (Instant Response Team)** | An ACS team that responds when a child is removed from the home or an arrest is made in a child abuse/neglect case. | P.G. 215-14 |
| **SVU (Special Victims Unit)** | NYPD unit handling sex crimes and crimes against children under 11 years of age. | P.G. 215-02, 215-14 |
| **Corporation Counsel** | NYC's Law Department attorney who represents the city in Family Court proceedings involving juvenile delinquents. Consulted for robbery charges designated as delinquent. | P.G. 215-09 |
| **Family Court** | Court with jurisdiction over juvenile delinquency, child abuse/neglect, PINS, and family offenses. JDs appear at 0900 hours on the next business day. | P.G. 215-09 |
| **Miranda (Juvenile Context)** | Warnings that must be read to BOTH the juvenile AND the parent/guardian using form PD244-1413. Parent/guardian must be present. If parent objects or requests attorney, no questioning is permitted. | P.G. 215-09 |
| **School Safety Agent (SSA)** | Civilian agents who conduct security at DOE schools. Search authority derives from the principal/designee, not independent police authority. | P.G. 215-18 |
| **Reasonable Suspicion** | The legal standard for School Safety Agent searches of students. Lower than probable cause but requires articulable facts. | P.G. 215-18 |
| **Strip-Search Prohibition** | School Safety Agents are absolutely prohibited from conducting strip-searches under any circumstances. | P.G. 215-18 |
| **Licensed Premises** | Public dance halls, cabarets, catering establishments, bars/grills, pool/billiard parlors, bowling alleys, skating rinks, theaters, motion picture theaters. | P.G. 215-11 |
| **State Central Registry** | New York State database of child abuse/maltreatment reports. ACS CPS may only request police assistance when a complaint is lodged here. | P.G. 215-14, 215-22 |
| **10-68A** | Radio code for "Request for Assistance to ACS Child Protection Specialists." | P.G. 215-22 |
| **Juvenile Strategies Unit** | NYPD unit that must be contacted BEFORE beginning the arrest process for a juvenile delinquent. | P.G. 215-09 |
| **School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit** | Unit that must be consulted BEFORE enforcement action on school-related threats. Serves as central repository of all school threats. | P.G. 215-27 |
| **School Safety Division Operations Unit** | Unit that must be notified PRIOR to any arrest at a DOE school. A log number must be obtained. | P.G. 215-17 |

## Critical Numbers and Ages

| Number/Age | Significance | Source |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Under 11** | Abuse cases: notify SVU (not precinct detectives) | P.G. 215-02, 215-14 |
| **11 or older** | Abuse cases: notify precinct detective squad | P.G. 215-02 |
| **Under 12** | Only qualifies as JD for homicide offenses | P.G. 215-09 |
| **12 to 17 (at least 12, less than 18)** | Juvenile Delinquent age range | P.G. 215-09 |
| **Under 16** | Runaway child definition (New York) | P.G. 215-05 |
| **Under 18** | Juvenile definition for licensed premises; sex crime victim threshold for child advocacy | P.G. 215-11, 215-14 |
| **Under 2 years** | Rear-facing child car seat required | P.G. 215-28 |
| **Under 4 years** | Child car seat required | P.G. 215-28 |
| **Ages 4-7** | Booster seat required | P.G. 215-28 |
| **Under 8 years** | Child restraint of some form required | P.G. 215-28 |
| **0900 hours** | Family Court appearance time (next business day) | P.G. 215-09 |
| **10-68A** | Radio code: ACS CPS assistance request | P.G. 215-22 |
| **10-84** | Radio code: arrival at ACS CPS scene | P.G. 215-22 |
| **Section 1024 FCA** | Authorizes emergency removal of children | P.G. 215-22 |
| **MAR BAR MAK + Gun** | Mnemonic for felonies making a 14/15-year-old a Juvenile Offender: Murder/Assault/Rape, Burglary/Arson/Robbery, Manslaughter/Agg Sexual Abuse/Kidnapping, + CPW 2nd within 1000 ft of school | P.G. 215-10 |
| **S.C.A.M.** | Suspected Child Abuse or Maltreatment — the report (and procedure PG 215-03) for investigating and documenting child abuse/neglect cases | P.G. 215-03 |
| **Paper Chase (6 Reports)** | The 6 documents prepared in a child removal case: Complaint Report, DIR, SCAM, Aided Report, Notice of Removal, Affidavit of Service. Only 3 are prepared if no removal (Complaint, DIR, SCAM). | P.G. 215-03 |
| **Constructive Restraint** | Physical control with no restraining device, using minimal force necessary to control movement. Used for a child who is obeying commands. | P.G. 215-13 |
| **Alternative Restraint** | Velcro device used as an alternative to metal handcuffs for possible safety/flight risk and/or non-compliant child. Used only by MOS trained in them; all SSAs carry and are trained. | P.G. 215-13 |
| **Crisis Intervention Team** | School-based team that should be requested when responding to a student in emotional crisis. MOS should inquire about their involvement before engaging. | P.G. 215-13 |
| **Deadly Weapon (9 types)** | 1 loaded weapon (shot can cause S.P.I./D.O.A.), 4 slappers (blackjack, billy, metal knuckles, plastic knuckles), 4 knives (switchblade, pilum ballistic, metal knuckle, dagger). Defined in Penal Law Section 10.00. | P.G. 215-10 |
| **School Grounds** | For CPW 2nd degree JO purposes: inside school property line (elementary through high school) OR outside property line but within 1000 feet in a public place (includes parked vehicle). Penal Law Section 220.00 Sub 14. | P.G. 215-10 |
| **SVU-IRST (Instant Response Tracking Unit)** | SVU unit that accepts law enforcement referrals from State Central Registry, determines appropriate police response, and may prepare complaint reports. | P.G. 215-03 |
| **Safe Horizon** | Organization whose phone number is provided to the non-abusing parent/guardian for child services. | P.G. 215-03 |
| **Juvenile Crime Desk** | Notified when a juvenile is removed to a Department facility. Assists supervisors in determining whether juvenile should be released on JR Worksheet or processed as JD. | P.G. 215-13 |
| **10-90J(1)** | Radio disposition: D.I.R. made, no offense (child abuse "J" code) | P.G. 215-03 |
| **10-92J** | Radio disposition: D.I.R. made, Arrest (child abuse "J" code) | P.G. 215-03 |
| **10-93J** | Radio disposition: D.I.R. made, 61 Prepared (child abuse "J" code) | P.G. 215-03 |
| **10-75F** | Radio disposition: Home visit | P.G. 215-03 |

## Critical Numbers and Ages (Additional)

| Number/Age | Significance | Source |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **13-year-old JO** | Can ONLY be a JO for Murder 2nd degree, Subs 1 and 2 (Intentional and Deathly Reckless) | P.G. 215-10 |
| **14- and 15-year-old JO** | Full MAR BAR MAK + Gun list applies | P.G. 215-10 |
| **Under 12 (restraint)** | Metal cuffs should NOT be used without prior supervisor approval; NOT handcuffed to fixed objects | P.G. 215-13 |
| **16 and above (restraint)** | Metal handcuffs used when necessary; alternative restraints will NOT be utilized | P.G. 215-13 |
| **1000 feet** | Distance from school that defines "School Grounds" for CPW 2nd degree JO | P.G. 215-10 |
| **24 hours** | Time limit for mailing Notice of Removal (UMOS) and Affidavit (D.O. to Family Court) | P.G. 215-03 |
| **9** | Number of Deadly Weapons defined in Penal Law Section 10.00 | P.G. 215-10 |

## Key Forms

| Form | Number | Used For |
|------|--------|----------|
| AIDED REPORT | — | Lost child, runaway child |
| REPORT OF SUSPECTED CHILD ABUSE OR MALTREATMENT | PD377-154 | Suspected abuse/neglect |
| JUVENILE REPORT SYSTEM WORKSHEET | PD377-159A | All juvenile incidents |
| COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET | PD313-152A | Criminal complaints involving juveniles |
| MIRANDA WARNINGS FOR JUVENILE INTERROGATIONS | PD244-1413 | Juvenile questioning |
| ON LINE BOOKING SYSTEM ARREST WORKSHEET | PD244-159 | Juvenile arrests |
| DIR (DOMESTIC INCIDENT REPORT) | — | Child abuse cases involving DV; part of Paper Chase |
| NOTICE — TEMPORARY REMOVAL OF CHILD AND RIGHT TO HEARING | — | Served on P/G or affixed to door when child is removed |
| AFFIDAVIT OF SERVICE OF NOTICE | — | Documents who served/posted/mailed the Notice; D.O. countersigns and mails to Family Court |
| SCHOOL SAFETY DIVISION CRIMINAL INCIDENT REPORT | — | SSA Level I-II documents police action on school grounds |

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-09, a Juvenile Delinquent is defined as a person who is:
options: A) At least 7 years of age but less than 16 years of age
B) At least 12 years of age but less than 18 years of age
C) At least 13 years of age but less than 18 years of age
D) At least 10 years of age but less than 16 years of age
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 215-09 — A Juvenile Delinquent is a person who is at least 12 but less than 18 years of age who commits an act that would be a crime if committed by an adult. The exception is for homicide offenses, where a child under 12 may also be classified as a JD.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-09 — A Juvenile Delinquent is a person who is at least 12 but less than 18 years of age who commits an act that would be a crime if committed by an adult. The exception is for homicide offenses, where a child under 12 may also be classified as a JD.

questions.text: When processing an arrest of a juvenile under P.G. 215-09, what unit must be contacted BEFORE beginning the arrest process?
options: D) School Safety Division Operations Unit
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-09 — The Juvenile Strategies Unit must be contacted BEFORE beginning the arrest process for a juvenile delinquent. This is a mandatory step that cannot be skipped.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-09 — The Juvenile Strategies Unit must be contacted BEFORE beginning the arrest process for a juvenile delinquent. This is a mandatory step that cannot be skipped.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-09, a juvenile's parent/guardian objects to questioning but the juvenile states willingness to speak. What should the officer do?
options: A) Proceed with questioning since the juvenile consented
B) Continue questioning with the parent/guardian removed from the room
C) Not question the juvenile — the parent's objection overrides the juvenile's consent
D) Contact Corporation Counsel for guidance before proceeding
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-09 — If the parent/guardian objects to questioning OR requests an attorney, NO questioning is permitted, even if the juvenile is willing to speak. The parent's objection controls.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-09 — If the parent/guardian objects to questioning OR requests an attorney, NO questioning is permitted, even if the juvenile is willing to speak. The parent's objection controls.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-09, Miranda warnings for a juvenile must be administered using which form, and who must be present?
options: A) PD244-159, with the juvenile's attorney present
B) PD244-1413, with the parent/guardian present
C) PD377-154, with the desk officer present
D) PD244-1413, with the patrol supervisor present
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 215-09 — Miranda warnings must be read using MIRANDA WARNINGS FOR JUVENILE INTERROGATIONS (PD244-1413) with the parent/guardian present. Both the juvenile AND the parent/guardian must be advised of rights.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-09 — Miranda warnings must be read using MIRANDA WARNINGS FOR JUVENILE INTERROGATIONS (PD244-1413) with the parent/guardian present. Both the juvenile AND the parent/guardian must be advised of rights.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-17, prior to effecting an arrest at a DOE school, which unit must be notified?
options: A) School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit
C) School Safety Division Operations Unit
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-17 — The School Safety Division Operations Unit must be notified PRIOR to any arrest at a DOE school, and a log number must be obtained.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-17 — The School Safety Division Operations Unit must be notified PRIOR to any arrest at a DOE school, and a log number must be obtained.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-17, arrests of students for off-campus incidents should be:
options: A) Made at the school to minimize disruption to the community
B) Effected off school property whenever possible
C) Made only with the written consent of the school principal
D) Deferred until the student reaches age 18
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 215-17 — Arrests of students for off-campus incidents should be effected OFF school property when possible. On-campus arrests are reserved for specific qualifying offenses.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-17 — Arrests of students for off-campus incidents should be effected OFF school property when possible. On-campus arrests are reserved for specific qualifying offenses.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-18, School Safety Agents conduct searches under what authority and legal standard?
options: A) Independent police authority; probable cause
B) On behalf of the principal/designee; reasonable suspicion
C) Under ACS supervision; preponderance of evidence
D) As peace officers; probable cause
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 215-18 — School Safety Agents conduct searches on behalf of the principal/designee (except in emergencies) under the reasonable suspicion standard. They do not have independent police search authority.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-18 — School Safety Agents conduct searches on behalf of the principal/designee (except in emergencies) under the reasonable suspicion standard. They do not have independent police search authority.

questions.text: Which of the following is TRUE regarding School Safety Agent searches under P.G. 215-18?
options: A) Strip-searches are permitted with supervisor approval
B) Same-gender searches are preferred but not required
C) Strip-searches are absolutely prohibited under any circumstances
D) Searches may only be conducted with a court order
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-18 — Strip-searches of students are absolutely prohibited regardless of the circumstances. Same-gender searches are required to the maximum extent possible.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-18 — Strip-searches of students are absolutely prohibited regardless of the circumstances. Same-gender searches are required to the maximum extent possible.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-14, when a child under 11 is a victim of abuse, the desk officer must notify:
options: A) Precinct detective squad, then ACS
B) Special Victims Unit, then the appropriate borough Child Abuse Squad
C) Missing Persons Squad, then SVU
D) Borough Command, then the precinct detective squad
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 215-14 — For a child under 11 who is a victim of abuse, the desk officer must notify the Special Victims Unit first, then the appropriate borough Child Abuse Squad. ACS Instant Response Team must also be notified when a child is removed or an arrest is made.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-14 — For a child under 11 who is a victim of abuse, the desk officer must notify the Special Victims Unit first, then the appropriate borough Child Abuse Squad. ACS Instant Response Team must also be notified when a child is removed or an arrest is made.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-02, a lost child who may be a victim of abuse is 13 years old. Who should the desk officer notify regarding the potential abuse?
options: C) ACS Instant Response Team only
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 215-02 — For suspected abuse cases, the desk officer notifies the precinct detective squad if the child is 11 years of age or older, or SVU if under 11. Since this child is 13, the precinct detective squad is the correct notification. However, for any sex crime allegations regardless of age, SVU is notified.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-02 — For suspected abuse cases, the desk officer notifies the precinct detective squad if the child is 11 years of age or older, or SVU if under 11. Since this child is 13, the precinct detective squad is the correct notification. However, for any sex crime allegations regardless of age, SVU is notified.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-22, the radio code designated for "Request for Assistance to ACS Child Protection Specialists" is:
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-22 — Radio code 10-68A has been created specifically for "Request for Assistance to ACS Child Protection Specialists." When an ACS CPS appears at a police facility requesting assistance, the desk officer ensures a 10-68A code is dispatched.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-22 — Radio code 10-68A has been created specifically for "Request for Assistance to ACS Child Protection Specialists." When an ACS CPS appears at a police facility requesting assistance, the desk officer ensures a 10-68A code is dispatched.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-28, a 3-year-old child being transported in a Department vehicle must be placed in:
options: A) A booster seat in the front passenger seat
B) A rear-facing car seat in the back seat
C) A child car seat in the back seat
D) A regular seat belt in the back seat
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-28 — Children under 4 years of age require a child car seat, and children under 2 require a rear-facing child car seat. A 3-year-old requires a child car seat (not necessarily rear-facing since the child is 2 or older). Both child car seats and booster seats must be placed in the back seat only.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-28 — Children under 4 years of age require a child car seat, and children under 2 require a rear-facing child car seat. A 3-year-old requires a child car seat (not necessarily rear-facing since the child is 2 or older). Both child car seats and booster seats must be placed in the back seat only.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-27, before taking enforcement action regarding a school-related threat, an officer must first confer with:
options: A) School Safety Division Operations Unit
C) School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-27 — The School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit must be consulted BEFORE any enforcement action on school-related threats. This unit serves as the central repository of all school threats. The Commanding Officer makes the final determination.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-27 — The School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit must be consulted BEFORE any enforcement action on school-related threats. This unit serves as the central repository of all school threats. The Commanding Officer makes the final determination.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-09, a juvenile delinquent must appear in Family Court at what time?
options: A) 1000 hours on the next calendar day
B) 0900 hours on the next business day
C) 0830 hours on the day of arrest
D) 1400 hours on the next business day
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 215-09 — A juvenile delinquent must appear in Family Court at 0900 hours on the next business day following arrest.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-09 — A juvenile delinquent must appear in Family Court at 0900 hours on the next business day following arrest.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-22, which of the following is NOT a reason ACS Child Protection Specialists may request police assistance?
options: A) Executing an Order of Entry endorsed by a Family Court judge
B) Conducting an emergency removal pursuant to Section 1024 Family Court Act
C) Serving a civil summons related to a custody dispute
D) Concern for personal safety due to suspicion of guns, drugs, or threatened violence
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-22 — ACS CPS may request police assistance for four specific reasons: (1) executing an Order of Entry, (2) executing a Child Removal Order or warrant, (3) conducting an emergency removal under Section 1024 FCA, or (4) personal safety concerns. Civil custody disputes are not among the authorized reasons for police assistance.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-22 — ACS CPS may request police assistance for four specific reasons: (1) executing an Order of Entry, (2) executing a Child Removal Order or warrant, (3) conducting an emergency removal under Section 1024 FCA, or (4) personal safety concerns. Civil custody disputes are not among the authorized reasons for police assistance.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-02, what is the FIRST step a UMOS should take upon finding a lost child?
options: A) Bring the child to the command
C) Notify the desk officer/counterpart AND radio dispatcher
D) Make inquiry in the vicinity of where the child was found
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-02 — A past promotion test asked this exact question. The first step is to notify the Desk Officer/counterpart AND the Radio Dispatcher. Making inquiry in the vicinity comes second, followed by bringing the child to the command if a relative is not located.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-02 — A past promotion test asked this exact question. The first step is to notify the Desk Officer/counterpart AND the Radio Dispatcher. Making inquiry in the vicinity comes second, followed by bringing the child to the command if a relative is not located.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-01 (Child/Minor Requiring Care/Shelter), who may take custody of a child? Compare this to P.G. 215-02 (Lost Child).
options: A) Both procedures allow a relative or friend to take the child
B) 215-01 allows a relative or friend; 215-02 allows only a relative
C) Both procedures allow only a relative to take the child
D) 215-01 allows only a relative; 215-02 allows a relative or friend
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 215-01 states the UMOS should inquire if a "relative OR friend" will provide care. P.G. 215-02 says only "relative" — it does NOT say friend. This is a critical distinction between the two procedures.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-01 states the UMOS should inquire if a "relative OR friend" will provide care. P.G. 215-02 says only "relative" — it does NOT say friend. This is a critical distinction between the two procedures.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-03 (Suspected Child Abuse/Maltreatment), when a complaint is made but NO child removal occurs, how many reports are prepared?
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 215-03 — The "Paper Chase" requires: Complaint but NO removal = 3 reports (Complaint Report, DIR, and SCAM). When there IS a removal, all 6 reports are prepared (adding Aided Report, Notice of Removal, and Affidavit of Service).
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-03 — The "Paper Chase" requires: Complaint but NO removal = 3 reports (Complaint Report, DIR, and SCAM). When there IS a removal, all 6 reports are prepared (adding Aided Report, Notice of Removal, and Affidavit of Service).

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-03, the Affidavit of Service of Notice must be mailed by the Desk Officer to Family Court in which borough?
options: A) The borough where the child was found
B) The borough where the child resides
D) The borough where the precinct of arrest is located
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-03 — The Affidavit goes to Family Court in the borough of REMOVAL (within 24 hours, except weekends/holidays). The SCAM report, by contrast, goes to the ACS Office in the borough of RESIDENCE. Do not confuse the two.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-03 — The Affidavit goes to Family Court in the borough of REMOVAL (within 24 hours, except weekends/holidays). The SCAM report, by contrast, goes to the ACS Office in the borough of RESIDENCE. Do not confuse the two.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-13, metal handcuffs should NOT be used on a student less than what age without prior approval of a supervisor?
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-13 — Metal cuffs should NOT be used on students less than 12 (11) years old without prior approval of a Precinct NCO Sergeant, Patrol Sergeant, or School Safety Supervisor. Metal cuffs are a last resort. Also, a student less than 12 will NOT be handcuffed to a fixed object.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-13 — Metal cuffs should NOT be used on students less than 12 (11) years old without prior approval of a Precinct NCO Sergeant, Patrol Sergeant, or School Safety Supervisor. Metal cuffs are a last resort. Also, a student less than 12 will NOT be handcuffed to a fixed object.

questions.text: A 14-year-old commits a Robbery 2nd degree, Sub 2 (causing physical injury). Under P.G. 215-10, is this person a Juvenile Offender?
options: A) No, because Robbery 2nd is not serious enough
B) Yes, because Robbery 2nd Sub 2 (causing P.I.) is on the JO list
C) No, because a 14-year-old can only be a JO for Murder 2nd
D) Yes, but only if a deadly weapon was used
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 215-10 — Under the MAR BAR MAK + Gun mnemonic, Robbery 2nd degree Sub 2 (causing physical injury) makes a 14- or 15-year-old a Juvenile Offender. The shortcut: if there is an INJURY in a robbery, it is a JO.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-10 — Under the MAR BAR MAK + Gun mnemonic, Robbery 2nd degree Sub 2 (causing physical injury) makes a 14- or 15-year-old a Juvenile Offender. The shortcut: if there is an INJURY in a robbery, it is a JO.

questions.text: Under P.G. 215-13, when a conflict arises between a UMOS who wishes to arrest a student and school personnel who object, what is the proper course of action?
options: A) Defer to the school principal's wishes
B) Contact the School Safety Division Operations Unit for guidance
C) Request response of the Precinct NCO Sergeant or Patrol Supervisor; school personnel may NOT interfere with an arrest deemed warranted
D) Release the student and file a report for follow-up
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 215-13 — Whether probable cause exists is determined by the Police Department. While school personnel's views may be considered, they are NOT controlling. Under no circumstances will school personnel be permitted to interfere with an arrest the NCO Sgt or Patrol Supervisor has deemed warranted. If the school administrator objects, advise them to contact their Legal Department.
questions.explanation: P.G. 215-13 — Whether probable cause exists is determined by the Police Department. While school personnel's views may be considered, they are NOT controlling. Under no circumstances will school personnel be permitted to interfere with an arrest the NCO Sgt or Patrol Supervisor has deemed warranted. If the school administrator objects, advise them to contact their Legal Department.

sergeantFocus.text: Verify the desk officer has completed the full notification chain: ACS IRT, State Central Registry, SVU, and the appropriate borough Child Abuse Squad. Ensure the REPORT OF SUSPECTED CHILD ABUSE OR MALTREATMENT (PD377-154) is prepared.
A patrol supervisor MUST respond to ACS CPS assistance calls.
The desk officer must ensure MOS are trained in proper child car seat installation and use.
The training sergeant is responsible for ensuring ALL members are trained on child car seat usage.

Ensure the desk officer has verified that (1) the Juvenile Strategies Unit was contacted before arrest, (2) parent/guardian was notified immediately, (3) Miranda warnings were administered with parent/guardian present using PD244-1413, and (4) no questioning occurred if the parent objected or requested an attorney.
The CO must forward a report to the Chief of Department for specific premises types.
Ensure the desk officer completes the full notification chain — Missing Persons Squad in all cases, ACS if child unclaimed, and detective squad/SVU if abuse is suspected.
Verify that the desk officer has made all required notifications. For runaway children, Missing Persons Squad notification is mandatory regardless of whether the parent has been contacted.
Evaluate whether the offense justifies an on-campus arrest. For property crimes that are felonies, consider whether the arrest can be deferred to off-campus.
When responding to school incidents, ensure:

Ensure School Safety Agents understand that their search authority derives from the principal/designee, not from independent police authority. Monitor for compliance with the strip-search prohibition and same-gender search requirement.

The School Safety Division Counterterrorism Unit must be consulted before taking enforcement action. The CO makes the final call on the threat level and response. Low-level threats should be handled through school discipline, not enforcement.

chapters.title: Aided Cases
chapters.readme:
# Section 216 — Aided Cases

## Chapter Overview

Section 216 of the NYPD Patrol Guide governs all **aided cases** — incidents where a person (other than a prisoner) requires medical aid or assistance. These procedures cover situations ranging from routine sick-at-home calls to dead body investigations, psychiatric admissions, defibrillator use, and involuntary removals under Mental Hygiene Law.

Sergeants must understand when to respond, what notifications are required, how to supervise aided scenes, and the specific rules that govern specialized scenarios such as Department of Correction deaths, carbon monoxide alarms, and Mobile Crisis Team removals.

## Learning Objectives

After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

1. Define "aided case" and distinguish it from routine sick-at-home and nursing home calls.
2. Identify the correct steps for handling a dead human body, including Medical Examiner notifications and body identification at the morgue.
3. Explain firearm safety station procedures at psychiatric wards.
4. Describe the patrol supervisor's role in drug overdose, carbon monoxide, and EDP-related aided cases.
5. State the rules for animal bite reporting and when firearms may be discharged at an animal.
6. List the notifications required for aided cases involving DOC facilities, firefighters, and MTA incidents.
7. Explain the AED program, including kit contents, utilization definition, and documentation requirements.
8. Describe the procedures for TB shelter client removals and the Department's limited role.
9. Articulate the requirements for Mobile Crisis Team / ACT Team removals under Section 9.58 of Mental Hygiene Law.
10. Identify key forms, reports, and their associated procedure numbers.

## Procedure Index

| File | Procedures Covered |
|------|-------------------|
|  | 216-01, 216-02 (cross-ref), 216-03 (cross-ref) |
|  | 216-04, 216-05 (cross-ref), 216-06 (cross-ref) |
|  | 216-07, 216-08, 216-09, 216-10, 216-11, 216-12 (cross-ref) |
|  | 216-13, 216-14, 216-15, 216-16 |
|  | 216-17 (cross-ref), 216-18 (cross-ref), 216-19, 216-20, 216-21, 216-22 |
|  | 216-23 |
|  | Definitions, forms, mnemonics, critical numbers |
|  | 28 multiple-choice exam prep questions |

## Study Tips

- Pay close attention to **who may disturb a dead body** (P.G. 216-04) — a frequent exam topic.
- Memorize the **AED kit contents** and the definition of "utilized" (P.G. 216-19).
- Understand the difference between **988 hotline** referrals and EDP handling (P.G. 216-01).
- Know when uniformed members **will NOT** respond (nursing homes, DOC facilities).
- The **investigating supervisor hierarchy** for auxiliary police injuries (P.G. 216-13) is testable.
- Learn the **CHILD-ERS** mnemonic for the 8 aided case categories and **I C no P.A.I.N.** for Routine Sick at Home.
- Know **TED the M.D. from D.C.** for the 7 categories of persons who may disturb a dead body.
- Understand the **3 triggers** for requesting detective squad at overdose scenes: DOA, likely to die, NLX administered.
- Remember hypodermic needles are **NOT** drug paraphernalia and Naloxone is **NOT** a controlled substance.
chapters.keyTerms:
# Section 216 — Key Terms, Forms, and Critical Numbers

## Definitions

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Aided Case | Any occurrence requiring a person OTHER THAN A PRISONER to receive medical aid or assistance (sick/injured, dead, lost, mentally ill, abandoned child, runaway, homeless) | P.G. 216-01 |
| Routine Sick at Home | Aided at residence who is conscious, identified, no dependent adults/uncared children, no other police service needed — requires only Activity Log entry | P.G. 216-01 |
| Routine Sick in Nursing Home | Uniformed members WILL NOT respond; exceptions: injury, cardiac, D.O.A., etc. | P.G. 216-01 |
| Human Exposure to Rabies | Bite/scratch from rabid or suspected rabid animal, or contamination of open wound (bled within 24 hours) or mucous membranes with infectious material | P.G. 216-09 |
| Communicable Disease | Infectious disease capable of being passed by direct contact with an infected individual or their discharges | P.G. 216-11 |
| Line of Duty (Auxiliary) | After "sign-in" on Auxiliary Police Roll Call (APS #10) until sign-out; NO off-duty designation | P.G. 216-13 |
| Carbon Monoxide | Colorless, odorless gas; symptoms include headache, nausea, vomiting, confusion; severe cases show cherry red skin | P.G. 216-21 |
| AED Utilization | Once AED pads are placed on an aided — even if no shock is administered | P.G. 216-19 |
| CPR Administered | Once a member performs rapid chest compressions or mouth-to-mouth ventilation | P.G. 216-19 |
| QMHP | Qualified Mental Health Professional — licensed psychologist, certified social worker, or registered professional nurse eligible to direct a Section 9.58 removal | P.G. 216-22 |
| Mobile Crisis Outreach Teams | Hospital/community-based teams funded by DOHMH that assess, stabilize, and refer patients | P.G. 216-22 |
| ACT Teams | "Hospitals on wheels" — richly staffed mental health teams licensed by NYS Office of Mental Health; may order involuntary transport | P.G. 216-22 |
| Opioids | Opiate, synthetic or otherwise — includes heroin, morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl, methadone, hydrocodone, codeine | P.G. 216-23 |
| Fentanyl | Fast-acting synthetic opioid, 30-50x more powerful than heroin, 80-100x more powerful than morphine; can be absorbed through the skin | P.G. 216-23 |
| Opioid Antagonist | FDA-approved drug (Naloxone/Narcan) that negates or neutralizes effects of an opioid; limited to meds approved by Dept of Health | P.G. 216-23 |
| Naloxone (NLX) | NOT a controlled substance; possession is legal; does NOT establish probable cause to arrest | P.G. 216-23 |
| Drug Paraphernalia | Items associated with narcotics use/preparation/storage (straws, scales, glassines, etc.); hypodermic needles are NOT classified as drug paraphernalia | P.G. 216-23 |
| Stamps | Image/writing drug dealers use to ID their "brand"; located on wax folds or glassines | P.G. 216-23 |
| PSNAR | NYS Public Safety Naloxone Administration Reporting — prepared by UMOS who administers NLX; given to Desk Officer | P.G. 216-23 |
| ECMS | Enterprise Case Management System — ECT uploads overdose photos here | P.G. 216-23 |
| 911 Good Samaritan Law | Protects from arrest for possessing controlled substance/paraphernalia/alcohol (under 21) when seeking help in good faith for drug/alcohol overdose | P.G. 216-23 |
| School Crossing Guard LOD | No LODI Report; uses Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23) instead; SCG prepares in own handwriting if possible | P.G. 216-12 |

## Mnemonics

| Mnemonic | Meaning | Source |
|----------|---------|--------|
| **CHILD-ERS** | **C**hild, **H**omeless, **I**njured, **L**ost, **D**OA, **E**DP, **R**equires care, **S**ick — the 8 aided case categories | P.G. 216-01 |
| **TED the M.D. from D.C.** | **T**echnician (Highway CIS), **E**SU, **D**octor/Paramedic/EMT, **M**edical examiner, **D**istrict attorney, **D**etective Bureau, **C**rime Scene Unit — persons who may disturb a dead body | P.G. 216-04 |
| **I C no P.A.I.N.** | **I**dentified and **C**onscious, no **P**olice service, no dependent **A**dults/uncared children, no **I**nvestigation, no **N**otifications — Routine Sick at Home criteria (Activity Log only) | P.G. 216-01 |
| **3 "UNs"** | **UN**-identified, **UN**-conscious, **UN**-sound mind (EDP) — ride in back of ambulance with aided | P.G. 216-01 |

## Key Forms and Reports

| Form Name | Form Number | Used In |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| AIDED REPORT | Electronic (FORMS) | All aided cases |
| IDENTIFICATION TAG | PD317-091 | Dead bodies, body parts, fetuses delivered to morgue |
| COMPLAINT REPORT | PD313-152 | Deaths, suspicious circumstances, DOC deaths |
| COMPLAINT REPORT WORKSHEET | PD313-152A | Dead body cases, CO alarm cases |
| MISSING/UNIDENTIFIED PERSONS REPORT | PD336-151 | Unidentified deceased or aided |
| Police Accident Report | MV104AN | Vehicle collisions, auxiliary police vehicle collisions |
| DANGEROUS ANIMAL/BITE REPORT | PD311-152 | ALL animal bite/dangerous animal incidents |
| RECEIPT FOR CREDENTIALS | PD616-011 | Taxicab driver cases |
| REPORT OF VIOLATION | PD672-151 | Taxicab driver cases (to TLC) |
| REQUEST FOR LABORATORY EXAMINATION REPORT | PD521-168 | Poison/substance cases sent to DOH Laboratories |
| MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER | PD244-150 | Communicable disease prisoners; AED/CPR on prisoners |
| Employee's Notice of Injury | WCD 23 (Law Department) | Auxiliary police LOD injury |
| WITNESS STATEMENT — INJURY TO MEMBER OF DEPARTMENT | PD429-065 | Auxiliary police LOD injury witnesses |
| UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT | PD370-152 | Serious auxiliary police injuries/deaths |
| THREAT, RESISTANCE OR INJURY (T.R.I.) INCIDENT REPORT | — | Auxiliary police force incidents |
| OMH 482 Form | OMH 482 | Mobile Crisis Team Authorization for Transport |
| Section 9.58 Identification Card | — | QMHP identification for involuntary removal |
| NYS Public Safety Naloxone Administration Reporting (PSNAR) | — | Prepared when UMOS administers NLX; emailed to Operations Division |
| Opioid Antagonist Kit | — | Intranasal atomization devices, safety gloves, rescue breathing face shield, OA delivery devices |
| Complaint Report (Overdose) | PD313-152 | "Investigate Aided-Drug Overdose" or "Investigate DOA-Possible Drug Overdose" |

## Critical Numbers and Time Limits

| Item | Value | Source |
|------|-------|--------|
| Second ambulance call | **20 minutes** after first call if ambulance does not arrive | P.G. 216-01 |
| ME follow-up call | **1 hour** if ME fails to respond | P.G. 216-04 |
| Morgue identification (homicide) | **0900 hours, next day** — officer who discovered body reports to morgue | P.G. 216-04 |
| Reports to Auxiliary Police Section | Within **10 days** | P.G. 216-13 |
| DOHMH Animal Bite Unit hours | **0900-1700, Monday-Friday** | P.G. 216-09 |
| After-hours animal bite notification | **Poison Control Center** | P.G. 216-09 |
| 9-8-8 Hotline availability | **24 hours/day, 7 days/week** in 200+ languages | P.G. 216-01 |
| Rabies wound definition | Open wound that **bled within last 24 hours** | P.G. 216-09 |
| NLX re-dose window | **3-5 minutes** — if no response to Naloxone, administer 1 additional dose | P.G. 216-23 |
| Waterproof body covers in P/S car | **4** covers required | P.G. 219-01 |
| SCG — D.O. notify Operations | **Within 24 hours** | P.G. 216-12 |
| SCG — Supervisor's Report of Injury | **Within 48 hours** (C.O. directs) | P.G. 216-12 |
| 3 triggers for detective squad (OD) | DOA, Likely to die, or Naloxone administered | P.G. 216-23 |
| 3 requirements to close UF 61 (DOA) | Identified, ME says natural, relatives present and notified | P.G. 216-04 |

## Critical Notification Contacts

| Situation | Notify |
|-----------|--------|
| Poisoned/infected food or denatured alcohol | Operations Unit (via desk officer) |
| Homicide, suspicious death, suicide | Operations Unit, detective squad, Crime Scene Unit (via detective) |
| DOC suspicious death/suicide | Detective squad, CO/duty captain, patrol borough, **IAB** (get log number), Operations Unit |
| Scaffold injury/death | Department of Buildings |
| MTA bus injury/death | NYC Transit |
| Construction site, Parks property, factory/store injury/death | Operations Unit |
| Firefighter killed/injured at fire | DO NOT notify family without **fire officer** permission |
| DOC inmate death | DO NOT notify family unless **Correction supervisor** requests |
| Auxiliary police injury requiring hospital | Operations Unit (promptly) |
| Carbon monoxide alarm (City-owned building) | Department of Buildings (via Communications) |
| Section 9.58 removal validity in question | Legal Bureau |

## HHC Psychiatric Emergency Facilities

| Hospital | Borough |
|----------|---------|
| Bellevue Hospital Center | Manhattan |
| Metropolitan Hospital Center | Manhattan |
| Harlem Hospital Center | Manhattan |
| Lincoln Hospital | Bronx |
| Bronx Municipal Hospital Center | Bronx |
| North Central Bronx Hospital | Bronx |
| Kings County Hospital Center | Brooklyn |
| Woodhull Hospital | Brooklyn |
| Coney Island Hospital | Brooklyn |
| City Hospital Center at Elmhurst | Queens |
| Queens Hospital Center | Queens |

## Persons Authorized to Disturb a Dead Body

| Letter | Authorized Person |
|--------|-------------------|
| a | Paramedic, E.M.T., or doctor (caution: do not disturb evidence) |
| b | Medical Examiner or assistant |
| c | District Attorney or assistant |
| d | Members of Detective Bureau, detective squad, or Crime Scene Unit |
| e | Highway District member assigned as "collision technician" or "collision investigator" |
| f | ESU member, if body is on train tracks or in subway tunnel |

## AED Kit Contents

| Item | Quantity |
|------|----------|
| Operational AED device | 1 |
| Electrode pads with wire leads (sealed packages) | 2 packages (2 pads each) |
| Pocket mask | 1 |
| Gloves | 1 pair |
| Cloth/gauze compress | 1 |
| Razor | 1 |

## Investigating Supervisor Hierarchy — Auxiliary Police

| Priority | Role | Condition |
|----------|------|-----------|
| 1st | Lieutenant platoon commander | If available |
| 2nd | Patrol supervisor | If LPC unavailable |
| 3rd | CO/XO/duty captain | Serious physical injury, likely to die, or death |

questions.text: A uniformed member responds to a residence where a person is conscious, properly identified, and requesting an ambulance for stomach pain. There are no dependent adults or uncared-for children, and no other police service is required. What documentation is needed?
options: A) An AIDED REPORT and digital Activity Log entry
B) Only a digital Activity Log entry
C) An AIDED REPORT, COMPLAINT REPORT, and Activity Log entry
D) No documentation is required for routine sick at home
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-01 — A "Routine Sick at Home" requires ONLY a digital Activity Log entry when the aided is at a residence AND is conscious and properly identified, no other police service is required, no dependent adults or uncared-for children are present, and no other investigation is needed. All four conditions are met here.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-01 — A "Routine Sick at Home" requires ONLY a digital Activity Log entry when the aided is at a residence AND is conscious and properly identified, no other police service is required, no dependent adults or uncared-for children are present, and no other investigation is needed. All four conditions are met here.

questions.text: Which of the following persons is NOT authorized to disturb a dead body at a scene?
options: A) A member of the Emergency Service Unit, if the body is on train tracks
B) A member of Highway District assigned as a collision technician
C) The patrol supervisor directing the investigation
D) The Medical Examiner or assistant Medical Examiner
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 216-04 — Only six categories of persons may disturb a body: (a) paramedic/EMT/doctor, (b) ME or assistant, (c) DA or assistant, (d) Detective Bureau/squad/Crime Scene Unit, (e) Highway District collision technicians/investigators, (f) ESU on train tracks/subway tunnel. The patrol supervisor is not among them.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-04 — Only six categories of persons may disturb a body: (a) paramedic/EMT/doctor, (b) ME or assistant, (c) DA or assistant, (d) Detective Bureau/squad/Crime Scene Unit, (e) Highway District collision technicians/investigators, (f) ESU on train tracks/subway tunnel. The patrol supervisor is not among them.

questions.text: When a uniformed member responds to an aided case involving a suspected drug overdose (cocaine psychosis), what is the proper disposition of the aided?
options: A) Bring the aided to the precinct stationhouse for processing
B) Have the aided brought to an appropriate hospital facility
C) Release the aided to a responsible family member
D) Have the aided transported to a police detention facility for evaluation
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-01 — The aided is NOT to be brought to a police facility. The patrol supervisor determines if the aided should be removed to the appropriate hospital by RMP or await the ambulance. Drug overdose/cocaine psychosis aided persons go to the hospital, never to a police facility.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-01 — The aided is NOT to be brought to a police facility. The patrol supervisor determines if the aided should be removed to the appropriate hospital by RMP or await the ambulance. Drug overdose/cocaine psychosis aided persons go to the hospital, never to a police facility.

questions.text: At a psychiatric ward, the attending psychiatrist refers a patient to the hospital emergency room for medical evaluation. What should the uniformed member do regarding their firearm?
options: A) Keep the firearm unloaded for the entire duration
B) Reload at the firearms safety station before leaving the psychiatric area, and unload upon return
C) Secure the firearm with hospital security
D) Leave the firearm unloaded and in the holster at all times while in the hospital
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-07 — If the psychiatrist refers a patient to the hospital ER for medical evaluation and clearance, the member shall reload firearm(s) at the safety station before leaving the psychiatric admitting area, and unload upon return. Interim entries must be made in the Psychiatric Admitting Log.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-07 — If the psychiatrist refers a patient to the hospital ER for medical evaluation and clearance, the member shall reload firearm(s) at the safety station before leaving the psychiatric admitting area, and unload upon return. Interim entries must be made in the Psychiatric Admitting Log.

questions.text: A raccoon is observed in a park during mid-afternoon, approaching people in a friendly manner. How should this animal be treated?
options: A) As a normal animal; raccoons sometimes adjust their schedules
B) As potentially rabid until proven otherwise
C) As a danger only if it displays aggressive behavior
D) As requiring no police action unless it bites someone
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-09 — Raccoons are primarily nocturnal animals; any raccoon seen active during the daytime should be considered rabid until proven otherwise. Any raccoon acting friendly or aggressively should be considered rabid.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-09 — Raccoons are primarily nocturnal animals; any raccoon seen active during the daytime should be considered rabid until proven otherwise. Any raccoon acting friendly or aggressively should be considered rabid.

questions.text: Under what circumstances may a uniformed member discharge a firearm at a dog or other animal?
options: A) When the animal has bitten a person
B) When the animal is suspected of being rabid
C) To protect themselves or another person from physical injury when there is no other reasonable means to eliminate the threat
D) When directed to do so by a supervisor
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 216-09 — Members shall not discharge their firearms at a dog or other animal except to protect themselves or another person from physical injury and there is no other reasonable means to eliminate the threat.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-09 — Members shall not discharge their firearms at a dog or other animal except to protect themselves or another person from physical injury and there is no other reasonable means to eliminate the threat.

questions.text: Who may authorize the release of information about a prisoner's communicable disease status?
options: A) The commanding officer of the precinct
B) The Deputy Commissioner — Legal Matters, with written consent
C) The precinct desk officer, in an emergency
D) The patrol supervisor on scene
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-11 — Information concerning a prisoner's communicable disease status must be kept confidential. Any release may only be made with the written consent of the Deputy Commissioner — Legal Matters.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-11 — Information concerning a prisoner's communicable disease status must be kept confidential. Any release may only be made with the written consent of the Deputy Commissioner — Legal Matters.

questions.text: An auxiliary police officer sustains a serious physical injury while on duty. Who acts as the investigating supervisor?
options: A) The patrol supervisor, precinct of occurrence
B) The lieutenant platoon commander, precinct of occurrence
C) The precinct commanding officer/executive officer/duty captain
D) The commanding officer of the Auxiliary Police Section
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 216-13 — The CO/XO/duty captain will be the investigating supervisor if the injured auxiliary police officer sustains a serious physical injury, is likely to die, or dies. The lieutenant platoon commander handles non-serious cases.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-13 — The CO/XO/duty captain will be the investigating supervisor if the injured auxiliary police officer sustains a serious physical injury, is likely to die, or dies. The lieutenant platoon commander handles non-serious cases.

questions.text: How must the Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23) be prepared for an injured auxiliary police officer?
options: A) Typed by the desk officer
C) In the auxiliary police officer's own handwriting, if possible, in two copies
D) Dictated to the patrol supervisor for transcription
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 216-13 — The patrol supervisor will have the auxiliary police officer prepare, in own handwriting if possible, two copies of Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23). If the member is unable, the patrol supervisor will prepare and submit the report.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-13 — The patrol supervisor will have the auxiliary police officer prepare, in own handwriting if possible, two copies of Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23). If the member is unable, the patrol supervisor will prepare and submit the report.

questions.text: An aided case occurs on a NYC Transit train. What is the primary concern regarding service interruption?
options: A) Delays may cause commuter complaints
B) Unnecessary interruption can increase temperatures significantly and may cause panic on crowded trains stopped in tunnels
C) Service interruptions require MTA authorization
D) Union regulations prohibit stopping trains for more than 10 minutes
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-14 — Unnecessary interruption of train service can inconvenience thousands of passengers and endanger public welfare. In addition, unnecessary interruptions can increase temperatures significantly in trains and stations, and may cause panic on crowded trains stopped in tunnels. Service will be interrupted only when absolutely necessary.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-14 — Unnecessary interruption of train service can inconvenience thousands of passengers and endanger public welfare. In addition, unnecessary interruptions can increase temperatures significantly in trains and stations, and may cause panic on crowded trains stopped in tunnels. Service will be interrupted only when absolutely necessary.

questions.text: A homicide victim's family needs to be notified. Who is responsible for making that notification?
options: A) The desk officer at the precinct of occurrence
B) The patrol supervisor on scene
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 216-15 — The assigned detective will make notification to relatives or friends of a homicide victim.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-15 — The assigned detective will make notification to relatives or friends of a homicide victim.

questions.text: An inmate dies in a Department of Correction facility. Regarding notification of the inmate's family, what is the correct procedure?
options: A) The desk officer will immediately notify the family
B) The detective squad will notify the family
C) Notifications WILL NOT be made unless requested by the Correction supervisor in charge
D) The Operations Unit will coordinate notification
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 216-16 — Notifications will NOT be made to relatives or friends of an inmate who dies in a DOC facility unless requested to do so by the Correction supervisor in charge.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-16 — Notifications will NOT be made to relatives or friends of an inmate who dies in a DOC facility unless requested to do so by the Correction supervisor in charge.

questions.text: When notified of a suspicious death in a DOC facility, which agency must the desk officer notify to obtain a log number?
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 216-16 — The desk officer must notify the Internal Affairs Bureau and obtain a log number for DOC suspicious deaths and suicides.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-16 — The desk officer must notify the Internal Affairs Bureau and obtain a log number for DOC suspicious deaths and suicides.

questions.text: The Department considers an AED to have been "utilized" when:
options: A) A shock is delivered to the patient
B) The AED is removed from its carrying case
C) The AED pads are placed on the aided, even if no shock is administered
D) The AED is turned on and the analysis is completed
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 216-19 — Once an AED is placed on an aided, the Department considers that as utilized, even if a shock is not administered. When the AED pads are placed, the machine performs an analysis and advises what actions should be taken.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-19 — Once an AED is placed on an aided, the Department considers that as utilized, even if a shock is not administered. When the AED pads are placed, the machine performs an analysis and advises what actions should be taken.

questions.text: An AED should only be attached to a patient who meets which criteria?
options: A) Unconscious adult, not breathing, no pulse
B) Any unconscious person regardless of age
C) Any person experiencing chest pain
D) Unconscious adult or child, not breathing
questions.answerFull: A) P.G. 216-19 — The AED will only be attached to an unconscious adult patient after it has been confirmed that the patient is not breathing and does not have a pulse. All three criteria must be met.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-19 — The AED will only be attached to an unconscious adult patient after it has been confirmed that the patient is not breathing and does not have a pulse. All three criteria must be met.

questions.text: If AED/CPR is administered on a prisoner in Department custody, what form is completed?
options: C) Both AIDED REPORT and MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-19 — If AED/CPR is administered by a uniformed member on a prisoner in custody, a MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER (PD244-150) form will be completed instead of an AIDED REPORT.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-19 — If AED/CPR is administered by a uniformed member on a prisoner in custody, a MEDICAL TREATMENT OF PRISONER (PD244-150) form will be completed instead of an AIDED REPORT.

questions.text: During the removal of a TB shelter client, what is the Department's primary responsibility?
options: A) Force entry to the premises and remove the patient
C) Assist in the arrest and preserve the peace
D) Provide transportation to the hospital
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 216-20 — The primary responsibility of the Department during TB shelter removals is to assist in the arrest and preserve the peace. Members are NOT to personally assist in forcing entry, securing premises, or safeguarding property. Those duties are performed by other agencies at DOHMH direction.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-20 — The primary responsibility of the Department during TB shelter removals is to assist in the arrest and preserve the peace. Members are NOT to personally assist in forcing entry, securing premises, or safeguarding property. Those duties are performed by other agencies at DOHMH direction.

questions.text: Upon responding to a residential carbon monoxide alarm, what should a uniformed member do?
options: A) Enter the location immediately to check for victims
B) Refrain from entering the location and request the patrol supervisor if alarm is confirmed
C) Enter the location only if wearing a filter mask
D) Enter the location after confirming the CO detector is not malfunctioning
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-21 — The uniformed member will refrain from entering the location and request the response of the patrol supervisor if the alarm is confirmed. Only ESU determines whether the location is safe for re-entry.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-21 — The uniformed member will refrain from entering the location and request the response of the patrol supervisor if the alarm is confirmed. Only ESU determines whether the location is safe for re-entry.

questions.text: When a Mobile Crisis Team requests police assistance for an involuntary removal under Section 9.58, what two documents must the QMHP present?
options: A) Hospital admission form and physician's order
B) Section 9.58 identification card and OMH 482 form
C) Mental Hygiene warrant and court order
D) DOHMH removal order and patient consent form
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-22 — The uniformed member will request the physician or QMHP to present section 9.58 identification card along with signed and completed OMH 482 form (Mobile Crisis Outreach Team Authorization for Transport). Both must be photographed and uploaded to the AIDED REPORT.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-22 — The uniformed member will request the physician or QMHP to present section 9.58 identification card along with signed and completed OMH 482 form (Mobile Crisis Outreach Team Authorization for Transport). Both must be photographed and uploaded to the AIDED REPORT.

questions.text: If the validity of a Section 9.58 involuntary removal is questioned, whom should the uniformed member contact?
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 216-22 — Uniformed members of the service are reminded to contact the Legal Bureau if the validity of a removal is in question.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-22 — Uniformed members of the service are reminded to contact the Legal Bureau if the validity of a removal is in question.

questions.text: Using the CHILD-ERS mnemonic, which of the following is NOT one of the eight aided case categories?
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-01 — CHILD-ERS stands for: Child (runaway/abandoned/destitute/abused/neglected), Homeless, Injured, Lost, DOA, EDP, Requires care (due to arrest/hospitalization/death of guardian), Sick. Prisoners are specifically EXCLUDED from aided case definitions. Use the Medical Treatment of Prisoner form instead.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-01 — CHILD-ERS stands for: Child (runaway/abandoned/destitute/abused/neglected), Homeless, Injured, Lost, DOA, EDP, Requires care (due to arrest/hospitalization/death of guardian), Sick. Prisoners are specifically EXCLUDED from aided case definitions. Use the Medical Treatment of Prisoner form instead.

questions.text: A uniformed member responds to a residence where an elderly person is conscious, identified, and complaining of feeling ill. There are no dependent adults or uncared-for children, and no other police service is needed. Using the "I C no P.A.I.N." mnemonic, what documentation is required?
options: A) Full Aided Report and Activity Log entry
B) Only a digital Activity Log entry
C) Complaint Report and Aided Report
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-01 — "I C no P.A.I.N." = Identified and Conscious, no Police service needed, no dependent Adults or uncared-for children, no Investigation needed, no Notifications required. All conditions are met, making this a "Routine Sick at Home" requiring only a digital Activity Log entry.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-01 — "I C no P.A.I.N." = Identified and Conscious, no Police service needed, no dependent Adults or uncared-for children, no Investigation needed, no Notifications required. All conditions are met, making this a "Routine Sick at Home" requiring only a digital Activity Log entry.

questions.text: At a DOA scene, which of the following persons may disturb the body using the "TED the M.D. from D.C." mnemonic?
options: A) The patrol supervisor directing the investigation
B) A Highway District member on general patrol
C) A Technician/investigator from Highway's CIS (Collision Investigation Squad)
D) A nurse who happens to be on scene
questions.answerFull: C) P.G. 216-04 — TED the M.D. from D.C.: Technician (Highway collision tech/investigator), ESU (subway/train tracks), Doctor/Paramedic/EMT, Medical examiner (or assistant), District attorney (or assistant), Detective Bureau/squad, Crime Scene Unit. A general Highway member or nurse is NOT authorized.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-04 — TED the M.D. from D.C.: Technician (Highway collision tech/investigator), ESU (subway/train tracks), Doctor/Paramedic/EMT, Medical examiner (or assistant), District attorney (or assistant), Detective Bureau/squad, Crime Scene Unit. A general Highway member or nurse is NOT authorized.

questions.text: What three conditions must be met to close a UF 61 (Complaint Report) at the precinct level for a dead body case?
options: A) DOA identified, ME certifies death as natural, and detectives have completed investigation
B) DOA identified, ME certifies death as natural, and relatives are present and notified
C) DOA identified, Crime Scene Unit has processed scene, and desk officer has reviewed reports
D) DOA identified, Operations has been notified, and relatives have been contacted
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-04 — To close the UF 61 at precinct level, all three must be met: (1) DOA is identified, (2) M.E. states cause is natural, and (3) relatives are present and notified.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-04 — To close the UF 61 at precinct level, all three must be met: (1) DOA is identified, (2) M.E. states cause is natural, and (3) relatives are present and notified.

questions.text: A uniformed member arrives at a suspected overdose scene. The person is unresponsive and not breathing with no pulse. Naloxone was administered but the person has not responded within 3-5 minutes. What should the member do?
options: A) Administer CPR/AED only and wait for EMS
B) Administer 1 additional dose of Naloxone
C) Transport the person immediately via RMP
D) Request the patrol supervisor to determine next steps
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-23 — If person has NOT responded to Naloxone within 3-5 minutes, administer 1 additional dose. While waiting, administer CPR/AED if not breathing and no pulse.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-23 — If person has NOT responded to Naloxone within 3-5 minutes, administer 1 additional dose. While waiting, administer CPR/AED if not breathing and no pulse.

questions.text: At a suspected overdose scene, hypodermic needles are found. How should they be handled for evidence purposes?
options: A) Classify as drug paraphernalia evidence
B) Collect as evidence in the overdose investigation, but NOT as evidence of possession of drug paraphernalia
C) Leave them for EMS to dispose of in a sharps container
D) Place in the same security envelope with other drug evidence
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-23 — Hypodermic needles and syringes are advisable to collect at overdose scenes as evidence in an overdose investigation, but NOT as evidence of the crime of possession of drug paraphernalia. Hypo containers must be placed on a SEPARATE voucher and in a serialized Plastic Security Envelope (PSE) — no other evidence in the same envelope.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-23 — Hypodermic needles and syringes are advisable to collect at overdose scenes as evidence in an overdose investigation, but NOT as evidence of the crime of possession of drug paraphernalia. Hypo containers must be placed on a SEPARATE voucher and in a serialized Plastic Security Envelope (PSE) — no other evidence in the same envelope.

questions.text: A School Crossing Guard is injured in the line of duty. What form replaces the LODI Report, and who prepares it?
options: A) Aided Report, prepared by the UMOS
B) Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23), prepared by the SCG in their own handwriting if possible
C) Supervisor's Report of Injury, prepared by the patrol supervisor
D) Unusual Occurrence Report, prepared by the desk officer
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-12 — There is no LODI Report for Crossing Guards. Instead, it is an Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23) prepared by the SCG in their own handwriting, if possible, in two copies. If unable, the patrol supervisor will prepare and submit it.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-12 — There is no LODI Report for Crossing Guards. Instead, it is an Employee's Notice of Injury (WCD 23) prepared by the SCG in their own handwriting, if possible, in two copies. If unable, the patrol supervisor will prepare and submit it.

questions.text: At a suspected overdose scene, when must the patrol supervisor request the detective squad to respond?
options: A) Only when the overdose is confirmed as fatal
B) When the person is DOA, likely to die, or Naloxone was administered
C) For all suspected overdoses, fatal and non-fatal
D) Only when fentanyl is suspected
questions.answerFull: B) P.G. 216-23 — The patrol supervisor requests ECT (Evidence Collection Team) to respond to ALL overdoses. However, the detective squad is requested only for the three triggers: DOA, likely to die, or Naloxone was administered.
questions.explanation: P.G. 216-23 — The patrol supervisor requests ECT (Evidence Collection Team) to respond to ALL overdoses. However, the detective squad is requested only for the three triggers: DOA, likely to die, or Naloxone was administered.

sergeantFocus.text: In death or likely-to-die cases, the desk officer will ensure a notification is made in a timely manner and **document the time, date, and name** of the member making notification on the AIDED REPORT or Police Accident Report.
Memorize all six categories. Note that Highway District members must be specifically assigned as collision technicians/investigators — not just any Highway member.

The patrol supervisor must confer with the ME or assistant ME **prior to directing** a member to search the body to safeguard property. The P/S also examines UMOS Digital A/L and certifies accuracy by signing rank and name, and makes their own Digital Activity Log entry per P.G. 216-04.

In cases where a **death has occurred** or the person is **seriously injured and likely to die**, the desk officer will ensure that notification is made in a timely manner and document the time, date, and name of the member making notification under caption "Notification" on the AIDED REPORT.
The patrol supervisor will immediately respond, ascertain facts, and attempt resolution. If unresolved, make a second request for an EMS supervisor.

The patrol supervisor decides whether to transport via RMP or wait for ambulance. Either way, destination is the hospital — never a police facility. The Sgt. can decide to use the RMP *or* await the bus per P.G. 216-01.
AED accountability happens **every tour** — the desk officer must account for all kits, assign them, notify dispatch, and make Command Log entries.
The patrol supervisor must **confirm the Removal and Detention Order** before proceeding. This is a legal document that authorizes the removal.
Patrol supervisors should be guided by P.G. 212-37, "Hazardous Materials," when responding to CO alarms and setting up frozen areas.
Patrol supervisors/integrity control officers shall comply with unloading procedures and make a **visit entry** in the Psychiatric Admitting Log when supervising subordinates.

chapters.title: Vehicle Collisions
chapters.readme:
# Section 217 — Vehicle Collisions

## Chapter Overview

Section 217 of the NYPD Patrol Guide governs procedures for **vehicle collisions**, including fatal and serious injury collisions, leaving the scene investigations, the Directed Accident Response Program (D.A.R.P.) for towing, city and NYCHA liability cases, aircraft accidents, and chemical test warrant applications for DWI suspects.

Sergeants must understand towing protocols, notification responsibilities, agency routing for city-involved incidents, and the chemical test warrant process — particularly the critical timeframes and escalation procedures.

## Learning Objectives

After studying this chapter, you should be able to:

1. Explain the mechanical defect investigation procedures for fatal/serious injury collisions.
2. Define "City Involved" and route reports to the correct city agency.
3. Describe the procedures for investigating leaving-the-scene collisions, including VTL Section 602 authority.
4. Articulate the full D.A.R.P. tow dispatch process, including rotation rules, 30-minute wait, and disposition codes.
5. State the rules for unsolicited tow trucks at collision scenes.
6. Explain aircraft accident procedures, including the pilot's 10-hour reporting obligation.
7. Describe NYCHA-involved accident reporting and notification requirements.
8. Identify the correct reference marker procedures for highway, bridge, and tunnel collisions.
9. Define "Chemical Test Warrant" and explain when it can and cannot be applied for.
10. List the escalation timeline for contacting the ADA in chemical test warrant cases.

## Procedure Index

| File | Procedures Covered |
|------|-------------------|
|  | 217-01 (cross-ref), 217-02 (cross-ref), 217-03 |
|  | 217-04, 217-11 |
|  | 217-05, 217-06 (cross-ref), 217-08, 217-10 |
|  | 217-09 |
|  | 217-14, 217-16 |
|  | Definitions, forms, critical numbers |
|  | 15 multiple-choice exam prep questions |

## Study Tips

- Memorize all **six D.A.R.P. disposition codes** (10-99T4 through 10-99T9) — a frequent exam topic.
- Know the **agency routing table** for city-involved incidents (P.G. 217-04).
- Understand the **chemical test warrant** restrictions — especially that the warrant CANNOT be applied for if the operator is the only injured person.
- The **30-minute tow wait** rule and the prohibition against precinct commands making tow notifications are highly testable.
- Know the **reference marker** system: green, 9"x8", every 1/10 mile — and that bridge/tunnel markers must come from the Listing book, NOT posted markers.
chapters.keyTerms:
# Section 217 — Key Terms

## Key Definitions

| Term | Definition | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| **Critical Injury** | Determined based upon the on-scene assessment of Emergency Medical Services (EMS) personnel | P.G. 217-03 |
| **City Involved** | Any reported incident involving personal injury and/or property damage which may involve the City in a civil action | P.G. 217-04 |
| **N.Y.C.H.A. Involved** | ALL accidents involving serious injury or death, or an aided case which may involve the Housing Authority in legal action | P.G. 217-11 |
| **Chemical Test Warrant** | A Court Order directing medical personnel to draw blood from a DWI suspect in a collision causing serious physical injury or death to another individual, to determine blood alcohol/drug content | P.G. 217-16 |
| **D.A.R.P.** | Directed Accident Response Program — the system for dispatching designated tow trucks through Communications Section to collision scenes on strict rotation basis | P.G. 217-09 |
| **Reference Marker** | Green, 9"×8" marker located every 1/10 mile on limited access highways, CDRs, and entrance/exit ramps | P.G. 217-14 |
| **SPI** | Serious Physical Injury — triggers Breathalyzer requirement (when combined with STV) in both 217-01 and 217-06 | P.G. 217-01, 217-06 |
| **STV** | Serious Traffic Violation — triggers Breathalyzer requirement (when combined with SPI) | P.G. 217-01, 217-06 |
| **PBT** | Portable Breath Test (Breathalyzer) — administered when SPI AND STV conditions both exist | P.G. 217-01, 217-06 |
| **CR-PDV** | Collision Report — Police Department Vehicle — the primary Department vehicle collision report prepared by the Investigating Supervisor | P.G. 217-06 |
| **GWR** | Gross Weight Rating — the rated maximum weight of a vehicle alone; over 10,000 lbs triggers Qualifying Vehicle status for commercial trucks | P.G. 217-01 |
| **CWR** | Combined Weight Rating — the combined rated weight; over 10,000 lbs (combined with GWR) triggers Qualifying Vehicle status | P.G. 217-01 |
| **A-QOL-O** | Administrative Quality of Life Officer — replaced the Traffic Safety Officer (TSO); reviews all PARs and handles Department vehicle collision reporting into intranet | P.G. 217-01, 217-15 |
| **TSO** | Traffic Safety Officer — former title, no longer exists; replaced by A-QOL-O | P.G. 217-01 |
| **CR-PDV** | Collision Report — Police Department Vehicle — prepared in quadruplicate; Part A (Investigating Supervisor), Part B (Review Board recommendations), Part C (C.O. approval/disapproval) | P.G. 217-06 |
| **MV104L** | DMV form for UMOS drivers only — internal DMV use, not available to public; failure to complete is a misdemeanor and grounds for license suspension/revocation | P.G. 217-06 |
| **Crossover Collision** | When a vehicle strikes OR crosses a median, jersey barrier, etc., separating opposing traffic — does not require actual crossover; defined in P.G. 202-06 | P.G. 202-06, 217-06 |
| **Qualifying Vehicle** | Commercial truck (GWR/CWR over 10,000 lbs), HazMat vehicle (any vehicle with HAZ MAT placard), or Bus (seating for 9 or more including driver) — requires MV104S when Condition of Severity exists | P.G. 217-01 |
| **Condition of Severity** | Fatal (at least 1 fatal injury), Hospital/Medical Attention (at least 1 person transported for immediate care), or Tow/Transported (at least 1 vehicle towed for other than flat tire) — triggers MV104S requirement | P.G. 217-01 |
| **MV104S** | Truck and Bus Supplemental PAR — prepared for each Qualifying Vehicle when a Condition of Severity exists; one MV104S per qualifying vehicle, attached to corresponding PAR | P.G. 217-01 |

## Critical Numbers and Timeframes

| Number | Context | Source |
|--------|---------|--------|
| **24 hours** | Deadline to fax Early Notification of Fatal Accident (MV-104EN) to NYS DMV | P.G. 217-03 |
| **24 hours** | Deadline to fax 3 Dept vehicle collision reports (PAR, MV104-L, CR-PDV) to Personnel Safety Desk | P.G. 217-06 |
| **90 days** | Deadline for filing claims against the City with the Comptroller | P.G. 217-04 |
| **10 hours** | Pilot must report aircraft accident to desk officer (failure = misdemeanor) | P.G. 217-08 |
| **10 days** | MV104 must be submitted to DMV within 10 days by operator (civilian) when collision has injury or over $1,000 damage | P.G. 217-01 |
| **30 days** | Operator can obtain copy of PAR from Pct of occurrence within 30 days (in person or by mail) | P.G. 217-01 |
| **30 days** | A-QOL Officer enters Part C of CR-PDV into intranet within 30 days | P.G. 217-15 |
| **30 days** | C.O. forwards CR-PDV package to Personnel Safety Desk within 30 days of collision (10 days after Review Board) | P.G. 217-06 |
| **15,000 lbs** | GVW threshold — vehicles at or above this weight may use their own tow company | P.G. 217-09 |
| **30 minutes** | Wait time for D.A.R.P. tow before cancelling and requesting next in rotation | P.G. 217-09 |
| **20 days** | Review Board must be convened within this period | P.G. 217-06 |
| **72 hours** | A-QOL Officer enters Parts A and B of CR-PDV into intranet within 72 hours of collision | P.G. 217-15 |
| **90 days** | C.O. has 90 days (absent exigencies) to have member attend collision retraining | P.G. 217-06 |
| **1/10 mile** | Spacing of reference markers on limited access highways | P.G. 217-14 |
| **2 hours** | If unconscious DWI suspect under arrest for this long without chemical test, warrant must be obtained | P.G. 217-16 |
| **1 hour** | Escalation trigger if ADA has not contacted arresting officer for chemical test warrant | P.G. 217-16 |
| **30 minutes** | Follow-up interval for contacting ADA supervisor after initial 1-hour escalation | P.G. 217-16 |
| **15 days** | Hearing must occur within this period after arraignment for chemical test refusal | P.G. 217-16 |
| **0700–2300** | Legal Bureau availability, Monday–Friday | P.G. 217-16 |
| **0700–1830** | Business hours window for faxing Dept vehicle reports and confirming receipt | P.G. 217-06 |
| **6 members** | Review Board size when operator is from same command | P.G. 217-06 |
| **7 members** | Review Board size when operator is from an outside command | P.G. 217-06 |
| **VTL Section 602** | Authorizes summons for leaving scene (property damage only) without observing the offense | P.G. 217-05 |
| **Admin Code 20-518(b)(2)** | Violation for unsolicited tow truck response to collision scene | P.G. 217-09 |
| **Admin Code 20-515(b)** | Violation for soliciting towing at collision scene | P.G. 217-09 |
| **Admin Code 20-524(c)** | Exempts governmental agencies from D.A.R.P. requirements | P.G. 217-09 |

## D.A.R.P. Disposition Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **10-99T4** | Vehicle Collision — No tow required |
| **10-99T5** | Vehicle Collision — D.A.R.P. tow service |
| **10-99T6** | Vehicle Collision — Other tow service |
| **10-99T7** | Vehicle Collision — D.A.R.P. and other tow service |
| **10-99T8** | Vehicle Collision — Highway-authorized tow service |
