questions.answerFull: C) The four required notifications are: Operations Unit, Electronics Section, Internal Affairs Bureau, and Stolen Property Inquiry Unit. Fleet Services Division is not notified for radio losses. (P.G. 219-15)
questions.explanation: The four required notifications are: Operations Unit, Electronics Section, Internal Affairs Bureau, and Stolen Property Inquiry Unit. Fleet Services Division is not notified for radio losses. (P.G. 219-15)

questions.text: Weekly vehicle inspections are conducted on what day and after which platoon has been posted?
options: A) Saturday, after the first platoon
B) Sunday, after the second platoon
C) Monday, after the first platoon
D) Friday, after the third platoon
questions.answerFull: B) The lieutenant platoon commander/patrol supervisor inspects all Department vehicles each Sunday after the second platoon has been posted. (P.G. 219-02)
questions.explanation: The lieutenant platoon commander/patrol supervisor inspects all Department vehicles each Sunday after the second platoon has been posted. (P.G. 219-02)

questions.text: Regulated infectious waste from patrol kits is picked up by:
questions.answerFull: B) The Medical Division messenger picks up regulated infectious waste from the 32-gallon biohazard receptacle at the command. (P.G. 219-17)
questions.explanation: The Medical Division messenger picks up regulated infectious waste from the 32-gallon biohazard receptacle at the command. (P.G. 219-17)

questions.text: Before a law enforcement action is initiated based on a License Plate Reader (LPR) alarm, the operator must:
options: B) Contact the Real Time Crime Center
C) Verify the vehicle status through NYSPIN
questions.answerFull: C) It is absolutely essential that the LPR operator verify the current status of a vehicle through NYSPIN prior to initiating any law enforcement action. The LPR is NOT connected to NYSPIN and activates on partial scan matches. (P.G. 219-31)
questions.explanation: It is absolutely essential that the LPR operator verify the current status of a vehicle through NYSPIN prior to initiating any law enforcement action. The LPR is NOT connected to NYSPIN and activates on partial scan matches. (P.G. 219-31)

questions.text: NYCT NYPD OMNY Police Passes are NOT valid for use on which type of transit?
questions.answerFull: C) OMNY Police Passes are not valid for use on MTA express buses. Besides this exception, they have no electronic restrictions and may be used on subways and public buses. (P.G. 219-26)
questions.explanation: OMNY Police Passes are not valid for use on MTA express buses. Besides this exception, they have no electronic restrictions and may be used on subways and public buses. (P.G. 219-26)

questions.text: A vehicle with how many red light camera violations within a twelve-month period will be ineligible for a Restricted Parking Permit?
questions.answerFull: B) Vehicles with 5 or more red light camera violations OR 15 or more school speed camera violations within a twelve-month period are ineligible. (P.G. 219-29)
questions.explanation: Vehicles with 5 or more red light camera violations OR 15 or more school speed camera violations within a twelve-month period are ineligible. (P.G. 219-29)

questions.text: Commands with a single fuel tank (heating and generator) must maintain the fuel level above what percentage?
questions.answerFull: B) Commands with a single fuel tank must maintain the level above 50%. Commands with two separate tanks are responsible only for the heating tank; Building Maintenance Section maintains the generator tank. (P.G. 219-28)
questions.explanation: Commands with a single fuel tank must maintain the level above 50%. Commands with two separate tanks are responsible only for the heating tank; Building Maintenance Section maintains the generator tank. (P.G. 219-28)

questions.text: When a member's firearm is lost and disciplinary action is warranted, the commanding officer must consult with:
questions.answerFull: C) When disciplinary action is warranted for loss of or failure to safeguard a firearm, a consultation with the Department Advocate's Office is required to determine appropriate disciplinary action. (P.G. 219-21)
questions.explanation: When disciplinary action is warranted for loss of or failure to safeguard a firearm, a consultation with the Department Advocate's Office is required to determine appropriate disciplinary action. (P.G. 219-21)

questions.text: A member's firearm, shield, or ID card is lost within Police Headquarters (5th Precinct). The investigation will be conducted by:
options: B) A captain or above from the reporting member's command
questions.answerFull: B) At Police Headquarters or Police Academy, the loss is reported to the Headquarters Security Unit (or Police Academy Integrity Unit), but the investigation is conducted by a captain or above from the reporting member's command. (P.G. 219-21)
questions.explanation: At Police Headquarters or Police Academy, the loss is reported to the Headquarters Security Unit (or Police Academy Integrity Unit), but the investigation is conducted by a captain or above from the reporting member's command. (P.G. 219-21)

questions.text: Which of the following is a permitted use of a Department tow vehicle?
options: A) Towing a civilian vehicle to a repair shop
B) Towing an inoperable Department vehicle
C) Removing vehicles for alternate side parking
D) Towing abandoned civilian vehicles to the pound
questions.answerFull: B) Department Tow has limited authorized uses: inoperable Department vehicle, evidence vehicle, parade/emergency path clearance, assisting ESU, and lifting heavy articles. (P.G. 219-09)
questions.explanation: Department Tow has limited authorized uses: inoperable Department vehicle, evidence vehicle, parade/emergency path clearance, assisting ESU, and lifting heavy articles. (P.G. 219-09)

questions.text: How many business days prior to foreign travel must a member request ITB Information Security Desk permission to bring a Department mobile device?
questions.answerFull: C) Members must request permission from the ITB Information Security Desk at least 5 business days prior to taking a Department mobile digital device to a foreign country. (P.G. 219-32)
questions.explanation: Members must request permission from the ITB Information Security Desk at least 5 business days prior to taking a Department mobile digital device to a foreign country. (P.G. 219-32)

questions.text: During the daily vehicle inspection, a patrol officer discovers the vehicle is in a sector that includes a body of water. Which life safety equipment is required?
options: A) Life ring with rope attached
C) Fire extinguisher and life ring
D) Life ring and two vest-type life jackets
questions.answerFull: B) When an RMP is assigned to a sector with a body of water, two vest-type life jackets are required. A life ring with rope is required when the **command** itself borders or contains a body of water — this applies to all RMPs in that command, not just those in a specific sector. (P.G. 219-01)
questions.explanation: When an RMP is assigned to a sector with a body of water, two vest-type life jackets are required. A life ring with rope is required when the **command** itself borders or contains a body of water — this applies to all RMPs in that command, not just those in a specific sector. (P.G. 219-01)

questions.text: During a start-of-tour vehicle inspection, an operator finds a serious deficiency. The Platoon Commander is not available. To whom should the Patrol Supervisor report the serious deficiency?
questions.answerFull: C) In the absence of the Platoon Commander, the Patrol Supervisor reports serious vehicle deficiencies directly to the Commanding Officer — not to the Desk Officer. (P.G. 219-01)
questions.explanation: In the absence of the Platoon Commander, the Patrol Supervisor reports serious vehicle deficiencies directly to the Commanding Officer — not to the Desk Officer. (P.G. 219-01)

questions.text: The weekly vehicle inspection requires the Lt. Platoon Commander to check which item that is NOT included in the operator's daily start-of-tour inspection?
options: B) E-Z Pass tag serial number
questions.answerFull: C) Visual Message Signs are specifically checked during the weekly inspection (219-02) but are NOT mentioned as part of the operator's daily (start-of-tour) inspection (219-01). This distinction has appeared on prior exams. (P.G. 219-02)
questions.explanation: Visual Message Signs are specifically checked during the weekly inspection (219-02) but are NOT mentioned as part of the operator's daily (start-of-tour) inspection (219-01). This distinction has appeared on prior exams. (P.G. 219-02)

questions.text: A member of the service discovers that their computer password has been compromised. According to P.G. 219-14, who must be notified?
options: C) Desk Officer/designated supervisor OR ICO
D) Internal Affairs Bureau and the Desk Officer
questions.answerFull: C) If a password is compromised or misused, the member must notify the D.O./designated supervisor OR the ICO (comply with P.G. 207-21 regarding corruption). The procedure does not require notifying IAB directly. (P.G. 219-14)
questions.explanation: If a password is compromised or misused, the member must notify the D.O./designated supervisor OR the ICO (comply with P.G. 207-21 regarding corruption). The procedure does not require notifying IAB directly. (P.G. 219-14)

questions.text: A UMOS loses a Department-issued NYCT MetroCard (Police Pass) in the confines of their command. Who investigates?
questions.answerFull: C) Loss or theft of a MetroCard (Police Pass) is processed per P.G. 219-20, where the Operations Coordinator conducts the investigation. Note the contrast: a **damaged** MetroCard is investigated by the **ICO** per P.G. 219-26. This distinction was tested on the 2017 Lieutenant's exam. (P.G. 219-20, 219-26)
questions.explanation: Loss or theft of a MetroCard (Police Pass) is processed per P.G. 219-20, where the Operations Coordinator conducts the investigation. Note the contrast: a **damaged** MetroCard is investigated by the **ICO** per P.G. 219-26. This distinction was tested on the 2017 Lieutenant's exam. (P.G. 219-20, 219-26)

questions.text: A UMOS loses their shield in Nassau County. Which Desk Officer must be notified first?
options: A) Desk Officer at the pct of occurrence
questions.answerFull: C) Nassau and Suffolk County losses are reported to Desk Officer 105 Pct. Westchester, Putnam, Rockland, or Orange County losses go to Desk Officer 50 Pct. Losses outside those residence counties require notifying the Operations Unit directly. (P.G. 219-21)
questions.explanation: Nassau and Suffolk County losses are reported to Desk Officer 105 Pct. Westchester, Putnam, Rockland, or Orange County losses go to Desk Officer 50 Pct. Losses outside those residence counties require notifying the Operations Unit directly. (P.G. 219-21)

questions.text: A commanding officer's firearm is lost and disciplinary action may be warranted. Before initiating a Command Discipline, the CO of the MOS concerned must:
options: A) Notify IAB first and obtain a log number
B) Consult with the Department Advocate
C) Consult with the Legal Bureau
D) File a report with the Chief of Department
questions.answerFull: B) When a firearm is lost and discipline is contemplated, the CO of the MOS concerned MUST consult with the Department Advocate before initiating a CD or charges. This requirement applies specifically to firearms — not shields or ID cards. (P.G. 219-21)
questions.explanation: When a firearm is lost and discipline is contemplated, the CO of the MOS concerned MUST consult with the Department Advocate before initiating a CD or charges. This requirement applies specifically to firearms — not shields or ID cards. (P.G. 219-21)

questions.text: A member's firearm is lost in the confines of Police Headquarters (5th Precinct). The 61 is recorded by the 5th Precinct. Who conducts the investigation?
options: C) A captain or above from the reporting member's command
questions.answerFull: C) When a loss occurs at Police Headquarters, the loss is reported to the HQ Security Unit (which records the 61), but the investigation is conducted by a captain or above from the **reporting member's own command**. A supervisor from HQ makes the notifications, and a captain or above from HQ investigates and prepares the reports. (P.G. 219-21)
questions.explanation: When a loss occurs at Police Headquarters, the loss is reported to the HQ Security Unit (which records the 61), but the investigation is conducted by a captain or above from the **reporting member's own command**. A supervisor from HQ makes the notifications, and a captain or above from HQ investigates and prepares the reports. (P.G. 219-21)

questions.text: When the Commanding Officer of a MOS distributes the Lost/Stolen Firearm/Shield/ID Card Report (Part D), the distribution instructions are found where on the form?
questions.answerFull: D) The C.O. of the MOS distributes Part D per the **REAR** of the form. The C.O./Duty Captain distributes Part C per the **BOTTOM** of the form. These are different distribution instructions and this distinction has appeared on prior exams. (P.G. 219-21)
questions.explanation: The C.O. of the MOS distributes Part D per the **REAR** of the form. The C.O./Duty Captain distributes Part C per the **BOTTOM** of the form. These are different distribution instructions and this distinction has appeared on prior exams. (P.G. 219-21)

questions.text: A CCRB requests information from a Department smartphone belonging to a member subject to a CCRB inquiry. The member notifies their ICO. The ICO then contacts which unit for guidance?
questions.answerFull: B) When CCRB requests smartphone information: UMOS notifies ICO → ICO contacts **Legal Bureau** for guidance → IAB's CCRB Liaison then processes the request. The chain is UMOS → ICO → Legal Bureau → IAB handles the CCRB response. (P.G. 219-32)
questions.explanation: When CCRB requests smartphone information: UMOS notifies ICO → ICO contacts **Legal Bureau** for guidance → IAB's CCRB Liaison then processes the request. The chain is UMOS → ICO → Legal Bureau → IAB handles the CCRB response. (P.G. 219-32)

questions.text: An LPR device activates and shows a "hit" on a vehicle's license plate. Under what circumstance may the officer take law enforcement action WITHOUT first verifying through NYSPIN?
options: A) Never — NYSPIN verification is always required
B) When the LPR hot list was updated within the last 24 hours
C) When there are additional reasons for a lawful stop, such as an observed VTL infraction
D) Only when directed by a patrol supervisor
questions.answerFull: C) NYSPIN verification is "absolutely essential" UNLESS: (1) there are additional reasons for a lawful stop such as an observed VTL infraction, OR (2) circumstances provide sufficient reasonable suspicion to justify a Terry Stop/Level 3 encounter. In those cases, enforcement may proceed without the NYSPIN check. (P.G. 219-31)
questions.explanation: NYSPIN verification is "absolutely essential" UNLESS: (1) there are additional reasons for a lawful stop such as an observed VTL infraction, OR (2) circumstances provide sufficient reasonable suspicion to justify a Terry Stop/Level 3 encounter. In those cases, enforcement may proceed without the NYSPIN check. (P.G. 219-31)

questions.text: An entry is made on a member's Force Record (10 card) after their firearm is reported lost. What date and time is used for the entry?
options: A) Date and time the firearm was actually lost
B) Date and time the member notified the Desk Officer
C) Date and time of the Command Log Entry
D) Date and time Part C of the Loss Form was completed
questions.answerFull: C) The Force Record (10 card) entry uses the date and time of the **Command Log Entry** — NOT the date and time of the actual loss. This is a specific and frequently tested distinction. (P.G. 219-21)
questions.explanation: The Force Record (10 card) entry uses the date and time of the **Command Log Entry** — NOT the date and time of the actual loss. This is a specific and frequently tested distinction. (P.G. 219-21)

sergeantFocus.text: CO has **final authority** to approve or deny all Restricted Parking Permit applications.
CO must conduct a **quarterly survey** of generator/mechanical/electrical rooms to ensure proper use only.
Department reserves the right to review **all** computer activity — members have no privacy expectation on Department systems.
CO/designated supervisor must return Department smartphone to ITB when member has change in duty status (retired, suspended, modified).
The **Operations Coordinator** conducts the investigation into loss or theft of Department property.
CO must determine negligence and consult Department Advocate before initiating discipline for lost/stolen firearms.
Patrol supervisor vehicles carry additional emergency/tactical equipment. Ensure these items are present and serviceable each tour.

chapters.title: Citywide Incident Management
chapters.readme:
# Section 220 — Citywide Incident Management

## Source
- **Patrol Guide Section:** 220
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide4.pdf (OCR)
- **Priority:** High

## Learning Objectives
- Understand CIMS (Citywide Incident Management System) — NYC's adaptation of NIMS/ICS
- Know Single Command vs Unified Command structures and the Primary Agency Matrix
- Identify Command Staff (PIO, Safety, Liaison) and General Staff (Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance/Admin, I/I) roles
- Understand incident facilities (ICP, Operations Post, I/I Post, Staging Area) and their co-location rules
- Know resource categories (Single Resource, Resource Team, Special Response Team) and statuses (Assigned, Available, Out-of-Service)
- Recognize the CIMS incident sequence and Transfer of Command options
- Understand IMT activation authority and CBRN/HazMat command rules

## Procedures

| Procedure | Title | Status |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| P.G. 220-01 | CIMS Organizational Structure | completed |
| P.G. 220-02 | CIMS Primary Agency Matrix | completed |
| P.G. 220-03 | CIMS Core Competencies | completed |
| P.G. 220-04 | CIMS Agency Roles | completed |
| P.G. 220-05 | CIMS Command Element | completed |
| P.G. 220-06 | CIMS Public Information Officer | completed |
| P.G. 220-07 | CIMS Safety Officer | completed |
| P.G. 220-08 | CIMS Liaison Officer | completed |
| P.G. 220-09 | CIMS Operations Section Chief | completed |
| P.G. 220-10 | CIMS Planning Section Chief | completed |
| P.G. 220-11 | CIMS Logistics Section Chief | completed |
| P.G. 220-12 | CIMS Finance/Administration Section Chief | completed |
| P.G. 220-13 | CIMS Intelligence/Investigations Section Chief | completed |
| P.G. 220-14 | CIMS Supporting Agencies and Agency Representatives | completed |
| P.G. 220-15 | CIMS Coordinating Agency | completed |
| P.G. 220-16 | CIMS Integrated Communications | completed |
| P.G. 220-17 | CIMS Transfer of Command | completed |
| P.G. 220-18 | CIMS Incident Sequence | completed |
| P.G. 220-19 | CIMS Incident Facilities | completed |
| P.G. 220-20 | CIMS Incident Facilities — Staging Area | completed |
| P.G. 220-21 | CIMS Incident Facilities — Air Operations | completed |
| P.G. 220-22 | CIMS Demobilization | completed |
| P.G. 220-23 | CIMS Air Operations | completed |
| P.G. 220-24 | CIMS "Check-In" Process | completed |
| P.G. 220-25 | CIMS Resources | completed |
| P.G. 220-26 | CIMS Resource Assignments | completed |
| P.G. 220-27 | CIMS 201 Incident Briefing Form (ICS Form 201) | completed |
| P.G. 220-28 | CIMS Incident Management Team (IMT) Activation/Deployment | completed |

## Chapter Files

| File | Content |
|------|---------|
|  | 220-01 through 220-04: Organizational structure, Primary Agency Matrix, Core Competencies, Agency Roles |
|  | 220-05: Command Element duties, Single/Unified Command, Span of Control, Management by Objectives |
|  | 220-06 through 220-08: PIO, Safety Officer, Liaison Officer |
|  | 220-09 through 220-13: Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance/Admin, Intelligence/Investigations |
|  | 220-14 through 220-18, 220-22, 220-27, 220-28: Supporting Agencies, Coordinating Agency, Communications, Transfer of Command, Incident Sequence, Demobilization, ICS Form 201, IMT Activation |
|  | 220-19 through 220-21, 220-23 through 220-26: Incident Facilities, Staging Area, Air Operations, Check-In, Resources, Resource Assignments |
|  | Comprehensive definitions, acronyms, command matrices, resource categories, priorities, timeframes |
|  | 25 multiple-choice questions covering all major CIMS concepts |

## Sergeant Focus Areas

- **Transfer of Command**: An arriving sergeant has four options — assuming command is NOT mandatory; may elect to make no change if the current CE is managing effectively
- **Span of Control**: Law enforcement ratio is 1:8 to 1:10 (wider than general 1:3 to 1:7)
- **Squad composition**: 1 Sergeant + 8 Police Officers — the sergeant leads the basic CIMS tactical unit
- **Life Safety priority**: Always #1 — supersedes all other operations including investigation
- **Check-In**: Resources must check in BEFORE assignment to Operations Section
- **Safety Officer authority**: May directly stop unsafe activities when life is in imminent danger without chain of command approval
- **CBRN command**: NYPD assumes Single Command when crime/terrorism is involved; Unified Command otherwise
- **Clear text communications**: No 10-codes at CIMS incidents — use clear text and CIMS terminology
chapters.keyTerms:
# Section 220 — Key Terms and Definitions

## CIMS Organizational Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **CIMS** | Citywide Incident Management System — NYC's adaptation of NIMS/ICS |
| **Command Element** | Individual(s) responsible for overall command function; may be Single or Unified. PG uses "Command Element" not "Incident Commander" because command may be shared with other agencies |
| **Single Command** | One Primary Agency with one Incident Commander |
| **Unified Command** | Multiple Primary Agencies, each with an Agency Incident Commander; all co-locate at one ICP |
| **Single-CBRN** | Hybrid command type for Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear or HazMat incidents — NYPD is sole IC until no criminal/terror activity is determined, then reverts to Unified; Ops Section is Unified throughout |
| **Primary Agency** | Agency designated by the Primary Agency Matrix to command the incident |
| **Supporting Agency** | Agency assisting but not commanding |
| **Coordinating Agency** | NYCEM — facilitates inter-agency coordination and support |
| **Potential Primary Agency** | State/federal agencies with jurisdictional authority under specific conditions |
| **Subject Matter Expert Agency** | Agency with technical expertise but no tactical personnel deployed |
| **Core Competency** | Functional area of expertise assigned to one single agency — describes capability only, not management authority |
| **OEM** | Former name for NYCEM (NYC Emergency Management); older exam questions may still use "OEM" |
| **DCPI** | Deputy Commissioner of Public Information — preferred source for the PIO when NYPD is in command |
| **MODULAR** | Key word describing CIMS structure — IC can run an operation alone or open any combination of staff positions as needed |

## Command Staff Positions

| Position | Designation Authority (Override) | Note |
|----------|-------------------------------|------|
| **Public Information Officer** | Mayor's Press Office may designate | Preferably from DCPI when NYPD is in command |
| **Safety Officer** | No external override — IC designates | In Unified Command, each IC picks own; if all agree, may share one |
| **Liaison Officer** | NYCEM may designate | NYCEM formerly known as OEM |

## General Staff Sections

| Section | Chief Manages | Subordinate Elements | Memory Aid |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|------------|
| **Operations** | All tactical operations | Branches, Sectors (geographic), Groups (functional) | Sectors = Spatial; Groups = Given function |
| **Planning** | Information collection, IAP preparation, resource tracking | Resources Unit, Situation Unit, Demobilization Unit, Documentation Unit | RSDD |
| **Logistics** | All service and support | Service Branch (Communications, Medical, Food); Support Branch (Supply, Facilities, Ground Support) | Service = CMF; Support = SFG |
| **Finance/Administration** | Financial/administrative functions | Time Unit, Procurement Unit, Claims Unit, Cost Unit | TPCC |
| **Intelligence/Investigations** | Investigative/intelligence operations | Investigative Operations Group, Intelligence Group, Forensics Group, Electronic Communications/Surveillance/Evidence Group, Missing/Unidentified Person and Human Remains Group, Investigative Support Group | Groups only — no Branches, no Units |

## Unified Command — Joint vs. Individual Picks

| Jointly Picked (one for entire command) | Each IC Picks Own |
|----------------------------------------|-------------------|
| Public Information Officer | Safety Officer (each agency picks own) |
| Liaison Officer | Operations Section Chief (each agency picks own) |
| Planning Section Chief | — |
| Logistics Section Chief | — |
| Finance/Admin Section Chief | — |
| Intelligence Section Chief | — |

## Three Types of CIMS

| Type | PG Section | Key Feature |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **Single Command** | 220-04 | One IC from core agency; IC picks all staff |
| **Unified Command** | 220-03 | Multiple Co-ICs; jointly pick most staff; each picks own Safety Officer and Ops Chief |
| **Single-CBRN** | 220-02 | NYPD IC until no crime/terror determined; Ops Section is always Unified; nuclear weapon = Unified always |

## Single Command Primary Agency Matrix (High-Frequency Exam Items)

| Incident Type | Primary Agency |
|--------------|---------------|
| Civil Disturbance | NYPD |
| EDP | NYPD |
| Explosive Device / Bomb Threat | NYPD |
| Fire | FDNY |
| Hostage Incident | NYPD |
| Sniper Incident | NYPD |
| Special Event | NYPD |
| Structural Collapse | FDNY |
| Suspicious Package | NYPD |
| Water/Ice Rescue | NYPD |
| Auto Extrication | NYPD/FDNY (first to arrive) |
| Boat in Distress | NYPD/FDNY (first to arrive) |
| Confined Space Rescue | FDNY |
| Downed Tree | Dept of Parks & Recreation / FDNY |
| Elevator Incident | FDNY |
| Correctional Facility Disturbance | DOC |

## Incident Facilities

| Facility | Key Rule |
|----------|----------|
| **Incident Command Post (ICP)** | ONE per incident (except nuclear); identified by incident name |
| **Operations Section Post** | NOT co-located with ICP |
| **I/I Section Post** | NOT co-located with ICP or Operations Post |
| **Staging Area** | Resources in "Available" status; deploy within 3 minutes; named by geography/number |
| **Incident Base** | NOT activated in NYC (absent extraordinary circumstances) |
| **Camp** | NOT activated in NYC (absent extraordinary circumstances) |

## Resource Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Single Resource** | One vehicle + crew, single personnel, K-9 team, or pre-designated team |
| **Resource Team** | Same Kinds/Types assembled for a tactical assignment; requires Leader + communications |
| **Special Response Team** | Different Kinds/Types assembled for a tactical assignment; requires Leader + communications |
| **Kind** | Broad class of similar resources |
| **Type** | Level of capability: I (greatest) to IV (least) |
| **Squad** | 1 Sergeant + 8 Police Officers |
| **Mobile Field Force** | 2 to 6 Squads |

## Resource Status Conditions

| Status | Description | Readiness |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| **Assigned** | Performing work tasks under supervisor | Working |
| **Available** | Ready for assignment | Deploy within **3 minutes** |
| **Out-of-Service** | Not able to be assigned | Mechanical, rest, illness, injury |

## Incident Management Priorities

| Priority | Operations |
|----------|-----------|
| **#1** | Life Safety Operations (immediate, supersedes all) |
| #2 | Site Management Operations |
| #3 | Investigative Operations |
| #4 | Recovery/Restoration Operations |

## Span of Control

| Context | Ratio |
|---------|-------|
| General | **1:3 to 1:7** |
| Law Enforcement | **1:8 to 1:10** |

## Key Principles

| Principle | Definition |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Span of Control** | Number of personnel one supervisor can effectively manage |
| **Unity of Command** | Every subordinate has only ONE designated supervisor |
| **Chain of Command** | Direction given through established authority levels |
| **Management by Objectives** | Incident Objectives must be Attainable, Measurable, Adaptable/Flexible |

## Critical Timeframes and Thresholds

| Item | Threshold |
|------|-----------|
| Operational Period | Usually not over **24 hours** |
| Written IAP required | When incident extends beyond **24 hours** (multiple Operational Periods) |
| Available resource readiness | **3 minutes** to deploy |
| Check-In | Required **before** assignment to Operations |

## CBRN/HazMat Command Rules

| Situation | Command Structure |
|-----------|------------------|
| Actual/suspected crime or terrorism | NYPD **Single Command** (Incident Commander) |
| No crime/terrorism | **Unified Command** (NYPD, FDNY, DEP, DOHMH) |
| Nuclear weapon (anticipated/suspected/actual) | **Unified Command** with borough-centric ICPs |

## IMT Activation Authority

| IMT Type | Activated By |
|----------|-------------|
| Citywide | Police Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner, or Chief of Department |
| Borough | Chief of Patrol or Patrol Borough Commander (or Duty Chief) |
| Transit | Chief of Transit (or Duty Chief) |
| Housing | Chief of Housing (or Duty Chief) |

## Key Forms

| Form | Number | Used For |
|------|--------|----------|
| DETAIL ROSTER / ASSIGNMENT SHEETS | PD406-141 | Check-In recording |
| ICS Form 201 (Incident Briefing) | — | Standardized incident briefing |

## Transfer of Command Options

| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Assume command (no current CE) |
| 2 | Assume from current CE |
| 3 | Make no change |
| 4 | Designate third party |

## NYPD Single Command Incidents

Civil Disturbance, EDP, Explosive Device/Bomb Threat, Hostage, Sniper, Special Event, Suspicious Package, Water/Ice Rescue

*Note: Downed Tree, Elevator Incident, Confined Space Rescue, and Structural Collapse are NOT NYPD — commonly tested trap.*

## NYPD Core Competencies

Law Enforcement & Investigation, Intelligence Collection & Analysis, Crime Scene Processing/Evidence, Site Management (Perimeter, Traffic, Crowd Control), Site Security & Force Protection, Evacuation, Water Search & Rescue, CBRN/HazMat Site Management/Assessment/Investigation, Collision Investigation, VIP Protection, Arson Investigation (Major Case)

questions.text: What is the #1 priority in CIMS incident management?
questions.answerFull: C) Life Safety is always the immediate #1 priority and supersedes all other operations. (P.G. 220-05)
questions.explanation: Life Safety is always the immediate #1 priority and supersedes all other operations. (P.G. 220-05)

questions.text: What is the span of control ratio for law enforcement operations under CIMS?
questions.answerFull: D) The general span of control is 1:3 to 1:7, but law enforcement operations use a ratio of 1:8 to 1:10. (P.G. 220-05)
questions.explanation: The general span of control is 1:3 to 1:7, but law enforcement operations use a ratio of 1:8 to 1:10. (P.G. 220-05)

questions.text: How many Incident Command Posts (ICPs) may be established per incident?
options: D) As many as needed based on incident size
questions.answerFull: C) There is only ONE ICP per incident. The exception is a nuclear weapon incident (anticipated, suspected, or actual), which uses borough-centric ICPs under Unified Command. (P.G. 220-19)
questions.explanation: There is only ONE ICP per incident. The exception is a nuclear weapon incident (anticipated, suspected, or actual), which uses borough-centric ICPs under Unified Command. (P.G. 220-19)

questions.text: What is the composition of a Squad under CIMS?
questions.answerFull: B) A Squad consists of 1 Sergeant and 8 Police Officers. (P.G. 220-25)
questions.explanation: A Squad consists of 1 Sergeant and 8 Police Officers. (P.G. 220-25)

questions.text: Who has authority to designate the Public Information Officer at an incident?
questions.answerFull: C) While the Incident Commander or Agency Incident Commanders may designate the PIO, the Mayor's Press Office has overriding authority and may designate the PIO. (P.G. 220-06)
questions.explanation: While the Incident Commander or Agency Incident Commanders may designate the PIO, the Mayor's Press Office has overriding authority and may designate the PIO. (P.G. 220-06)

questions.text: Resources in "Available" status at a Staging Area must be ready for deployment within how many minutes?
questions.answerFull: B) Resources at a Staging Area are in "Available" status and must be ready for deployment within 3 minutes. (P.G. 220-20)
questions.explanation: Resources at a Staging Area are in "Available" status and must be ready for deployment within 3 minutes. (P.G. 220-20)

questions.text: Which agency is designated as the Coordinating Agency under CIMS?
questions.answerFull: C) NYCEM is the designated Coordinating Agency. It facilitates, coordinates, and supports inter-agency activities but does NOT command the incident. (P.G. 220-15)
questions.explanation: NYCEM is the designated Coordinating Agency. It facilitates, coordinates, and supports inter-agency activities but does NOT command the incident. (P.G. 220-15)

questions.text: When a CBRN/HazMat incident involves actual or suspected crime or terrorism, what command structure is used?
options: A) Unified Command with NYPD, FDNY, DEP, and DOHMH
B) NYPD Single Command with an Incident Commander
D) Unified Command with borough-centric ICPs
questions.answerFull: B) When a CBRN/HazMat incident involves actual or suspected crime or terrorism, NYPD exercises Single Command. (P.G. 220-02)
questions.explanation: When a CBRN/HazMat incident involves actual or suspected crime or terrorism, NYPD exercises Single Command. (P.G. 220-02)

questions.text: A ranking member arriving at an incident has how many options regarding the Command Element?
options: A) Two — assume command or make no change
B) Three — assume command, make no change, or leave the scene
C) Four — assume command (no CE), assume from current CE, make no change, designate a third party
D) Five — the four options above plus request a higher-ranking officer
questions.answerFull: C) The four Transfer of Command options are: (1) Assume command when no CE exists, (2) Assume command from current CE, (3) Make no change, (4) Designate a third party. (P.G. 220-17)
questions.explanation: The four Transfer of Command options are: (1) Assume command when no CE exists, (2) Assume command from current CE, (3) Make no change, (4) Designate a third party. (P.G. 220-17)

questions.text: The Safety Officer may exercise emergency authority to directly stop unsafe activities under what condition?
options: A) When any safety concern is identified
B) When authorized by the Command Element
C) When life or safety of incident personnel is in imminent danger
questions.answerFull: C) The Safety Officer may exercise emergency authority to directly prevent, stop, and/or correct hazardous activities when life or safety is in imminent danger — without waiting for chain of command approval. (P.G. 220-07)
questions.explanation: The Safety Officer may exercise emergency authority to directly prevent, stop, and/or correct hazardous activities when life or safety is in imminent danger — without waiting for chain of command approval. (P.G. 220-07)

questions.text: Which General Staff section prepares the Incident Action Plan?
questions.answerFull: C) The Planning Section chief is responsible for preparing and distributing the Incident Action Plan (IAP). (P.G. 220-10)
questions.explanation: The Planning Section chief is responsible for preparing and distributing the Incident Action Plan (IAP). (P.G. 220-10)

questions.text: A formal written Incident Action Plan is required when an incident extends beyond what timeframe?
questions.answerFull: C) When an incident extends beyond 24 hours (multiple Operational Periods), a formal written IAP is required. (P.G. 220-28)
questions.explanation: When an incident extends beyond 24 hours (multiple Operational Periods), a formal written IAP is required. (P.G. 220-28)

questions.text: Who may activate a Citywide Incident Management Team (IMT)?
options: A) Any Deputy Chief or above
B) Chief of Patrol or Patrol Borough Commander
C) Police Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner, or Chief of Department
D) Chief of Department or any Bureau Chief
questions.answerFull: C) The Citywide IMT is activated by the Police Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner, or Chief of Department. The Borough IMT is activated by the Chief of Patrol or Patrol Borough Commander. (P.G. 220-28)
questions.explanation: The Citywide IMT is activated by the Police Commissioner, First Deputy Commissioner, or Chief of Department. The Borough IMT is activated by the Chief of Patrol or Patrol Borough Commander. (P.G. 220-28)

questions.text: What is the correct CIMS incident sequence?
options: A) Life Safety → Investigation → Site Management → Recovery → Demobilization
B) Response → Life Safety → Site Management → Investigation → Recovery/Restoration → Demobilization
C) Response → Site Management → Life Safety → Investigation → Demobilization
D) Life Safety → Site Management → Recovery → Investigation → Demobilization
questions.answerFull: B) The correct CIMS incident sequence is: Initial Response, Life Safety Operations, Site Management Operations, Investigative Operations, Recovery/Restoration Operations, Demobilization. Phases may overlap with CE approval. (P.G. 220-18)
questions.explanation: The correct CIMS incident sequence is: Initial Response, Life Safety Operations, Site Management Operations, Investigative Operations, Recovery/Restoration Operations, Demobilization. Phases may overlap with CE approval. (P.G. 220-18)

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a CIMS resource status condition?
questions.answerFull: C) The three CIMS resource statuses are Assigned (performing work tasks), Available (ready for assignment, deploy within 3 minutes), and Out-of-Service (not able to be assigned). "Standby" is not a CIMS status. (P.G. 220-25)
questions.explanation: The three CIMS resource statuses are Assigned (performing work tasks), Available (ready for assignment, deploy within 3 minutes), and Out-of-Service (not able to be assigned). "Standby" is not a CIMS status. (P.G. 220-25)

questions.text: The Operations Section Post is generally:
options: A) Co-located with the ICP for efficiency
B) NOT co-located with the ICP
C) Located at the Staging Area
D) Located outside the Outer Perimeter
questions.answerFull: B) The Operations Section Post is generally NOT co-located with the ICP. The I/I Section Post is also NOT co-located with either the ICP or Operations Post. (P.G. 220-09, 220-19)
questions.explanation: The Operations Section Post is generally NOT co-located with the ICP. The I/I Section Post is also NOT co-located with either the ICP or Operations Post. (P.G. 220-09, 220-19)

questions.text: What form is used for Check-In recording at CIMS incidents?
questions.answerFull: C) The PD406-141 (DETAIL ROSTER / ASSIGNMENT SHEETS) or other appropriate forms are used for the Check-In process. (P.G. 220-24)
questions.explanation: The PD406-141 (DETAIL ROSTER / ASSIGNMENT SHEETS) or other appropriate forms are used for the Check-In process. (P.G. 220-24)

questions.text: A Mobile Field Force consists of:
questions.answerFull: B) A Mobile Field Force consists of 2 to 6 Squads. Each Squad = 1 Sergeant + 8 Police Officers. (P.G. 220-25)
questions.explanation: A Mobile Field Force consists of 2 to 6 Squads. Each Squad = 1 Sergeant + 8 Police Officers. (P.G. 220-25)

questions.text: Under CIMS, the Incident Base and Camp are:
options: A) Required at every incident with more than 50 personnel
B) Activated only during Unified Command
C) NOT activated in NYC absent extraordinary circumstances
D) Managed by the Logistics Section only during planned events
questions.answerFull: C) Incident Base and Camp are NIMS ICS concepts that are NOT activated in NYC absent extraordinary circumstances. (P.G. 220-19)
questions.explanation: Incident Base and Camp are NIMS ICS concepts that are NOT activated in NYC absent extraordinary circumstances. (P.G. 220-19)

questions.text: What type of communications must be used for inter-agency communications at a CIMS incident?
options: C) Clear text with CIMS terminology
questions.answerFull: C) All incident personnel must use clear text (no 10-codes) for inter-agency communications and use CIMS nomenclature/terminology. (P.G. 220-16)
questions.explanation: All incident personnel must use clear text (no 10-codes) for inter-agency communications and use CIMS nomenclature/terminology. (P.G. 220-16)

questions.text: Who has authority to designate the Liaison Officer under CIMS?
questions.answerFull: B) While the Incident Commander may designate the Liaison Officer, NYCEM has overriding authority and may designate the Liaison Officer. The Mayor's Press Office designates the PIO, not the Liaison Officer. (P.G. 220-08)
questions.explanation: While the Incident Commander may designate the Liaison Officer, NYCEM has overriding authority and may designate the Liaison Officer. The Mayor's Press Office designates the PIO, not the Liaison Officer. (P.G. 220-08)

questions.text: Resources must formally Check-In before reporting to which section for assignment?
questions.answerFull: C) Resources must formally Check-In before reporting to the Operations Section for assignment (absent extraordinary circumstances). (P.G. 220-24)
questions.explanation: Resources must formally Check-In before reporting to the Operations Section for assignment (absent extraordinary circumstances). (P.G. 220-24)

questions.text: The Safety Officer function is required by law at which type of incident?
options: A) Any incident with more than 20 personnel
questions.answerFull: B) The Safety Officer function is required by law at hazardous materials incidents. (P.G. 220-07)
questions.explanation: The Safety Officer function is required by law at hazardous materials incidents. (P.G. 220-07)

questions.text: Under CIMS, Incident Objectives must be:
options: A) Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
questions.answerFull: B) Under Management by Objectives, CIMS Incident Objectives must be Attainable, Measurable, and Adaptable/Flexible. (P.G. 220-05)
questions.explanation: Under Management by Objectives, CIMS Incident Objectives must be Attainable, Measurable, and Adaptable/Flexible. (P.G. 220-05)

questions.text: Which of the following is an NYPD Single Command incident?
questions.answerFull: D) NYPD Single Command incidents include: Civil Disturbance, EDP, Explosive Device/Bomb Threat, Hostage, Sniper, Special Event, Suspicious Package, and Water/Ice Rescue. Building collapse and structural fire are not NYPD Single Command; HazMat without crime is Unified Command. (P.G. 220-02)
questions.explanation: NYPD Single Command incidents include: Civil Disturbance, EDP, Explosive Device/Bomb Threat, Hostage, Sniper, Special Event, Suspicious Package, and Water/Ice Rescue. Building collapse and structural fire are not NYPD Single Command; HazMat without crime is Unified Command. (P.G. 220-02)

questions.text: Under a Unified Command, which of the following positions does each Agency Incident Commander pick independently (not jointly with other ICs)?
questions.answerFull: C) In Unified Command, each IC independently picks their own Safety Officer and their own Operations Section Chief. PIO, Liaison Officer, Planning Section Chief, Logistics Section Chief, Finance/Admin Section Chief, and Intelligence Section Chief are all jointly picked by the Co-ICs. (P.G. 220-05, 220-07)
questions.explanation: In Unified Command, each IC independently picks their own Safety Officer and their own Operations Section Chief. PIO, Liaison Officer, Planning Section Chief, Logistics Section Chief, Finance/Admin Section Chief, and Intelligence Section Chief are all jointly picked by the Co-ICs. (P.G. 220-05, 220-07)

questions.text: At a CBRN/HazMat incident involving suspected terrorism, which agency serves as the primary Incident Commander?
questions.answerFull: C) When a CBRN/HazMat incident involves actual or suspected crime or terrorism, NYPD serves as the sole Incident Commander (Single Command). NYPD remains primary IC until it is determined there is no criminal or terrorist activity, at which point command reverts to Unified Command. (P.G. 220-02)
questions.explanation: When a CBRN/HazMat incident involves actual or suspected crime or terrorism, NYPD serves as the sole Incident Commander (Single Command). NYPD remains primary IC until it is determined there is no criminal or terrorist activity, at which point command reverts to Unified Command. (P.G. 220-02)

questions.text: Which of the following incident types uses a Unified Command structure with FDNY, NYPD, PANYNJ, USCG, and NTSB as Potential Primary Agencies?
questions.answerFull: B) Aviation Incidents use a Unified Command structure with FDNY and NYPD as Primary Agencies, and PANYNJ, USCG, and NTSB as Potential Primary Agencies/Subject Matter Experts. Rail Incidents use MTA, PANYNJ, NJT, Amtrak as PPAs. Explosions use DDC, DOB, HPD. (P.G. 220-02)
questions.explanation: Aviation Incidents use a Unified Command structure with FDNY and NYPD as Primary Agencies, and PANYNJ, USCG, and NTSB as Potential Primary Agencies/Subject Matter Experts. Rail Incidents use MTA, PANYNJ, NJT, Amtrak as PPAs. Explosions use DDC, DOB, HPD. (P.G. 220-02)

questions.text: At a structural collapse incident, which agency is the Primary Agency?
questions.answerFull: B) Structural Collapse is an FDNY Single Command incident. This question has been tested on the Sergeant exam. Other FDNY Single Command incidents include: Confined Space Rescue, Elevator Incident, Entrapment/Impalement, and Fire. (P.G. 220-02)
questions.explanation: Structural Collapse is an FDNY Single Command incident. This question has been tested on the Sergeant exam. Other FDNY Single Command incidents include: Confined Space Rescue, Elevator Incident, Entrapment/Impalement, and Fire. (P.G. 220-02)

questions.text: The General Staff Section Chief who uses the keyword "MODULAR" to describe how the Incident Commander can staff the incident is associated with which concept?
options: A) The Incident Commander must use all five General Staff sections at every incident
B) The IC can run an operation with one or two people or any combination of staff positions
C) Only the Operations Section must always be activated
D) The Command Element always requires at least three Command Staff members
questions.answerFull: B) "MODULAR" is the keyword for how CIMS works — the structure is flexible and scalable. The IC can run an operation with one or two people or open any combination of staff positions as needed. No section is mandatory. (P.G. 220-01)
questions.explanation: "MODULAR" is the keyword for how CIMS works — the structure is flexible and scalable. The IC can run an operation with one or two people or open any combination of staff positions as needed. No section is mandatory. (P.G. 220-01)

questions.text: In a CBRN/HazMat incident that starts as NYPD Single Command, which agency is responsible for setting Hot, Warm, and Cold zones?
questions.answerFull: C) In Single-CBRN incidents, the Operations Section is Unified. DEP is responsible for Chemical — setting Hot, Warm, and Cold zones, and directing the mitigation effort. FDNY handles Life Safety, Search & Rescue, and Mass Decontamination. DOHMH handles Biological/Radiological zone-setting. NYPD handles overall site management and crime/terrorism assessment. (P.G. 220-02)
questions.explanation: In Single-CBRN incidents, the Operations Section is Unified. DEP is responsible for Chemical — setting Hot, Warm, and Cold zones, and directing the mitigation effort. FDNY handles Life Safety, Search & Rescue, and Mass Decontamination. DOHMH handles Biological/Radiological zone-setting. NYPD handles overall site management and crime/terrorism assessment. (P.G. 220-02)

questions.text: When the Finance/Administration Section Chief is not needed as a full General Staff section, which section may absorb its functions?
questions.answerFull: B) When minimal finance/admin tasks are required (e.g., only managing overtime), the Finance/Admin role may not be a full General Staff section. Instead, it could be one specialist person working for the Planning Section Chief (per PG 220-12, step 1a). (P.G. 220-12)
questions.explanation: When minimal finance/admin tasks are required (e.g., only managing overtime), the Finance/Admin role may not be a full General Staff section. Instead, it could be one specialist person working for the Planning Section Chief (per PG 220-12, step 1a). (P.G. 220-12)

questions.text: Under CIMS, which two General Staff sections can have Branches?
questions.answerFull: C) Operations and Logistics can have Branches. Operations can also have Groups/Sectors. Planning, Finance/Admin can have Units only. Intelligence/Investigations can have Groups/Sectors only. Of the sections that can have Units, only Logistics can also have Branches. (P.G. 220-01)
questions.explanation: Operations and Logistics can have Branches. Operations can also have Groups/Sectors. Planning, Finance/Admin can have Units only. Intelligence/Investigations can have Groups/Sectors only. Of the sections that can have Units, only Logistics can also have Branches. (P.G. 220-01)

questions.text: The Downed Tree incident type designates which agency as the Primary Agency?
options: D) Department of Parks & Recreation / FDNY
questions.answerFull: D) Downed Tree incidents are Primary Agency for Department of Parks & Recreation / FDNY. This is a Single Command incident that is often overlooked — it is not NYPD or FDNY alone. Correctional Facility Disturbance is DOC; Confined Space Rescue is FDNY. (P.G. 220-02)
questions.explanation: Downed Tree incidents are Primary Agency for Department of Parks & Recreation / FDNY. This is a Single Command incident that is often overlooked — it is not NYPD or FDNY alone. Correctional Facility Disturbance is DOC; Confined Space Rescue is FDNY. (P.G. 220-02)

questions.text: Which agency has authority to designate the Liaison Officer in CIMS, and what is the history of that agency's name?
options: A) Mayor's Press Office, formerly known as the Mayor's Communications Office
B) NYCEM, formerly known as OEM (Office of Emergency Management)
C) DCPI, formerly known as the Press Secretary
D) DSNY, formerly known as the Sanitation Commission
questions.answerFull: B) NYCEM (NYC Emergency Management) has authority to designate the Liaison Officer. NYCEM was formerly known as OEM (Office of Emergency Management). Exam questions may use either name. The Mayor's Press Office designates the PIO, not the Liaison Officer. (P.G. 220-08)
questions.explanation: NYCEM (NYC Emergency Management) has authority to designate the Liaison Officer. NYCEM was formerly known as OEM (Office of Emergency Management). Exam questions may use either name. The Mayor's Press Office designates the PIO, not the Liaison Officer. (P.G. 220-08)

sergeantFocus.text: A Sergeant arriving first at a multi-agency incident may serve as the initial Command Element/Incident Commander until relieved through Transfer of Command by a higher-ranking officer.
An arriving sergeant may elect to **make no change** to the command element if the current officer is managing effectively — assuming command is not mandatory.

chapters.title: Tactical Operations
chapters.readme:
# Section 221 — Tactical Operations

## Source
- **Patrol Guide Section:** 221
- **Source PDF:** public-pguide4.pdf (OCR)
- **Priority:** Medium

## Learning Objectives
- Understand force guidelines, firearm prohibitions, and restraint prohibitions
- Know the 4 force incident levels and corresponding investigating entities
- Master less lethal weapons procedures (O.C. pepper spray, CEW, tear gas)
- Know EDP handling procedures including zone of safety, isolate/contain, spit hood rules
- Understand firearms discharge investigation procedures and temporary assignment rules
- Recognize special weapons issuance requirements and distraction device approval process
- Know vehicle checkpoint rules and canine team authorized uses

## Procedures

| Procedure | Title | Status |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| P.G. 221-01 | Force Guidelines | completed |
| P.G. 221-02 | Use of Force | completed |
| P.G. 221-03 | Reporting and Investigation of Force Incident or Injury to Persons During Police Action | completed |
| P.G. 221-04 | Firearms Discharge by Uniformed Members of the Service | completed |
| P.G. 221-05 | Person Dies or Sustains Serious Injury in Police Custody or in Connection with Police Action | completed |
| P.G. 221-06 | Member of the Service Subjected to Force While Performing Lawful Duty | completed |
| P.G. 221-07 | Use of Oleoresin Capsicum Pepper Spray Devices | completed |
| P.G. 221-08 | Use of Conducted Electrical Weapons (CEW) | completed |
| P.G. 221-09 | Use of Tear Gas | completed |
| P.G. 221-10 | Use and Issuance of Shotguns/Long Guns/Automatic Weapons | completed |
| P.G. 221-11 | Guidelines and Approval Process Pertaining to the Use of Distraction Devices | completed |
| P.G. 221-13 | Mentally Ill or Emotionally Disturbed Persons | completed |
| P.G. 221-15 | Vehicle Pursuits | completed |
| P.G. 221-16 | Vehicle Checkpoints | completed |
| P.G. 221-18 | Use of Department Canine Teams | completed |
| P.G. 221-21 | School Safety Agent — Victim of an Offense While Performing Duty | completed |
| P.G. 221-22 | Traffic Enforcement Agent Victim of an Offense While Performing Duty | completed |
| P.G. 221-23 | Alcohol Testing for UMOS Involved in Firearms Discharges Resulting in Injury, Death, or Likelihood of Injury or Death | completed |

> **Note:** Procedure numbers not listed above (221-12, 221-14, 221-17, 221-19, 221-20) are not covered in the public Patrol Guide. P.G. 221-14 (Hostage/Barricaded) is referenced in the EDP section but its content is not included.

## Chapter Files

| File | Content |
|------|---------|
|  | 221-01, 221-02: Force guidelines, firearm/restraint prohibitions, use of force, de-escalation, duty to intervene |
|  | 221-03: Force incident levels 1-4, T.R.I. reports, immediate/investigating supervisor duties, BWC rules |
|  | 221-04, 221-05, 221-23: Firearms discharge investigation, death in custody, alcohol testing |
|  | 221-07, 221-08, 221-09: O.C. pepper spray, CEW (TASER 7), tear gas |
|  | 221-10, 221-11: Shotguns/long guns/automatic weapons, distraction devices |
|  | 221-15: Vehicle pursuits, initiation criteria, prohibited tactics, termination rules, post-pursuit requirements |
|  | 221-06, 221-13, 221-16, 221-18, 221-21, 221-22: MOS subjected to force, EDP, vehicle checkpoints, canine teams, school safety/TEA |
|  | Comprehensive definitions, force levels, approval authorities, timeframes, forms |
|  | 25 multiple-choice questions covering all major tactical operations concepts |

## Sergeant Focus Areas

- **Force incident response**: Immediate supervisor must be NOT involved, one rank higher; for Level 1, responsible for all investigative steps; complete T.R.I. before end of tour
- **Duty to intervene**: Must intervene to stop excessive force — failure = serious misconduct up to dismissal
- **Firearms discharge**: Assume command, establish crime scene, inspect/secure firearms, secure BWC, notify desk officer
- **CEW accountability**: Account for all CEWs each tour (Command Log entry with serial numbers); observe function test; carry CEW on patrol if available
- **EDP handling**: Establish firearms control immediately; maintain 20-foot zone of safety; if contained and believed armed but no immediate threat — NO additional action without CO/duty captain; only sergeant+ can cancel ESU
- **De-escalation**: Supervisor assumes command and coordinates de-escalation; consider whether non-compliance is inability vs. deliberate resistance
- **Less lethal prohibitions**: Never use O.C. spray, CEW, or impact weapons on passively resisting subjects; never use CEW on rear-cuffed prisoner
- **Post-force**: Ensure subject positioned for free breathing; inquire about medical needs; obtain medical attention for any injured person
chapters.keyTerms:
# Section 221 — Key Terms and Definitions

## Force Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **De-Escalation** | Taking action to stabilize situation, reduce immediacy of threat; gain voluntary compliance |
| **Objectively Reasonable Standard** | Reasonableness based on totality of circumstances; perspective of similarly trained/experienced member |
| **Excessive Force** | Force greater than what a reasonable officer would use under same circumstances |
| **Active Resisting** | Physically evasive movements (bracing, tensing, pushing, verbal intent to resist) |
| **Active Aggression** | Threat or overt act of assault with present ability, indicating imminent assault/injury |
| **Passive Resistance** | Minimal physical action (standing motionless, going limp) |
| **Prohibited Methods of Restraint** | Sitting, kneeling, standing on chest/back compressing diaphragm |
| **Chokehold** | Any pressure to throat, carotid artery, or windpipe affecting breathing or blood flow |

## Force Incident Levels

| Level | Key Criteria | Investigating Entity |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Level 1** | Hand/foot strikes, take-downs, grappling, O.C. spray, mesh blanket, CEW **cartridge mode**, physical injury from police action | Immediate Supervisor |
| **Level 2** | Striking with object (baton, vehicle), canine bite, CEW **drive stun**, prohibited acts (excl. chokehold), alleged excessive force, attempted prisoner suicide, unconsciousness/lost tooth/stitches | CO/XO/Duty Captain + IAB |
| **Level 3** | Force readily capable of causing death/serious injury (not firearm), chokehold/prohibited restraint, excessive force + serious injury, attempted prisoner suicide + serious injury | Internal Affairs Bureau |
| **Level 4** | ALL firearms discharges, UMOS firearm fired by another, subject seriously injured/likely to die/dies in/prior to custody | Force Investigation Division |

## Immediate Supervisor Definition

Available supervisor NOT involved in the incident, from same command, at least **one rank higher** than all involved members. If none available, **duty captain** determines.

## Injury Classifications

| Term | Examples |
|------|---------|
| **Physical Illness/Injury** | Minor swelling, contusions, lacerations, abrasions |
| **Serious Physical Injury/Illness** | Broken/fractured bones, aneurysm, hospital admission required, heart attack, stroke, life-threatening |

## Less Lethal Weapons

| Weapon | Key Parameters |
|--------|---------------|
| **O.C. Pepper Spray** | Two 1-second bursts, minimum 3 feet, aim at eyes; NOT on passive resistance |
| **CEW (TASER 7)** | Cartridge mode = Level 1; Drive stun = Level 2; standard cycle 5 seconds; never on rear-cuffed; support side holster |
| **Tear Gas** | Borough commander approval; ESU only; dry cleaning does NOT remove residue |
| **Distraction Device** | Chief of Department approval; ESU only |

## CEW Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Function Test** | Mandatory test into firearms safety station in test mode under supervisor observation |
| **Laser Warning** | De-escalation — placing laser dot on subject for voluntary compliance |
| **Warning Arc** | Visible/audible arcing of electricity on TASER 7 |
| **Cartridge Mode** | Probes fired for neuromuscular incapacitation (Level 1) |
| **Drive Stun** | Direct/close contact with front electrodes (Level 2) |

## Special Weapons

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Special Weapons** | Shotguns, long guns, automatic weapons |
| **Special Weapons Record** | Three-section log: Qualification, Issuance, Maintenance |

## EDP Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **EDP** | Person apparently mentally ill/temporarily deranged, likely to result in serious injury to self or others |
| **Zone of Safety** | Distance from EDP > effective range of weapon (not firearm); minimum **20 feet** |
| **Crossfire** | Unintentional placement of person in position to be injured by firearms discharge |
| **Crossfire Awareness** | Being aware of threat/surroundings; communicate "crossfire" when aware |
| **CIT** | Crisis Intervention Team — specially trained members as contact officers |

## "Resisting Recidivist"

Prisoner arrested for charges against MOS (resisting arrest, assault 2nd, assault on PO, aggravated assault on PO) **more than once within 5 years**. Requires detective squad enhancement.

## Firearm Prohibitions Summary

| SHALL NOT |
|-----------|
| Discharge to endanger innocent persons |
| Discharge in defense of property |
| Discharge at fleeing felon with no imminent threat |
| Fire warning shots |
| Discharge at/from moving vehicle (unless deadly force by other means) |
| Discharge at animal (unless imminent injury, no retreat) |
| Cock a firearm (double action only) |

## Critical Timeframes

| Item | Timeframe |
|------|-----------|
| PD324-140 (Firearms Discharge Report) | Within **48 hours** |
| Temporary assignment after discharge causing death/injury | Minimum **3 consecutive tours** |
| Post-incident member welfare follow-up | **24-48 hours** |
| Report to NYS Commission of Correction (SCOC M187) | Fax within **6 hours**; mail within **10 days** |
| CEW replacement cartridge | Within **72 hours** |
| CEW unintentional discharge retraining | Within **5 business days** |
| O.C. pepper spray effects | Do not dissipate for **15-30 minutes** |
| Vehicle checkpoint maximum duration | **2 hours** at one location |
| CEW standard cycle | **5 seconds** |
| CEW exposure risk threshold | Beyond **15 seconds** |
| Alcohol — prudent abstinence before tour | **4 hours** |
| Undercover alcohol limit | **2 beverages** per tour |

## Approval Authorities

| Action | Authority Required |
|--------|-------------------|
| Tear gas use | **Patrol Borough Commander** (or designee) |
| Distraction device use | **Chief of Department** (or designee) |
| Return to permanent command after firearms discharge | **First Deputy Commissioner** |
| Non-standard vehicle checkpoint | **Deputy Commissioner, Legal Matters** (written) |
| Cancel ESU response at EDP | **Sergeant or above** |
| Canine use (non-authorized tactical) | **Borough Commander** |
| Canine at non-peaceful demonstration | **Chief of Patrol** |

## Key Forms

| Form | Used For |
|------|---------|
| **T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT** | One per force incident — regardless of number of MOS/subjects |
| **T.R.I. INTERACTION REPORT** | Each involved MOS completes for every reportable force/injury |
| **PD324-140** (UMOS Report of Firearms Discharge) | All firearms discharges — within 48 hours |
| **SCOC M187** (Report of Inmate Death) | Death in custody — NYS Commission of Correction |
| **PD469-150** (Supervisor's Fitness for Duty Report) | Alcohol testing documentation |
| **VEHICLE CHECKPOINT FORM** | Document checkpoint operations |
| **Special Weapons Record** | Log for issuance/qualification/maintenance of special weapons |

## NYPD Duty to Intervene

All MOS **must** intervene to stop excessive force by MOS or other law enforcement. Failure to intervene or report = **serious misconduct** → criminal/civil liability + Department discipline up to **dismissal**.

questions.text: Which of the following is a firearm prohibition under P.G. 221-01?
options: A) Discharging a firearm at a fleeing felon who presents an imminent threat
C) Discharging a firearm to protect from imminent physical injury
D) Discharging a firearm in self-defense
questions.answerFull: B) Warning shots are prohibited. Members SHALL NOT fire warning shots under any circumstances. (P.G. 221-01)
questions.explanation: Warning shots are prohibited. Members SHALL NOT fire warning shots under any circumstances. (P.G. 221-01)

questions.text: A CEW discharge in cartridge mode is classified as what level force incident?
questions.answerFull: A) CEW discharge in cartridge mode is a Level 1 force incident. CEW in drive stun mode is Level 2. (P.G. 221-03)
questions.explanation: CEW discharge in cartridge mode is a Level 1 force incident. CEW in drive stun mode is Level 2. (P.G. 221-03)

questions.text: Who investigates Level 3 force incidents?
questions.answerFull: C) Level 3 force incidents (force readily capable of causing death/serious injury, chokehold, excessive force with serious injury) are investigated by the Internal Affairs Bureau. (P.G. 221-03)
questions.explanation: Level 3 force incidents (force readily capable of causing death/serious injury, chokehold, excessive force with serious injury) are investigated by the Internal Affairs Bureau. (P.G. 221-03)

questions.text: The immediate supervisor in a force incident must be:
options: A) Any available supervisor from the same command
B) A supervisor not involved in the incident, at least one rank higher than all involved members
C) The desk officer of the precinct of occurrence
D) The duty captain in all cases
questions.answerFull: B) The immediate supervisor must be available, NOT involved in the incident, from the same command, and at least one rank higher than all involved members. (P.G. 221-03)
questions.explanation: The immediate supervisor must be available, NOT involved in the incident, from the same command, and at least one rank higher than all involved members. (P.G. 221-03)

questions.text: After a firearms discharge causing death or injury, the involved UMOS is temporarily assigned for a minimum of:
questions.answerFull: B) The involved member is temporarily assigned to the patrol borough office for a minimum of three consecutive scheduled tours (exclusive of sick time or RDOs). (P.G. 221-04)
questions.explanation: The involved member is temporarily assigned to the patrol borough office for a minimum of three consecutive scheduled tours (exclusive of sick time or RDOs). (P.G. 221-04)

questions.text: O.C. pepper spray should be applied using:
options: A) One continuous burst at two feet
B) Two, one-second bursts at a minimum distance of three feet
C) Three short bursts at five feet
D) One long burst at arm's length
questions.answerFull: B) O.C. pepper spray is held upright, aimed at the subject's eyes, using two one-second bursts at a minimum distance of three feet. (P.G. 221-07)
questions.explanation: O.C. pepper spray is held upright, aimed at the subject's eyes, using two one-second bursts at a minimum distance of three feet. (P.G. 221-07)

questions.text: What is the recommended minimum "zone of safety" distance from an EDP?
questions.answerFull: C) A minimum distance of 20 feet is recommended for the zone of safety. The distance should be greater than the effective range of the weapon (other than a firearm). (P.G. 221-13)
questions.explanation: A minimum distance of 20 feet is recommended for the zone of safety. The distance should be greater than the effective range of the weapon (other than a firearm). (P.G. 221-13)

questions.text: The UMOS Report of Firearms Discharge (PD324-140) must be completed within:
questions.answerFull: B) The PD324-140 must be completed within 48 hours of the incident. (P.G. 221-04)
questions.explanation: The PD324-140 must be completed within 48 hours of the incident. (P.G. 221-04)

questions.text: Who has the authority to approve the use of tear gas?
options: B) Patrol borough commander or designee
questions.answerFull: B) The ultimate decision concerning use of tear gas rests with the patrol borough commander or designee. Technical decisions are made by the ranking ESU member. (P.G. 221-09)
questions.explanation: The ultimate decision concerning use of tear gas rests with the patrol borough commander or designee. Technical decisions are made by the ranking ESU member. (P.G. 221-09)

questions.text: Members of the service have a duty to intervene to stop excessive force. Failure to intervene may result in:
options: C) Criminal and civil liability and Department discipline up to dismissal
questions.answerFull: C) Failure to intervene in or report excessive force is serious misconduct that may result in criminal and civil liability and Department discipline up to and including dismissal. (P.G. 221-02)
questions.explanation: Failure to intervene in or report excessive force is serious misconduct that may result in criminal and civil liability and Department discipline up to and including dismissal. (P.G. 221-02)

questions.text: A "Resisting Recidivist" is defined as a prisoner arrested for charges against MOS:
options: A) Three or more times in ten years
B) More than once within five years
D) Any repeat offense regardless of timeframe
questions.answerFull: B) A Resisting Recidivist is a prisoner arrested for resisting arrest, assault on PO, or similar charges more than once within five years (i.e., the second time in five years). The detective squad must enhance the arrest. (P.G. 221-06)
questions.explanation: A Resisting Recidivist is a prisoner arrested for resisting arrest, assault on PO, or similar charges more than once within five years (i.e., the second time in five years). The detective squad must enhance the arrest. (P.G. 221-06)

questions.text: The use of distraction devices requires approval from:
options: C) Chief of Department or designee
questions.answerFull: C) Distraction devices require direct knowledge and specific approval of the Chief of Department (or designee), absent exigent circumstances. (P.G. 221-11)
questions.explanation: Distraction devices require direct knowledge and specific approval of the Chief of Department (or designee), absent exigent circumstances. (P.G. 221-11)

questions.text: Who may apply a spit hood to an EDP?
options: A) Any uniformed member of the service
C) Only Emergency Service Unit personnel
questions.answerFull: C) Only ESU personnel may apply a spit hood. Only ESU personnel or a medical professional with minimum EMT certification can remove it. (P.G. 221-13)
questions.explanation: Only ESU personnel may apply a spit hood. Only ESU personnel or a medical professional with minimum EMT certification can remove it. (P.G. 221-13)

questions.text: CEWs should be carried in a holster on which side?
options: A) Shooting side, same as firearm
B) Support side, opposite the firearm
C) Either side at the member's preference
questions.answerFull: B) The CEW holster must be attached to the duty belt on the support side, opposite the firearm. Cross-drawing is prohibited. (P.G. 221-08)
questions.explanation: The CEW holster must be attached to the duty belt on the support side, opposite the firearm. Cross-drawing is prohibited. (P.G. 221-08)

questions.text: An EDP must NEVER be transported to:
questions.answerFull: B) Under no circumstances will an EDP be transported to a police facility. EDPs are removed to a hospital via ambulance (or RMP if ambulance unavailable, at supervisor's direction). (P.G. 221-13)
questions.explanation: Under no circumstances will an EDP be transported to a police facility. EDPs are removed to a hospital via ambulance (or RMP if ambulance unavailable, at supervisor's direction). (P.G. 221-13)

questions.text: At the scene of a firearms discharge, the PBT alcohol test threshold is:
questions.answerFull: C) If the PBT reading is less than .08 and no indicia of intoxication, no further testing is required. At .08 or greater, an Intoxilyzer test at the IDTU facility is required. (P.G. 221-23)
questions.explanation: If the PBT reading is less than .08 and no indicia of intoxication, no further testing is required. At .08 or greater, an Intoxilyzer test at the IDTU facility is required. (P.G. 221-23)

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT an authorized use of O.C. pepper spray?
options: A) Gaining control of a person actively resisting arrest
B) Preventing an individual from injuring themselves
C) Controlling a subject who is passively resisting
questions.answerFull: C) O.C. pepper spray, CEW, and impact weapons should NOT be used on persons who are passively resisting. (P.G. 221-07)
questions.explanation: O.C. pepper spray, CEW, and impact weapons should NOT be used on persons who are passively resisting. (P.G. 221-07)

questions.text: Vehicle checkpoints should NOT exceed what duration at one location?
questions.answerFull: B) A vehicle checkpoint should not exceed two hours at one location, regardless of conditions. (P.G. 221-16)
questions.explanation: A vehicle checkpoint should not exceed two hours at one location, regardless of conditions. (P.G. 221-16)

questions.text: Department canine teams shall NOT be used for:
options: B) Crowd control or deterrent at peaceful demonstrations
C) Searching buildings for break-in suspects
questions.answerFull: B) Canine teams will not be used for crowd control or as a deterrent at peaceful demonstrations. Use at non-peaceful demonstrations requires prior approval of the Chief of Patrol. (P.G. 221-18)
questions.explanation: Canine teams will not be used for crowd control or as a deterrent at peaceful demonstrations. Use at non-peaceful demonstrations requires prior approval of the Chief of Patrol. (P.G. 221-18)

questions.text: When a UMOS discharges a firearm outside of New York City, the Operations Unit must be notified within:
questions.answerFull: B) If a firearm is discharged outside of NYC, the member must notify local police authorities immediately and the Operations Unit within 6 hours of discharge. (P.G. 221-04)
questions.explanation: If a firearm is discharged outside of NYC, the member must notify local police authorities immediately and the Operations Unit within 6 hours of discharge. (P.G. 221-04)

questions.text: A CEW standard cycle is how many seconds?
questions.answerFull: B) Members should use a CEW for one standard cycle of five seconds while constantly assessing. Exposure beyond 15 seconds may increase the risk of death or serious injury. (P.G. 221-08)
questions.explanation: Members should use a CEW for one standard cycle of five seconds while constantly assessing. Exposure beyond 15 seconds may increase the risk of death or serious injury. (P.G. 221-08)

questions.text: The response of Emergency Service Unit to an EDP scene may only be canceled by:
options: A) Any member of the service
C) A uniformed supervisor in the rank of sergeant or above
D) The precinct commanding officer only
questions.answerFull: C) The response of ESU may only be canceled by a uniformed supervisor in the rank of sergeant or above. (P.G. 221-13)
questions.explanation: The response of ESU may only be canceled by a uniformed supervisor in the rank of sergeant or above. (P.G. 221-13)

questions.text: Members SHALL NOT discharge firearms at or from a moving vehicle unless:
options: A) The vehicle is being used in a pursuit
C) Deadly physical force is being used by means other than a moving vehicle
D) The vehicle is approaching a checkpoint
questions.answerFull: C) Members SHALL NOT discharge firearms at or from a moving vehicle unless deadly physical force is being used against MOS or another person by means OTHER than a moving vehicle. (P.G. 221-01)
questions.explanation: Members SHALL NOT discharge firearms at or from a moving vehicle unless deadly physical force is being used against MOS or another person by means OTHER than a moving vehicle. (P.G. 221-01)

questions.text: How many T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORTS are prepared per force incident?
options: C) Only ONE per incident, regardless of number of MOS or subjects
questions.answerFull: C) Only one T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT will be prepared for an incident, regardless of the number of MOS involved or number of subjects/civilians/bystanders involved. (P.G. 221-03)
questions.explanation: Only one T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT will be prepared for an incident, regardless of the number of MOS involved or number of subjects/civilians/bystanders involved. (P.G. 221-03)

questions.text: For Level 3 and Level 4 force incidents, the immediate supervisor should handle body-worn camera footage by:
options: A) Reviewing all videos and documenting findings
B) Uploading footage to FORMS T.R.I. application
C) Safeguarding cameras and NOT viewing — await IAB/FID direction
D) Forwarding footage to the commanding officer
questions.answerFull: C) Body-worn camera footage concerning Level 3 and Level 4 force incidents will NOT be viewed by the immediate supervisor. Immediate supervisors will safeguard the cameras and await direction from IAB or FID. (P.G. 221-03)
questions.explanation: Body-worn camera footage concerning Level 3 and Level 4 force incidents will NOT be viewed by the immediate supervisor. Immediate supervisors will safeguard the cameras and await direction from IAB or FID. (P.G. 221-03)

sergeantFocus.text: As a supervisor, you must **respond to the scene** when feasible and **actively monitor** the pursuit via radio. You have the **authority and responsibility** to direct termination if the danger to the public outweighs the benefit of apprehension.

Know the **prohibited tactics** — Forcible stopping and roadblocks are **NEVER authorized** during a vehicle pursuit. Officers may confuse these with permissible tactics like traffic control or PIM (which is only allowed on **stationary** vehicles).
**Reckless Driving (VTL § 1212)** is explicitly defined as a **non-violent misdemeanor** — pursuits for this offense alone are **NOT authorized**. This is heavily tested.
Force incident levels (1-4) do NOT apply to MOS injuries. Follow this procedure and A.G. 330-03 instead.

If EDP is contained and believed armed/violent but poses no immediate threat — take **no additional action** without CO/duty captain authorization. Establish **firearms control** immediately upon arrival. Use CIT-trained members as contact officers.

Patrol supervisor's priorities at firearms discharge: (1) call ambulance/render aid, (2) assume command, (3) establish crime scene, (4) inspect/secure firearms, (5) secure BWC, (6) notify desk officer, (7) request detective squad.
When a subject ingests narcotics, transport **DIRECTLY** to nearest hospital — do NOT use force to prevent swallowing or attempt to remove from mouth.

Supervisor must **assume command**, coordinate de-escalation, and direct tactics to **minimize injury**. Must consider whether non-compliance is deliberate resistance or an inability to comply (medical condition, mental impairment, language barrier, etc.).

For Level 1 incidents, the immediate supervisor is responsible for **all investigative steps**. For Level 2-4, the immediate supervisor is guided by the investigating supervisor. Complete Level 1 T.R.I. INCIDENT REPORT **before end of tour**.
Ensure CEWs are accounted for at start of every tour with Command Log entry including serial numbers. Ensure function test is performed into firearms safety station under your observation.

chapters.title: Duties & Responsibilities
chapters.readme:
# Section 303 — Duties & Responsibilities

## Source
- **Administrative Guide Section:** 303
- **Source PDF:** Public Admin Guide1.pdf
- **Priority:** High

## Overview
